blocks:
Ayahs

سُورَةٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهَا وَفَرَضْنَـٰهَا وَأَنزَلْنَا فِيهَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (١)
[This is] a surah which We have sent down and prescribed, and We have sent down in it manifest signs so that you may take admonition. (1)
ٱلزَّانِيَةُ وَٱلزَّانِى فَٱجْلِدُوا۟ كُلَّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا مِا۟ئَةَ جَلْدَةٍ ۖ وَلَا تَأْخُذْكُم بِهِمَا رَأْفَةٌ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذَابَهُمَا طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢)
As for the fornicatress and the fornicator, strike each of them a hundred lashes, and let not pity for them overcome you in Allah’s law, if you believe in Allah and the Last Day, and let their punishment be witnessed by a group of the faithful. (2)
ٱلزَّانِى لَا يَنكِحُ إِلَّا زَانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً وَٱلزَّانِيَةُ لَا يَنكِحُهَآ إِلَّا زَانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ ۚ وَحُرِّمَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣)
The fornicator will not marry anyone but a fornicatress or an idolatress, and the fornicatress will be married by none except a fornicator or an idolater, and that is forbidden to the faithful. (3)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ فَٱجْلِدُوهُمْ ثَمَـٰنِينَ جَلْدَةً وَلَا تَقْبَلُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَهَـٰدَةً أَبَدًا ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٤)
As for those who accuse chaste women and do not bring four witnesses, strike them eighty lashes, and never accept any testimony from them after that, and they are transgressors, (4)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥)
excepting those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْوَٰجَهُمْ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ شُهَدَآءُ إِلَّآ أَنفُسُهُمْ فَشَهَـٰدَةُ أَحَدِهِمْ أَرْبَعُ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٦)
As for those who accuse their wives [of adultery], but have no witnesses except themselves, the testimony of such a man shall be a fourfold testimony [sworn] by Allah that he is indeed stating the truth, (6)
وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةُ أَنَّ لَعْنَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٧)
and a fifth [oath] that Allah’s wrath shall be upon him if he were lying. (7)
وَيَدْرَؤُا۟ عَنْهَا ٱلْعَذَابَ أَن تَشْهَدَ أَرْبَعَ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٨)
The punishment shall be averted from her by her testifying with four oaths [sworn] by Allah that he is indeed lying, (8)
وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةَ أَنَّ غَضَبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٩)
and a fifth [oath] that Allah’s wrath shall be upon her if he were stating the truth. (9)
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٠)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, and that Allah is all-clement, all-wise.... (10)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is indicated by its opening: سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها وَفَرَضْناها وَأَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ "A surah which We have sent down and which We have ordained, and We have sent down in it manifest signs that you may take admonition." (24:1). Thus, it is an admonition (تذكرة) comprising a selection of ordained and legislated rulings (الأحكام المفروضة المشرعة), followed by a set of divine gnoses (المعارف الإلهية) that correspond to them and by which the believers (المؤمنون) take heed.

It is a Medinan Surah (سورة مدنية) without dispute, and the context of its verses bears witness to this. Among its most illustrious verses is the Verse of Light (آية النور).

His saying, the Exalted: سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها وَفَرَضْناها وَأَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ "A surah which We have sent down and which We have ordained, and We have sent down in it manifest signs that you may take admonition." (24:1). A Surah (سورة) is a distinct body of discourse unified by a single purpose for which it was conveyed. For this reason, it is sometimes considered as the verses themselves in terms of the meanings they carry, hence it is said: "We have ordained it" (فَرَضْناها). At other times, it is considered as a vessel for some of the verses—the containment of the whole for the part—hence it is said: "We have sent down in it manifest signs" (أَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ). The term is among those established by the Quran to name a specific collection of its verses, and its usage is frequent in the speech of the Exalted. It seems to be derived from the wall of a city (سور البلد), which is the fence that surrounds it; the Quranic Surah is so named because it encompasses the verses within it or the purpose for which it was delivered.

Al-Raghib said: "Ordaining (الفرض) is the cutting of a hard object and making an impression in it, such as 'notching' iron or the wood of a fire-drill and a bow." He continued: "Ordaining (الفرض) is like obligation (الإيجاب), but obligation is used in consideration of its occurrence and stability, while ordaining is used in consideration of the decisiveness of the ruling (قطع الحكم) therein. The Exalted said: 'A surah which We have sent down and which We have ordained' (وَفَرَضْناها), meaning: We have made acting upon it obligatory for you." He added: "Every instance where 'Allah ordained upon him' (فرض الله عليه) appears, it refers to the obligation into which Allah has entered him. Whatever appears as 'Allah ordained for him' (فرض الله له), it refers to Him not restricting it for him, such as: ما كانَ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيما فَرَضَ اللهُ لَهُ 'There is no blame on the Prophet in what Allah has prescribed for him' (33:38)." End quote.

Thus, His saying: "A surah which We have sent down and which We have ordained" (سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها وَفَرَضْناها) means: This is a Surah We have revealed and We have made the practice of the rulings (الأحكام) therein mandatory. Acting upon an obligatory ruling (الحكم الإيجابي) is to perform it, and acting upon a prohibitive ruling (الحكم التحريمي) is to desist from it.

His saying: "and We have sent down in it manifest signs that you may take admonition" (وَأَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ)—the intended meaning here, as evidenced by the context, is the Verse of Light and the verses following it that clarify the reality of faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر), and monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and polytheism (الشرك), which serve as reminders of these divine gnoses.

His saying, the Exalted: الزَّانِيَةُ وَالزَّانِي فَاجْلِدُوا كُلَّ واحِدٍ مِنْهُما مِائَةَ جَلْدَةٍ "As for the fornicatress and the fornicator, strike each of them a hundred lashes" (24:2). Fornication (الزنا) is sexual intercourse without a marriage contract (عقد), the semblance of a contract (شبهة عقد), or right of ownership (ملك يمين). Lashing (الجلد) is striking with a whip. Pity (الرأفة) is tenderness and compassion; it is said to be mercy (رحمة) accompanied by a sense of pain. A group (الطائفة) originally refers to a company of people who circle (يطوفون) by traveling from place to place; it is said that it can also be applied to two people or even one.

His saying: "As for the fornicatress and the fornicator" (الزَّانِيَةُ وَالزَّانِي) etc., means: the woman and the man from whom fornication has been established, strike each of them a hundred lashes. This is the prescribed punishment (حد) for fornication according to the text of the verse, except that it is specified (مخصصة) for certain cases: among them is if they are married (محصنين) or one of them is married, in which case the punishment is stoning (الرجم); and if they are not free or one of them is a slave, the punishment is half the prescribed limit.

It is said that the fornicatress was mentioned before the fornicator because fornication from women is more heinous, and because desire in them is stronger and more frequent. The command to lash is addressed to the general body of Muslims, and it is carried out by those who hold authority (ذوي الولاية) among them, such as the Prophet, the Imam, or whoever represents them.

His saying: "and do not let pity for them overcome you in [matters of] Allah’s religion" (وَلا تَأْخُذْكُمْ بِهِما رَأْفَةٌ فِي دِينِ اللهِ) etc. The prohibition of pity is of the nature of prohibiting an effect by prohibiting its cause, since pity for one who deserves a type of punishment necessitates negligence (التساهل) in making them taste the punishment they deserve by lightening it, and it may even lead to abandoning it. Therefore, He qualified it by saying: "in Allah’s religion" (فِي دِينِ اللهِ), meaning: while the pity—that is, the negligence—is regarding Allah's religion and His law (شريعته).

It is also said that "Allah's religion" (دين الله) refers to Allah's ruling (حكم الله), as in His saying: ما كانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخاهُ فِي دِينِ الْمَلِكِ "He could not have detained his brother under the king’s law" (12:76), meaning: in his ruling. Thus, it means: let not pity for them seize you in executing Allah's ruling and establishing His prescribed punishment (حده).

His saying: "if you believe in Allah and the Last Day" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ) means: if you are such and such, then let not pity for them seize you, and do not be negligent in their matter. This contains an emphasis (تأكيد) of the prohibition.

His saying: "and let a group of the believers witness their punishment" (وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذابَهُما طائِفَةٌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) means: let a group of them be present and look upon that so they may take heed and not approach such indecency (الفاحشة).

His saying, the Exalted: الزَّانِي لا يَنْكِحُ إِلَّا زانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً وَالزَّانِيَةُ لا يَنْكِحُها إِلَّا زانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ وَحُرِّمَ ذلِكَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "The fornicator shall not marry anyone but a fornicatress or a polytheist, and the fornicatress shall not be married by anyone but a fornicator or a polytheist, and that has been forbidden to the faithful." (24:3). The apparent meaning of the verse, especially considering the context of its conclusion in relation to its beginning, is that what it contains is a prohibitive legislative ruling (حكم تشريعي تحريمي). Even though its beginning is phrased as a report (خبر), the intended meaning is a prohibition (النهي) to emphasize the demand, which is a common rhetorical device.

The sum of its meaning, as interpreted in the Sunnah (السنة) through the channels of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), is that if a man becomes notorious for fornication and the prescribed punishment (الحد) is carried out upon him, and his repentance (التوبة) is not manifest, it is forbidden for him to marry anyone other than a fornicatress or a polytheist. Similarly, if a woman becomes notorious for fornication and the punishment is carried out upon her, and her repentance is not manifest, it is forbidden for anyone to marry her except a fornicator or a polytheist.

Thus, the verse is decisive (محكمة), remaining in its decisiveness without abrogation (نسخ) or reinterpretation (تأويل). Its qualification by the establishment of the punishment and the manifestation of repentance is something that can be inferred from the context; for the occurrence of the ruling prohibiting marriage after the command to establish the punishment suggests that the intended subjects are the lashed fornicator and fornicatress. Furthermore, applying the terms "fornicator" and "fornicatress" to one who was afflicted by that but then repented with sincere repentance (توبة نصوحا) and made it manifest, is far from the custom and etiquette of the Quran.

The commentators have long-standing disputes and various opinions regarding the meaning of this verse:

Among them is that the discourse is intended to report what those who commit this indecency naturally seek. This is because one whose primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — has become corrupted only inclines toward those who resemble them in corruption and match them in depravity. The fornicator only inclines toward the fornicatress who shares in the indecency, or one who is even more corrupt, namely the polytheist. Likewise, the fornicatress only inclines toward her like, the fornicator, or one more corrupt, the polytheist. Thus, the ruling is based on the general and predominant case, as has been said regarding His saying: الْخَبِيثاتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَالْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثاتِ "Vicious women are for vicious men, and vicious men are for vicious women" (24:26).

Another opinion is that the verse is intended for vilification (التقبيح). The meaning would be: it is fitting for the state of a fornicator that he should not marry except a fornicatress or one lower than her, the polytheist; and it is fitting for the state of a fornicatress that none should marry her except a fornicator or one lower than him, the polytheist. "Marriage" (النكاح) here would mean the contract (العقد). His saying: "and that has been forbidden to the faithful" (وَحُرِّمَ ذلِكَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) would be coordinated with the beginning of the verse, meaning: and fornication has been forbidden to the believers.

The problem with this and the preceding view is their contradiction with the context of the verse, especially the connection of its conclusion to its beginning, as previously indicated.

Another view is that the verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by His saying: وَأَنْكِحُوا الْأَيامى مِنْكُمْ وَالصَّالِحِينَ مِنْ عِبادِكُمْ وَإِمائِكُمْ "Marry off those at home who are single, and those who are fit among your male and female slaves" (24:32).

The flaw in this is that the relationship between the two verses is one of general (العموم) and specific (الخصوص), and a general verse revealed after a specific one does not abrogate it, contrary to those who claim otherwise. However, it might be possible to infer abrogation from His saying: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا الْمُشْرِكاتِ حَتَّى يُؤْمِنَّ وَلأَمَةٌ مُؤْمِنَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِنْ مُشْرِكَةٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ وَلا تُنْكِحُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَتَّى يُؤْمِنُوا وَلَعَبْدٌ مُؤْمِنٌ خَيْرٌ مِنْ مُشْرِكٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكُمْ أُولئِكَ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى النَّارِ وَاللهُ يَدْعُوا إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ وَالْمَغْفِرَةِ بِإِذْنِهِ "Do not marry polytheistic women until they believe. A faithful slave girl is better than a polytheistic woman, though she should please you. And do not marry polytheistic men [to your women] until they believe. A faithful slave is better than a polytheistic man, though he should please you. It is they who invite to the Fire, while Allah invites to Paradise and forgiveness by His leave" (2:221). This is based on the claim that although the verse is a general ruling after a specific one, its tone resists specification (التخصيص), thus making it abrogating regarding the permissibility of marriage between a believer and a polytheist. Some have claimed that the marriage of a disbeliever to a Muslim woman was permissible until the sixth year of the Hijrah, after which the prohibition was revealed; perhaps the verse we are discussing was revealed before that, and the verse of prohibition was revealed after it. There are other opinions regarding the verse which we have omitted due to their manifest weakness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَناتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَداءَ فَاجْلِدُوهُمْ ثَمانِينَ جَلْدَةً "As for those who accuse chaste women and do not bring four witnesses, strike them eighty lashes" (24:4) etc. Accusing (الرمي) — literally "throwing" — is well known, and it is used metaphorically for attributing an undesirable matter to a person, such as fornication or theft, which is slander (القذف). The context bears witness that what is intended is the attribution of fornication to a chaste, virtuous woman (المحصنة العفيفة). "Bringing four witnesses" (الإتيان بأربعة شهداء) — who are the witnesses of fornication — means providing testimony to prove what was alleged. Allah has commanded the establishment of the prescribed punishment (الحد) upon them if they do not provide the testimony, and He ruled them to be transgressors (فسقهم) and that their testimony should never be accepted.

The meaning is: those who slander chaste women with fornication and then do not provide four witnesses to the truth of their slander, lash them eighty lashes for their slander; they are transgressors (فاسقون), and do not ever accept their testimony on any matter.

The verse, as you see, is absolute (مطلقة), encompassing both male and female slanderers, and both free persons and slaves. This is how the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) interpret it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ "excepting those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful." (24:5). The exception (الاستثناء) refers back to the final clause, which is His saying: "and it is they who are the transgressors" (وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ). However, since this clause serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying: "and never accept any testimony from them" (وَلا تَقْبَلُوا لَهُمْ شَهادَةً أَبَداً) — as indicated by the context — the necessary consequence of the removal of the ruling of transgression (الفسق) is the removal of the ruling of the eternal rejection of testimony. Consequently, the exception refers back, in terms of meaning, to both clauses together.

The meaning is: except those who repent after that and reform their deeds, for Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful; He forgives their sin and has mercy on them, so the ruling of transgression is lifted from them, as is the ruling of the eternal rejection of their testimony.

Some have mentioned that the exception refers only to the final clause; thus, if the slanderer repents and reforms after the punishment is carried out, his sin is forgiven, but his testimony is never accepted—contrary to those who say the exception refers to both clauses.

It appears that this disagreement is based on the principle of jurisprudence (المسألة الأصولية) regarding whether an exception occurring after multiple clauses relates to all of them or only the last one. The truth of the matter is that the exception in itself is capable of both, and the determination of one over the other depends on what the contextual clues (قرائن الكلام) require. What the context provides in this verse is the attachment of the exception to the final clause, but its function as a justification necessitates that the preceding clause also be qualified by its meaning, just like the last one, as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْواجَهُمْ وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ شُهَداءُ إِلَّا أَنْفُسُهُمْ — to His saying — مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ "As for those who accuse their wives and have no witnesses except themselves..." (24:6-7). That is, they have no witnesses to testify to what they witnessed—to bear the testimony and then deliver it—except themselves. His saying: "the testimony of any of them shall be four testimonies by Allah" (فَشَهادَةُ أَحَدِهِمْ أَرْبَعُ شَهاداتٍ بِاللهِ) means the testimony of one of them—meaning the accuser, who is one person—shall be four testimonies sworn by Allah that he is indeed among the truthful in what he reports of the accusation.

The meaning of the two verses is: those who accuse their wives and do not have four witnesses who saw what they saw—and this is the nature of the matter assuming they are truthful, for if they went to seek witnesses to bring them to the scene to witness it, the purpose would be lost by the couple's separation—then the testimony that one of them must provide is to testify four times, saying repeatedly: "I call Allah to witness my truthfulness in what I accuse her of," four times, and the fifth is to testify and say: "the curse of Allah be upon me if I am among the liars."

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَدْرَؤُا عَنْهَا الْعَذابَ أَنْ تَشْهَدَ "And it shall avert the punishment from her if she testifies..." (24:8-9). الدرء means to repel or avert, and the "punishment" (العذاب) refers to the prescribed punishment (حد) for fornication. The meaning is that if the woman testifies five times in response to the man's testimonies, that will avert the punishment of fornication from her. Her testimonies are to testify four times, saying: "I swear by Allah that he is indeed among the liars," then she testifies a fifth time, saying: "the wrath of Allah be upon me if he is among the truthful." This is the mutual imprecation (اللعان) — the act of swearing oaths to settle an accusation of adultery between spouses — by which the spouses are separated.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرحْمَتُهُ وَأَنَّ اللهَ تَوَّابٌ حَكِيمٌ "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, and that Allah is all-clement, all-wise..." (24:10). The apodosis (جواب) of "were it not" (لو لا) is omitted, indicated by the conditions mentioned in its protasis. Its meaning is: were it not for Allah's grace, His mercy, His acceptance of repentance, and His wisdom, that which these attributes and actions averted from you would have befallen you. The estimation, based on the conditions in the protasis, is: were it not for the blessing of religion Allah bestowed upon you, His acceptance of repentance for your sinners, and His legislation of laws to organize the affairs of your life, wretchedness would have overtaken you, disobedience and sin would have destroyed you, and the order of your life would have been disrupted by ignorance. And Allah knows best.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Salim, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "And Surah al-Nur was revealed after Surah al-Nisa'. The confirmation of this is that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed to him in Surah al-Nisa': وَاللَّاتِي يَأْتِينَ الْفاحِشَةَ مِنْ نِسائِكُمْ ـ فَاسْتَشْهِدُوا عَلَيْهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةً مِنْكُمْ ـ فَإِنْ شَهِدُوا فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِي الْبُيُوتِ ـ حَتَّى يَتَوَفَّاهُنَّ الْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلاً 'As for those of your women who commit indecency, call four witnesses from among yourselves against them; and if they testify, detain them in [their] houses until death takes them or Allah opens a way for them' (4:15). And the 'way' (السبيل) which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, spoke of is: سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها وَفَرَضْناها ـ وَأَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ الزَّانِيَةُ وَالزَّانِي فَاجْلِدُوا كُلَّ واحِدٍ مِنْهُما مِائَةَ جَلْدَةٍ ـ وَلا تَأْخُذْكُمْ بِهِما رَأْفَةٌ فِي دِينِ اللهِ ـ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ ـ وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذابَهُما طائِفَةٌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ 'A surah which We have sent down and which We have ordained... As for the fornicatress and the fornicator, strike each of them a hundred lashes... and let a group of the believers witness their punishment' (24:1-2)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذابَهُما "and let [a group of the believers] witness their punishment," he said: "Their striking. طائِفَةٌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ 'a group of the believers'—the people are gathered for them when they are lashed."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from Ghiyath ibn Ibrahim, from Ja'far, from his father, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلا تَأْخُذْكُمْ بِهِما رَأْفَةٌ فِي دِينِ اللهِ "and do not let pity for them overcome you in [matters of] Allah’s religion," he said: "In the establishment of the prescribed punishments (الحدود)." And regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذابَهُما طائِفَةٌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "and let a group of the believers witness their punishment," he said: "The group (الطائفة) can be one."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Salim, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "And there was revealed in Medina: الزَّانِي لا يَنْكِحُ إِلَّا زانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً ـ وَالزَّانِيَةُ لا يَنْكِحُها إِلَّا زانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ ـ وَحُرِّمَ ذلِكَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ 'The fornicator shall not marry anyone but a fornicatress or a polytheist... and that has been forbidden to the faithful' (24:3). Thus, Allah did not name the fornicator a believer (مؤمنا) nor the fornicatress a believer (مؤمنة). And the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said—and the people of knowledge do not dispute this—that he said: لا يزني الزاني حين يزني وهو مؤمن ، ولا يسرق السارق حين يسرق وهو مؤمن 'The fornicator does not fornicate while he is a believer, nor does the thief steal while he is a believer.' For when he does that, faith (الإيمان) is stripped from him as a shirt is stripped off."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, he said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: الزَّانِي لا يَنْكِحُ إِلَّا زانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً 'The fornicator shall not marry anyone but a fornicatress or a polytheist.' He said: 'They are women notorious for fornication and men notorious for fornication—they became notorious for it and known by it. And the people today are in that same position—whoever has the prescribed punishment for fornication established against him or is accused of fornication—it is not proper for anyone to marry him until his repentance is known.'"

I say: It was also narrated with its chain from Abu al-Sabbah from him (peace be upon him) similarly. And with its chain from Muhammad ibn Salim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), and its wording is: "They are men and women who, during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), were notorious for fornication. So Allah forbade those men and women. And the people today are in that same position—whoever becomes notorious for any of that and the punishment is established against him, do not marry him until you know of his repentance."

In the same source, with its chain from Hakam ibn Hakim from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "That is only regarding the public (الجهر)." Then he said: "If a person fornicates and then repents, he may marry wherever he wishes."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is recorded by Ahmad, 'Abd ibn Humayd, al-Nasa'i, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan, and Abu Dawud in his Nasikh, from 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar, who said: "There was a woman called Umm Mahzul, and she used to commit fornication with men and stipulate that she would spend on them. A man from the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wanted to marry her, so Allah revealed: الزَّانِيَةُ لا يَنْكِحُها إِلَّا زانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ 'and the fornicatress shall not be married by anyone but a fornicator or a polytheist.'"

I say: Something similar was narrated from several compilers of traditions from Mujahid.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim recorded from Muqatil, who said: "When the Emigrants (المهاجرون) arrived in Medina, they arrived in hardship except for a few of them. Medina was expensive and the hardship was severe. In the market, there were notorious fornicatresses from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). As for the Ansar (الأنصار), among them were Umayyah, the slave-girl of 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy, and Nusaykah bint Umayyah belonging to a man from the Ansar, among other prostitutes from the slave-girls of the Ansar. Each of these women had raised a sign (علامة) over her door so it would be known she was a fornicatress. They were among the most affluent and well-off people of Medina.

Some men from the Muslim Emigrants desired what they earned because of the hardship they were in. They suggested to one another: 'If we were to marry some of these fornicatresses, we could partake of some of their food.' Some said: 'Let us seek the command of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).' They came to him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, hardship has become difficult for us and we find nothing to eat. In the market are prostitutes from the women of the People of the Book and their slave-girls, and slave-girls of the Ansar who earn for themselves. Is it permissible for us to marry them so we may partake of the surplus of what they earn? Then, when we find ourselves self-sufficient, we will leave them.' So Allah revealed: الزَّانِي لا يَنْكِحُ 'The fornicator shall not marry...' the verse. Thus, He forbade the believers from marrying fornicatresses who commit fornication openly and notoriously."

I say: These two narrations only mention the occasion of revelation for His saying: "and the fornicatress shall not be married by anyone but a fornicator or a polytheist," but not for His saying: "The fornicator shall not marry anyone but a fornicatress or a polytheist..."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا "excepting those who repent"—there is a difference of opinion as to what this exception refers to, based on two views: one is that it refers specifically to transgression (الفسق) and not to His saying: "and never accept any testimony from them." ... And the other is that the exception refers to both matters; thus, if he repents, his testimony is accepted whether the punishment was carried out or not—narrated from Ibn 'Abbas... and it is the view of Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, 'Abd al-Razzaq, 'Abd ibn Humayd, and Ibn al-Mundhir recorded from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, who said: "Three people testified to fornication against al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah, but Ziyad refrained. So 'Umar lashed the three and said to them: 'Repent and your testimony will be accepted.' Two men repented, but Abu Bakrah did not repent, so his testimony was never accepted. Abu Bakrah was Ziyad's brother from his mother. When Ziyad's affair became what it became, Abu Bakrah swore never to speak to him, and he did not speak to him until he died."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain from al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "If a slave slanders a free person, he is lashed eighty times." And he said: "This is among the rights of the people (حقوق الناس)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْواجَهُمْ — to His saying — إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ "As for those who accuse their wives... if he is among the truthful" (24:6-9), it was revealed concerning mutual imprecation (اللعان). The cause of this was that when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) returned from the Battle of Tabuk, 'Uwaymir ibn Sa'idah al-'Ajlani, who was from the Ansar, came to him and said: "O Messenger of Allah, my wife has committed fornication with Sharik ibn al-Samha', and she is pregnant by him." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) turned away from him. He repeated the statement, and he turned away from him, until he had done so four times.

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) entered his house and the verse of mutual imprecation was revealed to him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) came out and prayed the 'Asr prayer with the people, then said to 'Uwaymir: "Bring your wife to me, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has revealed Quran concerning you both." He went to her and said: "The Messenger of Allah is calling you." She was of noble standing among her people, so a group came with her. When she entered the mosque, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to 'Uwaymir: "Step forward to the pulpit and perform the imprecation." He asked: "How shall I do it?" He replied: "Step forward and say: 'I call Allah to witness that I am indeed among the truthful in what I have accused her of.'" He stepped forward and said it. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Repeat it." He repeated it until he had done so four times. Then he said to him on the fifth: "The curse of Allah be upon you if you are among the liars in what you have accused her of." So he said on the fifth: "That the curse of Allah be upon him if he is among the liars in what he accused her of." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The curse is binding if you are a liar."

Then he said to him: "Step aside." He stepped aside. Then he said to his wife: "Testify as he testified, otherwise I will establish Allah's prescribed punishment upon you." She looked at the faces of her people and said: "I will not blacken these faces this evening." She stepped forward to the pulpit and said: "I call Allah to witness that 'Uwaymir ibn Sa'idah is among the liars in what he accused me of." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to her: "Repeat it." She repeated it until she had done so four times. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to her: "Curse yourself on the fifth if he is among the truthful in what he accused you of." She said on the fifth: "That the wrath of Allah be upon her if he is among the truthful in what he accused her of." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Woe to you, it is binding if you are a liar."

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to her husband: "Go, she is never permissible for you again." He said: "O Messenger of Allah, what of the wealth I gave her?" He replied: "If you were a liar, it is even further from you than she is; and if you were truthful, it is hers in exchange for what you made permissible of her private parts." The tradition continues.

In al-Majma', in the narration of 'Ikrimah from Ibn 'Abbas: Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah said: "If I were to find a scoundrel between the thighs of a woman, I would not be able to disturb him until I brought four witnesses? By Allah, I would not be able to bring four witnesses until he had finished his business and left; and if I said what I saw, there would be eighty lashes on my back."

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "O assembly of Ansar, do you hear what your leader has said?" They replied: "Do not blame him, for he is a jealous man. He has never married a woman except a virgin, and he never divorced a wife of his such that any man among us dared to marry her." Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah said: "O Messenger of Allah, may my father and mother be sacrificed for you—by Allah, I know it is from Allah and that it is the truth—but I was amazed by it when I informed you." He said: "Indeed, Allah insists on nothing but that." He said: "Allah and His Messenger have spoken the truth."

They did not wait long until a cousin of his called Hilal ibn Umayyah came from a garden of his; he had seen a man with his wife. When morning came, he went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "I came to my family in the evening and found a man with her; I saw him with my eyes and heard him with my ears." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) disliked this until the dislike was seen in his face. Hilal said: "I see the dislike in your face—and Allah knows that I am truthful, and I hope that Allah will provide a way out." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended to have him lashed.

He said: The Ansar gathered and said: "We have been tested by what Sa'd said; shall Hilal be lashed and his testimony invalidated?" Then the revelation descended, and they ceased speaking when they realized the revelation had descended. So Allah the Exalted revealed: وَالَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْواجَهُمْ "As for those who accuse their wives..." the verses.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Rejoice, O Hilal, for Allah the Exalted has provided a way out." He said: "I was hoping for that from Allah the Exalted." He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Send for her." She came, and he performed the mutual imprecation between them. When the imprecation was finished, he separated them and decreed that the child belonged to her—it would not be attributed to a father, nor would her child be slandered.

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "If she brings it forth looking like such and such, it belongs to her husband; and if she brings it forth looking like such and such, it belongs to the one about whom it was said."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several compilers of traditions from Ibn 'Abbas.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو بِٱلْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِّنكُمْ ۚ لَا تَحْسَبُوهُ شَرًّا لَّكُم ۖ بَلْ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُم مَّا ٱكْتَسَبَ مِنَ ٱلْإِثْمِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِى تَوَلَّىٰ كِبْرَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ لَهُۥ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١١)
Indeed those who initiated the calumny are a group from among yourselves. Do not suppose it is bad thing for you. No, it is for your good. Each man among them bears [the onus for] his share in the sin, and as for him who assumed its major burden from among them, there is a great punishment for him. (11)
لَّوْلَآ إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ ظَنَّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ خَيْرًا وَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٢)
When you [first] heard about it, why did not the faithful, men and women, think well of their folks, and say, ‘This is an obvious calumny’? (12)
لَّوْلَا جَآءُو عَلَيْهِ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ ۚ فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلشُّهَدَآءِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٣)
Why did they not bring four witnesses to it? So when they could not bring the witnesses, they are liars in Allah’s sight. (13)
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِى مَآ أَفَضْتُمْ فِيهِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٤)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you in this world and the Hereafter, there would have befallen you a great punishment for what you ventured into, (14)
إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُۥ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَاهِكُم مَّا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ وَتَحْسَبُونَهُۥ هَيِّنًا وَهُوَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَظِيمٌ (١٥)
when you were receiving it on your tongues, and were mouthing something of which you had no knowledge, supposing it to be a light matter, while it was a grave [matter] with Allah. (15)
وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ قُلْتُم مَّا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّتَكَلَّمَ بِهَـٰذَا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ هَـٰذَا بُهْتَـٰنٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٦)
And why did you not, when you heard it, say, ‘It is not for us to say such a thing. [O Allah!] You are immaculate! This is a monstrous calumny!’ (16)
يَعِظُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَن تَعُودُوا۟ لِمِثْلِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧)
Allah advises you lest you should ever repeat the like of it, should you be faithful. (17)
وَيُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٨)
Allah clarifies the signs for you, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (18)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ أَن تَشِيعَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةُ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (١٩)
Indeed those who want indecency to spread among the faithful—there is a painful punishment for them in the world and the Hereafter, and Allah knows and you do not know. (19)
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٠)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, and that Allah is all-kind, all-merciful… (20)
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّبِعْ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ ۚ وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢١)
O you who have faith! Do not follow in Satan’s steps. Whoever follows in Satan’s steps [should know that] he indeed prompts [you to commit] indecent and wrongful acts. Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever become pure. But Allah purifies whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (21)
وَلَا يَأْتَلِ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْفَضْلِ مِنكُمْ وَٱلسَّعَةِ أَن يُؤْتُوٓا۟ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلْيَعْفُوا۟ وَلْيَصْفَحُوٓا۟ ۗ أَلَا تُحِبُّونَ أَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٢)
The well-off and the opulent among you should not vow that they will not give [any longer] to the relatives and the needy, and to those who have migrated in the way of Allah, and let them excuse and forbear. Do you not love that Allah should forgive you? Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (22)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ٱلْغَـٰفِلَـٰتِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ لُعِنُوا۟ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٣)
Indeed those who accuse chaste and unwary faithful women shall be cursed in this world and the Hereafter, and there shall be a great punishment for them (23)
يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٤)
on the day when witness shall be given against them by their tongues, their hands, and their feet concerning what they used to do. (24)
يَوْمَئِذٍ يُوَفِّيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ دِينَهُمُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٢٥)
On that day, Allah will pay them in full their due recompense, and they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Reality. (25)
ٱلْخَبِيثَـٰتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَٱلْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثَـٰتِ ۖ وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَٱلطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مُبَرَّءُونَ مِمَّا يَقُولُونَ ۖ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٢٦)
Vicious women are for vicious men, and vicious men for vicious women. Good women are for good men, and good men for good women. These are absolved of what they say [about them]. For them is forgiveness and a noble provision. (26)

(Commentary)

The verses refer to the Incident of the Slander (الإفك). The Sunnis have narrated that the person accused in the story of the slander was the Mother of the Believers, ‘A’ishah. The Shi‘a have narrated that it was Mariyah the Copt, the mother of Ibrahim, whom al-Muqawqis, the King of Egypt, had gifted to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Neither of the two sets of narrations is free from certain issues, as will be discussed in the forthcoming "Discussion on Traditions."

Therefore, it is more appropriate to examine the text of the verses independently of both sets of narrations. However, it is certain that the slander (الإفك) mentioned therein pertained to some members of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) household—either his wife or the mother of his child. This is perhaps alluded to by His saying, the Exalted: وَتَحْسَبُونَهُ هَيِّناً وَهُوَ عِنْدَ اللهِ عَظِيمٌ "while you considered it a light matter, and it was a grave matter with Allah" (24:15). Similarly, what can be inferred from the verses is that the talk had spread among them, they had indulged in it, and other such indications found in the verses.

What is gathered from the verses is that they accused some of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) household of indecency (الفحشاء). The accusers were a group (عصبة) from among the people, and the talk spread among the people, one receiving it from another. Some hypocrites or those in whose hearts was a sickness assisted in disseminating the talk, out of a desire that indecency should spread among those who believe. Thus, Allah revealed the verses and defended His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِنْكُمْ "Indeed those who brought the lie are a group from among you" (24:11), etc. Slander (الإفك), as mentioned by al-Raghib, is falsehood in general. The root of its meaning is anything turned away from the face it ought to be upon—such as a belief turned from truth to falsehood, an act turned from beautiful to ugly, or a statement turned from truthfulness to lying. It has been used in the speech of the Exalted in all these meanings.

He also mentioned that a group (العصبة) is a faction that is partisan and mutually supportive; it is said to be between ten and forty people.

The address in this verse and those following it is to the general body of believers—those who outwardly manifest faith, encompassing the true believer, the hypocrite, and the one in whose heart is a sickness. As for the view of some that the first four addresses—or the second, third, and fourth—are directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the accused woman, and the accused man, this involves a disjointing of the addresses occurring in the first ten verses, which contain over twenty addresses, most of which are undoubtedly to the general body of believers.

Even worse is the view of others that these four or three addresses are for those believers who were distressed by the event. In addition to necessitating a disjointing of consecutive addresses, this is a manifest conjecture.

The meaning is: Indeed, those who brought this lie—the definite article in "the slander" (الإفك) refers to the specific known event—are a numbered group from among you, connected to one another. This indicates a conspiracy among them to disseminate this news to impugn the integrity of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) household and to disgrace him among the people.

This is the benefit of the report in His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِنْكُمْ "Indeed those who brought the lie are a group from among you." It is not merely to console the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or to console him and those distressed by this slander, as some have mentioned, for the context does not support that.

His saying: لا تَحْسَبُوهُ شَرًّا لَكُمْ بَلْ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ "Do not consider it a bad thing for you; rather it is good for you" (24:11). Since the address is to the general body of believers, the requirement is that the negation of it being "bad" and the affirmation of it being "good" means that it is part of the felicity of a righteous society that the people of deviation and corruption be distinguished within it, so that the believers may have insight into their affairs and rise to reform the corrupted members among them. This is especially true in a religious society connected to revelation (الوحي), where revelation descends upon them when such events occur to exhort them and remind them of what they were heedless of or negligent toward, so they may take precautions for their religion and become cognizant of what concerns them.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is His subsequent saying: لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ مَا اكْتَسَبَ مِنَ الْإِثْمِ "For every one of them is what he has earned of the sin" (24:11). For sin (الإثم) — the evil trace that remains with a person from committing a transgression — is mentioned here, and the apparent meaning of the sentence is that the people of the slander who brought it will be known by their sin and distinguished by it among you. Thus, they are disgraced by it instead of their intention to disgrace the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

As for the view of those who say that it being "good for them" means they will be rewarded for being falsely accused, just as the people of the slander will be burdened with sin, this is based on the address being specifically for the accused, and you have already seen the invalidity of that.

His saying: وَالَّذِي تَوَلَّى كِبْرَهُ مِنْهُمْ لَهُ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ "and as for him among them who took upon himself the bulk of it, for him there is a great punishment" (24:11). They have interpreted "the bulk of it" (كبره) to mean the greatest part of it, and the pronoun refers to the slander (الإفك). The meaning is: He who took upon himself the majority of the slander and persisted in disseminating it among the people from these liars, for him is a great punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: لَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ ظَنَّ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ خَيْراً وَقالُوا هذا إِفْكٌ مُبِينٌ "When you heard it, why did not the faithful, men and women, think well of their own people, and say, ‘This is a manifest lie’?" (24:12). This is a rebuke to them because they did not reject the talk when they heard it and did not think well of the one accused.

His saying: ظَنَّ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ "the faithful, men and women, think well of their own people [literally: themselves]" is an instance of using a noun in place of a pronoun; the original would be "why did you not think well of yourselves." The reason for replacing the pronoun with the description [of faith] is to indicate the cause of the ruling. For the attribute of faith (الإيمان) is naturally deterrent; it restrains the one characterized by it from indecency (الفحشاء) and wrongdoing (المنكر) in speech and action. Therefore, one characterized by it must think well of others characterized by it and avoid speaking about them without knowledge, for they are all as a single soul in their characterization by faith and its requirements and effects.

The meaning is: Why, when you heard the slander, did you not think well of the one accused? For you are all believers, some of you from others, and the accused is from yourselves. It is incumbent upon a believer to think well of a believer and not to describe him with that of which he has no knowledge.

His saying: قالُوا هذا إِفْكٌ مُبِينٌ "and say, ‘This is a manifest lie’"—meaning the believing men and women who heard it should have said (meaning: you should have said): "This is a manifest lie." This is because a report for which the reporter has no knowledge, and a claim for which the claimant has no evidence, is legally (شرعاً) judged as a lie, whether it is true or false in reality. The evidence for this is His saying in the following verse: فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِالشُّهَداءِ فَأُولئِكَ عِنْدَ اللهِ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ "Since they did not bring the witnesses, they are the liars in Allah’s sight" (24:13).

His saying, the Exalted: لَوْ لا جاؤُ عَلَيْهِ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَداءَ فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِالشُّهَداءِ فَأُولئِكَ عِنْدَ اللهِ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ "Why did they not bring four witnesses to it? Since they did not bring the witnesses, they are the liars in Allah’s sight" (24:13). That is, if they were truthful in what they said and accused, they would have established testimony for it, which in the case of adultery (الزنا) requires four witnesses. Since they did not bring the witnesses, they are legally judged as liars, because a claim without evidence is a lie and a slander.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيما أَفَضْتُمْ فِيهِ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you in this world and the Hereafter, a great punishment would have surely overtaken you for what you indulged in" (24:14). The people's "indulging" (إفاضة) in the talk is their plunging into it.

His saying: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ "Were it not for Allah’s grace," etc., is coordinated with His saying: لَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ "When you heard it," etc. It contains a second assault of rebuke upon the believers. In restricting the grace and mercy with the phrase فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ "in this world and the Hereafter," there is an indication that the punishment mentioned at the end is the punishment of this world and the Hereafter.

The meaning is: Were it not for Allah’s grace upon you and His mercy in this world and the Hereafter, a great punishment in this world and the Hereafter would have reached you because of the slander you plunged into.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِكُمْ ما لَيْسَ لَكُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ "when you were receiving it with your tongues and were saying with your mouths that of which you had no knowledge" (24:15), etc. The adverbial clause is connected to His saying: أَفَضْتُمْ "you indulged." A person's "receiving" (تلقي) a statement is taking the statement that another has cast toward him. Restricting the receiving to "tongues" (بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ) is to indicate that it was a mere transfer of speech from tongue to tongue without verification or reflection.

Accordingly, His saying: وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِكُمْ ما لَيْسَ لَكُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ "and were saying with your mouths that of which you had no knowledge" is a form of explanatory coordination. Restricting it also with the phrase بِأَفْواهِكُمْ "with your mouths" is to point out that the speech did not stem from heart-felt verification and clarity; it had no abode except the mouths, not exceeding them.

The meaning is: You indulged and plunged into it when you were taking it and transferring it from tongue to tongue, uttering that of which you had no knowledge.

His saying: وَتَحْسَبُونَهُ هَيِّناً وَهُوَ عِنْدَ اللهِ عَظِيمٌ "while you considered it a light matter, and it was a grave matter with Allah" (24:15). That is, you thought receiving it with your tongues and speaking with your mouths without knowledge was easy, while with Allah it is grave because it is calumny (بهتان) and fabrication. Furthermore, the matter is linked to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the spread of such a slander among the people disgraces him in their eyes and corrupts the matter of the religious mission.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ قُلْتُمْ ما يَكُونُ لَنا أَنْ نَتَكَلَّمَ بِهذا سُبْحانَكَ هذا بُهْتانٌ عَظِيمٌ "And why did you not say, when you heard it, ‘It is not for us to say such a thing. [O Allah!] You are immaculate! This is a monstrous calumny’?" (24:16). This is coordinated after coordination with His saying: لَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ "When you heard it," etc. It contains a third assault of rebuke upon the believers. His saying: سُبْحانَكَ "You are immaculate!" is an interjection of glorification for Allah, the Glorified. It is part of the etiquette of the Quran to declare Allah’s transcendence through glorification (التسبيح) whenever any sanctified person is declared innocent.

Calumny (البهتان) is fabrication (الافتراء); it is so named because it "stuns" (يبهت) the person against whom it is fabricated. It being a "monstrous calumny" is because it is a fabrication against honor, especially when its subject pertains to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It was calumny because it was reporting without knowledge and a claim without evidence, as previously mentioned in His saying: فإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِالشُّهَداءِ فَأُولئِكَ عِنْدَ اللهِ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ "Since they did not bring the witnesses, they are the liars in Allah’s sight." The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: يَعِظُكُمُ اللهُ أَنْ تَعُودُوا لِمِثْلِهِ أَبَداً "Allah exhorts you lest you should ever repeat the like of it" (24:17) until the end of the two verses. This is an exhortation by forbidding the return to the like of it. The meaning of the two verses is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ أَنْ تَشِيعَ الْفاحِشَةُ فِي الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "Indeed those who want indecency to spread among the faithful" (24:19) until the end of the verse. If the verse was revealed among the verses of the slander and is connected to what preceded it, and its specific instance is the accusation of adultery without evidence, then its content is a threat to the accusers who indulged in the slander, because it is an indecency (فاحشة) and its dissemination among the believers was out of their love for indecency to spread.

Thus, what is meant by indecency (الفاحشة) is absolute obscenity, such as adultery, slander (القذف), and the like. The love for its spread—including slander—among the believers necessitates a painful punishment for those who love it in this world and the Hereafter.

Accordingly, there is no reason to interpret the punishment in this world as the legal penalty (الحد), since the love for the spread of indecency is not something that necessitates the legal penalty. Yes, if the definite article in "the indecency" (الفاحشة) refers to the specific slander, and the love for its spread was a metaphor for the act of spreading it through indulgence, receiving it with tongues, and transferring it, then it would be possible to interpret the punishment as the legal penalty. However, the context does not support this.

Furthermore, the act of accusing, as soon as it occurs once, necessitates the legal penalty; there is no reason to restrict it to the intention of spreading it, nor is there a subtle point (نكتة) necessitating that.

His saying: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ "and Allah knows and you do not know" (24:19) is an emphasis and a magnification of the divine displeasure and wrath it contains, even if people are ignorant of it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you" (24:20) is a repetition of the bestowal of favor, and its meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ وَمَنْ يَتَّبِعْ خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ فَإِنَّهُ يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ "O you who have faith! Do not follow in Satan’s steps. Whoever follows in Satan’s steps [should know that] he prompts indecency and wrongdoing" (24:21). The interpretation of this verse has already been provided under verse 208 of Surah al-Baqarah in the second volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ ما زَكى مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَداً "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure" (24:21) until the end of the verse. This is a return after return to the bestowal of grace and mercy. This emphasis is not devoid of support for the fact that the slander pertained to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and it is only due to his nobility in the sight of Allah, the Glorified.

In this third instance, He has explicitly stated the apodosis of "were it not" (لو لا), which is His saying: ما زَكى مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَداً "not one of you would ever be pure." This is something indicated by reason, for the Bestower of good and felicity is Allah, the Glorified. Quranic teaching also provides this, as He, the Exalted, said: بِيَدِكَ الْخَيْرُ "All good is in Your hand" (3:26), and He said: ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللهِ "Whatever good befalls you is from Allah" (4:79).

His saying: وَلكِنَّ اللهُ يُزَكِّي مَنْ يَشاءُ وَاللهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ "but Allah purifies whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing" (24:21) is a digression from what preceded it. Thus, He, the Exalted, purifies whomever He wishes; the matter is left to His will. He does not wish except to purify the one who has prepared himself for it and asked Him with the tongue of that preparation. To this points His saying: وَاللهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ "and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing"—meaning He is all-hearing of the request of the one who asks Him for purification, and all-knowing of the state of the one who has prepared for it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَأْتَلِ أُولُوا الْفَضْلِ مِنْكُمْ وَالسَّعَةِ أَنْ يُؤْتُوا أُولِي الْقُرْبى وَالْمَساكِينَ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ "The well-off and the opulent among you should not vow not to give to the relatives and the needy, and those who have migrated in the way of Allah" (24:22), etc. The word al-i’tila’ (الايتلاء) means falling short, abandoning, or swearing an oath. Each of these three meanings is not without relevance. The meaning is: Let not the people of excellence and abundance among you—meaning the wealthy—fall short in giving to the relatives, the needy, and the migrants in the way of Allah from their wealth; or let them not abandon giving to them; or let them not swear not to give to them. "And let them excuse and forbear." Then He incited them by His saying: أَلا تُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَكُمْ وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ "Do you not love that Allah should forgive you? And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful."

In the verse—on the assumption that it was revealed among the group of verses and is connected to them—there is an indication that some believers intended to cut off what they used to give to some of the people of the slander. Allah forbade them from that and urged them to continue the giving, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْغافِلاتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ لُعِنُوا فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ "Indeed those who accuse chaste and believing women who are unaware [of the accusation] are cursed in this world and the Hereafter, and there is a great punishment for them" (24:23). The three attributes—chastity (الإحصان), unawareness (الغفلة), and faith (الإيمان)—were mentioned to indicate the gravity of the sin. For each of chastity (meaning virtue), unawareness, and faith is a complete cause for the accusation being an injustice (ظلم), the accuser being a wrongdoer (ظالم), and the accused being wronged (مظلومة). When these are combined, the injustice becomes greater and greater. Its recompense is the curse in this world and the Hereafter and a great punishment. The verse is general, even if the occasion of its revelation (شأن النزول)—if it was revealed among the verses of the slander—was specific.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "on the day when their tongues, their hands and their feet shall bear witness against them concerning what they used to do" (24:24). The adverbial clause is connected to His saying in the previous verse: وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ "and there is a great punishment for them."

What is meant by His saying: بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "concerning what they used to do," as required by its absolute sense, is absolute evil deeds—as has been said—not specifically the act of accusing, such that their tongues, hands, and feet would witness against their accusation. Rather, what is meant by the testimony is the testimony of the limbs against the evils and sins according to what suits them. Whatever was of the nature of speech—such as slander, lying, backbiting, and the like—the tongues witness against it. Whatever was of the nature of actions—such as theft, walking for slander, instigation, and others—the rest of the limbs witness against it. Since most sins of action belong to the hands and feet, they were specifically mentioned.

In reality, the witness against every act is the limb from which it issued, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَأَبْصارُهُمْ وَجُلُودُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "their hearing, their sight and their skins will bear witness against them concerning what they used to do" (41:20), and His saying: إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤادَ كُلُّ أُولئِكَ كانَ عَنْهُ مَسْؤُلاً "Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable" (18:36), and His saying: الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى أَفْواهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ "Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness to what they used to earn" (36:65). The discussion on the testimony of the limbs on the Day of Resurrection will come in an independent study in the commentary of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَئِذٍ يُوَفِّيهِمُ اللهُ دِينَهُمُ الْحَقَّ وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ "On that day Allah will pay them in full their due recompense, and they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Truth" (24:25). What is meant by "religion" (الدين) is recompense (الجزاء), as in His saying: مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ "Master of the Day of Retribution" (1:4). To "pay in full" (توفية) a thing is to grant it completely and perfectly. The meaning is: On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will give them their true recompense, a complete and perfect giving, and they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Truth.

This is considering the connection of the verse to what precedes it and its occurrence in the context of what has passed. As for considering it independently in itself, it is possible that "religion" (الدين) is intended as a synonym for "creed" (الملة), which is the way of life. This is a high meaning that refers to the manifestation of realities to man on the Day of Resurrection, and it would be more suitable for His saying: وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ "and they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Truth."

The verse is among the most brilliant of Quranic verses; it explains the meaning of the knowledge of Allah. For His saying: وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ "and they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Truth" informs that He, the Exalted, is the Truth upon Whom there is no veil in any way or under any estimation. He is among the most self-evident of self-evident truths (أبده البديهيات) to which ignorance does not attach. However, the self-evident is sometimes overlooked. Thus, knowledge of Him, the Exalted, is the lifting of heedlessness from Him, which is sometimes expressed as "knowledge." This is what appears to them on the Day of Resurrection, so they know that Allah is the Manifest Truth.

To the like of this points His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22).

His saying, the Exalted: الْخَبِيثاتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَالْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثاتِ وَالطَّيِّباتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَالطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّباتِ "Vicious women are for vicious men, and vicious men for vicious women. Good women are for good men, and good men for good women" (24:26), etc. The end of the verse, أُولئِكَ مُبَرَّؤُنَ مِمَّا يَقُولُونَ "These are absolved of what they say," is evidence that what is meant by the vicious and the good are women and men characterized by viciousness and goodness. Thus, the verse is part of the completion of the verses of the slander, connected to them and sharing in their context. It is general, with no specification from the perspective of the wording at all.

What is meant by the "good" (الطيب) which necessitates their being absolved of what they say—according to what the previous verses indicate—is the meaning required by their characterization by faith (الإيمان) and chastity (الإحصان). Thus, the believing men and women, along with chastity, are good men and good women, each group being specific to its counterpart. By virtue of faith and chastity, they are protected and legally absolved (مبرءون شرعاً) from accusation without evidence. They are judged, by virtue of their faith, as having forgiveness, as He, the Exalted, said: وَآمِنُوا بِهِ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ "and believe in Him, He will forgive you some of your sins" (46:31), and they have a noble provision, which is the good life in this world and the fair reward in the Hereafter, as He said: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do" (16:97).

What is meant by "viciousness" (الخبث) in the vicious men and women—who are the non-believers—is the loathsome state necessitated for them by their characterization by disbelief (الكفر). Their vicious women have been specified for their vicious men, and their vicious men for their vicious women, by virtue of homogeneity and essential affinity (المسانخة). They are not absolved from being characterized by indecency—though this is not a judgment that they have actually committed it.

From what has preceded, it has become clear:

First: That the verse is general according to the wording, describing the believing men and women as good. This does not contradict the specificity of its occasion of revelation and its application to it.

Second: That it indicates they are all legally judged to be innocent of what they are accused of as long as no evidence is established for it.

Third: That they are judged to have forgiveness and noble provision. All of this is an apparent ruling due to their nobility in the sight of Allah because of their faith. The disbelievers are the opposite of that.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Muslim, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Shu‘ab, from ‘A’ishah, who said: "Whenever the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intended to go out on a journey, he would cast lots among his wives. Whichever of them had her arrow come out, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would take her with him." ‘A’ishah said: "He cast lots among us in a campaign he undertook, and my arrow came out. So I went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) after the [verse of] the veil (الحجاب) had been revealed. I was carried in my litter (هودج) and stayed in it. We traveled until the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) finished that campaign of his and returned.

As we approached Medina, returning, he announced the departure one night. I stood up when they announced the departure and walked until I had passed the army. When I had finished my business, I returned to my mount. Suddenly, I noticed that a necklace of mine made of Dhafar beads (جزع ظفار) had broken. I searched for my necklace, and searching for it delayed me. The group who used to carry me came and lifted my litter, mounting it onto my camel that I used to ride, thinking that I was inside it. Women at that time were light; flesh had not burdened them. A woman would only eat a small morsel (العلقة) of food. So the people did not find the lightness of the litter strange when they lifted it. I was a young girl of recent age. They sent the camel off and traveled. I found my necklace after the army had moved on. I came to their campsites, and there was neither a caller nor a respondent. So I headed for my place where I had been, thinking they would miss me and return for me. While I was sitting in my place, my eyes overcame me and I slept.

Safwan ibn al-Mu‘attal al-Sulami, then al-Dhakwani, was behind the army. He traveled by night (أدلج) and reached my place by morning. He saw the silhouette of a sleeping person and came to me. He recognized me when he saw me, as he used to see me before the veil. I woke up at his saying 'To Allah we belong and to Him we shall return' (استرجاعه) when he recognized me. I covered my face with my outer garment (جلباب). By Allah, he did not speak a single word to me, nor did I hear a word from him other than his istirja‘, until he knelt his mount and stepped on its forelegs. I rode it, and he set off leading the mount with me until we reached the army after they had halted in the heat of the midday. Then, those who perished, perished.

The one who took upon himself the bulk of the slander was ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul. We arrived in Medina, and I fell ill for a month after our arrival. People were indulging in the talk of the people of the slander, while I was unaware of any of that. What made me suspicious during my illness was that I did not recognize from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) the kindness I used to see from him when I was ill. He would only enter upon me, offer salutations, and then say: 'How is that one (تيكم)?' then leave. That is what made me suspicious, yet I did not sense the evil. Eventually, I went out after I had recovered. Umm Mistah went out with me toward al-Manasi‘ (المناصع), which was our place of relieving ourselves. We used to go out only from night to night, and that was before we took latrines (الكنف) near our houses. Our practice was that of the early Arabs in going out to the open for relief; we found it offensive to have latrines near our houses.

So I and Umm Mistah went out. Then I and Umm Mistah returned toward my house. We had lifted (أشرعنا) our garments. Umm Mistah tripped over her cloak (مرطها) and said: 'May Mistah perish!' I said to her: 'Evil is what you have said! Do you revile a man who witnessed Badr?' She said: 'O you (أي هنتاه)! Have you not heard what he said?' I said: 'And what did he say?' So she informed me of the talk of the people of the slander, and I increased in illness upon my illness.

When I returned to my house, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered upon me, offered salutations, and then said: 'How is that one?' I said: 'Will you permit me to go to my parents?'" She said: "And I, at that time, wanted to verify the news from them." She said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) permitted me, so I came to my parents. I said to my mother: 'O mother, what are the people talking about?' She said: 'O my daughter, take it easy on yourself. By Allah, seldom is there a beautiful woman with a man who loves her, and she has co-wives, but that they increase [talk] against her.' I said: 'Glory be to Allah! Have the people really spoken of this?' I cried that night until morning; my tears did not cease (لا يرقأ), nor did I taste sleep. Then I spent the morning crying.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib and Usamah ibn Zayd when the revelation was delayed, to consult them regarding separating from his wife. As for Usamah, he advised the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with what he knew of the innocence of his wife and what he knew in himself of affection for them. He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, she is your wife, and we know nothing but good.' As for ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, he said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Allah has not made it narrow for you; women other than her are many. If you ask the maidservant, she will tell you the truth.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called Barirah and said: 'O Barirah, have you seen anything that makes you suspicious?' Barirah said: 'No, by the One who sent you with the truth! I have not seen any matter in her that I would find fault with, more than that she is a young girl of recent age who sleeps away from her family's dough, so the domestic animal (الداجن) comes and eats it.'

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stood up and sought justification (استعذر) that day against ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy. He said while on the pulpit: 'O assembly of Muslims, who will justify me against a man whose harm has reached me regarding my household? By Allah, I have known nothing of my wife but good. And they have mentioned a man of whom I have known nothing but good, and he never entered upon my wife except with me.'

Sa‘d ibn Mu‘adh al-Ansari stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I will justify you against him. If he is from al-Aws, I will strike his neck. If he is from our brothers from the Banu al-Khazraj, you command us and we will carry out your command.' Then Sa‘d ibn ‘Ubadah stood up—he was the leader of al-Khazraj and had been a righteous man before that, but partisan zeal (الحمية) carried him away—and said to Sa‘d: 'By the life of Allah, you lie! You shall not kill him, nor are you able to kill him.' Then Usayd ibn Hudayr—the cousin of Sa‘d—stood up and said to Sa‘d ibn ‘Ubadah: 'You lie! We shall surely kill him, for you are a hypocrite arguing on behalf of the hypocrites.' The two tribes, al-Aws and al-Khazraj, became agitated until they were about to fight, while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was standing on the pulpit. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) continued to calm them until they were silent and he was silent.

I cried that day of mine; my tears did not cease, nor did I taste sleep. My parents spent the morning with me, and I had cried for two nights and a day, not tasting sleep nor my tears ceasing. My parents thought that crying would split my liver.

While they were sitting with me and I was crying, a woman from the Ansar sought permission to enter. I permitted her, and she sat crying with me. While we were in that state, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered upon us and sat down. He had not sat with me since what was said was said before that. He had remained for a month without any revelation coming to him regarding my matter. He recited the testimony of faith (تشهد) when he sat, then said: 'To proceed: O ‘A’ishah, it has reached me concerning you such and such. If you are innocent, Allah will soon declare your innocence. If you have committed a sin, then seek Allah’s forgiveness and repent to Him. For when a servant confesses his sin and then repents, Allah accepts his repentance.'

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) finished his speech, my tears subsided (قلص) until I did not feel a single drop. I said to my father: 'Answer the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on my behalf.' He said: 'By Allah, I do not know what to say to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).' I said to my mother: 'Answer the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on my behalf.' She said: 'By Allah, I do not know what to say to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'

I said, being a young girl of recent age who did not read much of the Quran: 'By Allah, I know that you have heard this talk until it has settled in your souls and you have believed it. If I were to tell you that I am innocent—and Allah knows that I am innocent—you would not believe me. And if I were to confess to you a matter—and Allah knows that I am innocent of it—you would believe me. By Allah, I find no example for me and you except the saying of the father of Yusuf: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ وَاللهُ الْمُسْتَعانُ عَلى ما تَصِفُونَ "So [my recourse is] a goodly patience, and it is Allah whose help is to be sought against what you describe" (12:18).'

Then I turned away and lay down on my bed. I knew then that I was innocent and that Allah would declare my innocence. But by Allah, I did not think that Allah would reveal regarding my matter a revelation to be recited. My own estimation of myself was too humble for Allah to speak regarding me in a matter to be recited. However, I hoped that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would see a vision (رؤيا) by which Allah would declare my innocence.

She said: 'By Allah, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had not left his seat, nor had anyone from the household gone out, until revelation was sent down to him. He was overtaken by what used to overtake him of the intensity (البرحاء) during revelation, until sweat would drop from him like pearls, even on a wintry day, because of the weight of the speech revealed to him. When it was lifted from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he was laughing. The first word he spoke was: "Rejoice, O ‘A’ishah! As for Allah, He has declared your innocence." My mother said: "Stand up to him." I said: "By Allah, I will not stand up to him, nor will I praise anyone but Allah who revealed my innocence."' And Allah revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِنْكُمْ "Indeed those who brought the lie are a group from among you"—all ten verses.

When Allah revealed this regarding my innocence, Abu Bakr—who used to spend on Mistah ibn Uthathah due to his kinship to him and his poverty—said: 'By Allah, I will never spend anything on Mistah after what he said about ‘A’ishah.' Then Allah revealed: وَلا يَأْتَلِ أُولُوا الْفَضْلِ مِنْكُمْ وَالسَّعَةِ أَنْ يُؤْتُوا أُولِي الْقُرْبى وَالْمَساكِينَ "The well-off and the opulent among you should not vow not to give to the relatives and the needy"—until His saying—رَحِيمٌ "all-merciful." Abu Bakr said: 'By Allah, I love that Allah should forgive me.' So he returned to Mistah the maintenance he used to spend on him and said: 'By Allah, I will never withdraw it from him.'
