‘A’ishah said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to ask Zaynab bint Jahsh about my matter. He said: "O Zaynab, what have you known or seen?" She said: "O Messenger of Allah, I protect my hearing and my sight; I have known nothing but good." She was the one who used to rival me among the wives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but Allah protected her through godwariness (الورع). Her sister Hamnah began to fight on her behalf, so she perished among those who perished of the people of the slander.'"

I say: The narration is reported through other chains from ‘A’ishah as well, and from ‘Umar, Ibn ‘Abbas, Abu Hurayrah, Abu al-Yasar al-Ansari, Umm Ruman (the mother of ‘A’ishah), and others, and it contains some variations.

In them, it is mentioned that those who brought the slander were ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, Mistah ibn Uthathah—who was a veteran of Badr and among the foremost early migrants (المهاجرين)—Hassan ibn Thabit, and Hamnah, the sister of Zaynab, the wife of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In them, it is mentioned that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called them after the verses of the slander were revealed and administered the legal penalty (حد) to all of them, except that he administered two penalties to ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy. He administered two penalties to him because whoever slanders the wife of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is liable for two penalties.

In these narrations, despite their similarity in recounting the story, there are problems from several aspects:

First: What is certain from their context is that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in doubt regarding ‘A’ishah’s matter after the slander occurred, as indicated by the change in his state toward her in terms of kindness during the days of her illness and afterward until the verses were revealed. This is also indicated by her saying to him when the verses were revealed and he gave her the glad tidings: "By the praise of Allah, not by your praise." In some narrations, she said to her father, whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had sent to give her the glad tidings of the revelation of the excuse: "By the praise of Allah, not by the praise of your companion who sent you," meaning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). In another narration from her: When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) exhorted her to repent to Allah if anything had occurred from her, and there was a woman sitting in the room, ‘A’ishah said to him: "Are you not ashamed before this woman to mention such a thing?" It is well known that this type of speech, based on insult and disparagement, would not have issued from her had she not found the Prophet in doubt regarding her matter. All of this is in addition to the explicit statement in the narration of ‘Umar: "There was something in the heart of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from what they said."

In short, the indication of the general body of narrations that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in doubt regarding her matter until the revelation of the excuse is beyond doubt. This is something from which his station (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is far removed. How could it be otherwise, when He, the Glorified, says: لَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ ظَنَّ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ خَيْراً وَقالُوا هذا إِفْكٌ مُبِينٌ "When you heard it, why did not the faithful, men and women, think well of their own people, and say, ‘This is a manifest lie’?" Thus, He rebukes the believing men and women for their ill-suspicion and their failure to reject what they heard of the slander. Among the requirements of faith is thinking well of the believers, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the most worthy of being characterized by that and avoiding ill-suspicion, which is a sin, and he possesses the station of prophethood and divine inerrancy (العصمة).

Furthermore, He explicitly states in His speech that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is characterized by that, as He says: وَمِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ رَسُولَ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ "Among them are those who molest the Prophet and say, ‘He is [all] ear.’ Say, ‘An ear that is good for you: he has faith in Allah and trusts the faithful, and is a mercy for those of you who have faith.’ As for those who molest the Apostle of Allah, there is a painful punishment for them" (9:61).

Moreover, we say: The infiltration of indecency into the household of the Prophet would repel hearts from him. Therefore, it is mandatory that Allah, the Glorified, purify the courtyard of the wives of the prophets from the stain of adultery and indecency; otherwise, the mission would be futile. By this rational proof, their chastity is established in reality, not merely outwardly. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is more knowledgeable of this proof than we are; how then was it permissible for him to doubt the matter of his wife due to the accusation of an accuser or the spread of a slander?

Second: What the narrations indicate is that the talk of the slander was circulating among the people from the time the people of the slander began it until it concluded with their legal punishment, a period of more than a month. The ruling for slander (القذف) in the absence of witnesses was already known—which is the flogging of the accuser and the legal exoneration of the accused. What then is the meaning of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) hesitation in administering the penalty to the people of the slander for this long duration, and his waiting for revelation regarding her matter until it spread among the people, was received by tongues, and traveled with the riders, and the breach widened for the repairer? What the revelation brought of the excuse does not add to what the verse of slander (آية القذف) determines of the innocence of the accused as an apparent legal ruling.

If it is said: What descended of the excuse was her innocence in reality and the purity of her character in the matter itself, and this is something for which the verse of the penalty for slander is not sufficient; perhaps his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) patience for this long duration was for this reason.

I say: There is no indication of that in any of these sixteen verses. Rather, it is established by the previous rational proof indicating the purity of the houses of the prophets from the stain of indecency.

As for the first ten verses, which contain a trace of specificity, the clearest of them in indicating her innocence is His saying, the Exalted: لَوْ لا جاؤُ عَلَيْهِ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَداءَ فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِالشُّهَداءِ فَأُولئِكَ عِنْدَ اللهِ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ "Why did they not bring four witnesses to it? Since they did not bring the witnesses, they are the liars in Allah’s sight." In this, their lying is inferred from their failure to bring witnesses. It is clear that the failure to establish testimony is only evidence of apparent innocence—meaning the legal ruling of innocence—rather than real innocence, due to the obvious lack of necessary correlation.

As for the last six verses, His saying: الطَّيِّباتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَالطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّباتِ "Good women are for good men, and good men for good women," etc., is general without any specification from the perspective of the wording. Thus, the innocence it establishes is shared among all those accused without the establishment of evidence from the believing men and women. It is clear that the innocence suitable for this meaning is legal innocence.

The truth is that there is no escape from this problem except by saying that the verse of slander (آية القذف) had not been revealed before the incident of the slander; rather, it was revealed after it. The reason for his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) hesitation was the absence of Allah’s ruling regarding the event, so he was waiting for the heavenly ruling in the matter of the slander.

Among the clearest evidence for this is what is in the narration regarding the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seeking justification against the accuser in the mosque, and Sa‘d ibn Mu‘adh saying what he said, and Sa‘d ibn ‘Ubadah arguing with him, and the disagreement of al-Aws and al-Khazraj in the presence of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). In the narration of ‘Umar, after mentioning the disagreement of Ibn Mu‘adh and Ibn ‘Ubadah: "This one said: 'O people of al-Aws!' and that one said: 'O people of al-Khazraj!' and they struck each other with sandals and stones and slapped one another," the tradition continues. If the verse of slander had been revealed before that and the ruling of the penalty was known, Sa‘d ibn Mu‘adh would not have answered the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he would justify him by killing, and he and the rest of the people would have said: "O Messenger of Allah, the ruling for slander is known and your hand is extended [to implement it]."

Third: They explicitly state that the people of the slander were ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy, Mistah, Hassan, and Hamnah, then mention that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) administered two penalties to ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy and one penalty each to Mistah, Hassan, and Hamnah. Then they justify the two penalties for ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy by saying that whoever slanders the wives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is liable for two penalties. This is a manifest contradiction, for they were all accusers with no difference between them.

Yes, the narrations mention that ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy was the one who took upon himself the bulk of it from them, but no one from the Ummah has said that this description necessitates two penalties. Nor is the "great punishment" in His saying: الَّذِي تَوَلَّى كِبْرَهُ مِنْهُمْ لَهُ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ "and as for him among them who took upon himself the bulk of it, for him there is a great punishment" the establishment of two penalties.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِنْكُمْ "Indeed those who brought the lie are a group from among you": "The Sunnis (العامة) have narrated that it was revealed regarding ‘A’ishah and what she was accused of in the campaign of Banu al-Mustaliq from Khuza‘ah. As for the Shi‘a (الخاصة), they have narrated that it was revealed regarding Mariyah the Copt and what ‘A’ishah accused her of."

Muhammad ibn Ja‘far narrated to us from Muhammad ibn ‘Isa, from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali ibn Faddal, who said: ‘Abdullah ibn Bukayr narrated to me from Zurarah, who said: "I heard Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) saying: 'When Ibrahim, the son of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), died, he grieved for him with a severe grief. ‘A’ishah said: "What makes you grieve for him? He is nothing but the son of Jurayh." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent ‘Ali (peace be upon him) and commanded him to kill him.'

‘Ali (peace be upon him) went with the sword. Jurayh the Copt was in a garden (حائط). ‘Ali (peace be upon him) knocked on the gate of the garden. Jurayh came to open the gate. When he saw ‘Ali (peace be upon him), he recognized anger in his face, so he turned back and did not open the gate. ‘Ali (peace be upon him) leaped over the wall and descended into the garden and followed him. Jurayh turned to flee. When he feared that he would be overtaken (يرهقه), he climbed a palm tree. ‘Ali (peace be upon him) climbed after him. When he drew near him, Jurayh threw himself from the top of the palm tree, and his private parts were exposed. It turned out that he had neither what men have nor what women have.

‘Ali (peace be upon him) returned to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said to him: 'O Messenger of Allah, when you send me on a matter, should I be like a heated nail in wool, or should I verify?' He said: 'No, rather verify.' He said: 'By the One who sent you with the truth, he has neither what men have nor what women have.' He said: 'Praise be to Allah who has averted evil from us, the People of the House (أهل البيت).'"

In it, in a narration of ‘Ubaydullah ibn Musa from Ahmad ibn Rashid from Marwan ibn Muslim from ‘Abdullah ibn Bukayr, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom! Had the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded the killing of the Copt while he knew that she had lied against him, or did he not know? And Allah averted the killing from the Copt through the verification of ‘Ali (peace be upon him).' He said: 'By Allah, he knew. Had it been a firm decree (عزيمة) from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), ‘Ali (peace be upon him) would not have returned until he had killed him. However, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only did that so she might turn back from her sin. But she did not turn back, nor did the killing of a Muslim man weigh heavily upon her.'"

I say: There are other narrations indicating the participation of others with her in this accusation. This Jurayh was a eunuch servant of Mariyah, whom al-Muqawqis, the Great of Egypt, had gifted along with her to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and sent with her to serve her.

These narrations are not without room for scrutiny:

Firstly: Because the story they contain does not accept application to the verses, especially His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ "Indeed those who brought the lie," and His saying: لَوْ لا إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ ظَنَّ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ خَيْراً "When you heard it, why did not the faithful, men and women, think well of their own people," and His saying: تَلَقَّوْنَهُ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْواهِكُمْ ما لَيْسَ لَكُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ "when you were receiving it with your tongues and were saying with your mouths that of which you had no knowledge." The sum of the verses is that there was a group connected to one another who were disseminating the talk to disgrace the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and people were circulating it from tongue to tongue until it spread among them, and they remained in that state for a time, not observing the sanctity of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his nobility in the sight of Allah. Where is the content of these narrations from that?

Unless the narrations are deficient in their explanation of the story.

Secondly: The requirement of the story and the manifestation of her innocence was the implementation of the legal penalty (الحد), yet it was not implemented. There is no escape from this problem except by saying that the verse of slander was revealed a long time after the story of the slander.

What ought to be said, considering the problem of the legal penalty applicable to both types of narrations—as you have seen—is that the verses of the slander were revealed before the verse of the penalty for slander. Nothing was legislated by the revelation of the verses of the slander except the innocence of the accused in the absence of witnesses and the prohibition of slander.

Had the penalty for the slanderer been legislated before the incident of the slander, there would have been no justification for delaying it for a significant period and waiting for revelation, nor would any slanderer among them have escaped it. Had it been legislated with the revelation of the verses of the slander, it would have been alluded to therein; at the very least, the verses would be connected to the verse of slander. One who is familiar with the styles of speech does not doubt that His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ بِالْإِفْكِ "Indeed those who brought the lie" is disconnected from what precedes it.

Had there been two penalties for one who slanders the wives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), it would have been alluded to during the verses of the slander, with the emphasis, cursing, and threat of punishment they contain for the slanderers.

The problem is intensified on the assumption that the verse of slander was revealed along with the verses of the slander, for its requirement would be that the trial with the ruling of two penalties would occur, yet the ruling of a single penalty was revealed.

In al-Kafi, from ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from some of his companions, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "Whoever says regarding a believer what his eyes have seen and his ears have heard, he is among those of whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ 'Indeed those who want'—until His saying—وَالْآخِرَةِ 'and the Hereafter.'"

I say: It was also narrated by al-Qummi in his Tafsir, from his father, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from Hisham, from him (peace be upon him); and by al-Saduq in al-Amali, with his chain from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from Muhammad ibn Humran, from him (peace be upon him); and by al-Mufid in al-Ikhtisas, from him (peace be upon him) as a disconnected report (مرسلاً).

In it, with his chain from Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Whoever disseminates an indecency is like the one who initiated it.'"

In al-Majma‘, it is said: "His saying: وَلا يَأْتَلِ أُولُوا الْفَضْلِ مِنْكُمْ وَالسَّعَةِ 'The well-off and the opulent among you should not vow,' etc., was revealed regarding Abu Bakr and Mistah ibn Uthathah. He was the son of Abu Bakr’s maternal aunt, and he was among the migrants and the veterans of Badr. He was poor, and Abu Bakr used to provide for him and maintain him. When he plunged into the slander, he cut it off and swore that he would never benefit him with any benefit. When the verse was revealed, Abu Bakr returned to what he was doing and said: 'By Allah, I love that Allah should forgive me; by Allah, I will never withdraw it from him.'" This is from Ibn ‘Abbas, ‘A’ishah, and Ibn Zayd.

In it, it is also said: "It was revealed regarding a group of the companions who swore that they would not give charity to a man who spoke anything of the slander, nor would they assist them." This is from Ibn ‘Abbas and others.

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn ‘Abbas.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَلَا يَأْتَلِ أُولُو الْفَضْلِ مِنكُمْ وَالسَّعَةِ أَن يُؤْتُوا أُولِي الْقُرْبَىٰ "The well-off and the opulent among you should not vow not to give to the relatives" (24:22)—meaning the relatives of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—وَالْمَسَاكِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ ۖ وَلْيَعْفُوا وَلْيَصْفَحُوا "and the needy, and those who have migrated in the way of Allah, and let them excuse and forbear" (24:22). He says: "Let some of you excuse others, and let some of you forbear others; if you do so, the mercy of Allah will be yours." Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: أَلَا تُحِبُّونَ أَن يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۗ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ "Do you not love that Allah should forgive you? And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (24:22).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Salim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "And there was revealed in Medina: وَالَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَنَاتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَأْتُوا بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَاءَ فَاجْلِدُوهُمْ ثَمَانِينَ جَلْدَةً وَلَا تَقْبَلُوا لَهُمْ شَهَادَةً أَبَدًا ۚ وَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْفَاسِقُونَ * إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ 'As for those who accuse honorable women and do not bring four witnesses, flog them eighty lashes, and never accept any testimony from them, and it is they who are the transgressors (الفاسقون) — those who have strayed from the path of obedience and legal uprightness — except those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful' (24:4-5)."

"Thus, Allah stripped him—so long as he persisted in the slander (الفرية)—of being named with faith (الإيمان). Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: أَفَمَن كَانَ مُؤْمِنًا كَمَن كَانَ فَاسِقًا ۚ لَّا يَسْتَوُونَ 'Is he who is a believer like him who is a transgressor? They are not equal' (32:18). And He placed him among the allies of Iblis, saying: إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ كَانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ '...except Iblis. He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed against his Lord’s command' (18:50). And He made him accursed, saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَنَاتِ الْغَافِلَاتِ الْمُؤْمِنَاتِ لُعِنُوا فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ * يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ 'Indeed those who accuse honorable and unwary faithful women shall be cursed in this world and the Hereafter, and there shall be a great punishment for them on the day when their tongues, their hands and their feet shall bear witness against them concerning what they used to do' (24:23-24)."

"The limbs do not bear witness against a believer (مؤمن); they only bear witness against one for whom the word of punishment has become due. As for the believer, he is given his book in his right hand. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتَابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ يَقْرَؤُونَ كِتَابَهُمْ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا 'As for those who are given their book in their right hand—they will read their book, and they will not be wronged so much as a thread' (17:71)."

In al-Majma‘, regarding the saying of the Exalted: الْخَبِيثَاتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَالْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثَاتِ ۖ وَالطَّيِّبَاتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَالطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّبَاتِ "Vicious women are for vicious men, and vicious men for vicious women. Good women are for good men, and good men for good women" (24:26), several opinions were mentioned regarding its meaning—until he said—the third: "Vicious women (الخبيثات) are for vicious men (الخبيثين), and vicious men are for vicious women; this is narrated from Abu Muslim and al-Jubba’i, and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them). They both said: 'It is like His saying: الزَّانِي لَا يَنكِحُ إِلَّا زَانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً "The fornicator shall not marry anyone but a fornicatress or an idolatress" (24:3), except that some people intended to marry them, so Allah forbade them from that and disliked it for them.'"

In al-Khisal, from ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar and Abu Hurayrah, they said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'When the heart of a person is good (طاب), his body becomes good; and when the heart is vicious (خبث), the body becomes vicious.'"

In al-Ihtijaj, from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him), in a tradition of his with Mu‘awiyah and his companions—after they had reviled ‘Ali (peace be upon him): "الْخَبِيثَاتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَالْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثَاتِ 'Vicious women are for vicious men, and vicious men for vicious women' (24:26)—by Allah, O Mu‘awiyah, they are you, these companions of yours, and your followers (شيعتك). وَالطَّيِّبَاتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَالطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّبَاتِ 'Good women are for good men, and good men for good women' (24:26) to the end of the verse—they are ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, his companions, and his followers (شيعته)."

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تَسْتَأْنِسُوا۟ وَتُسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٧)
O you who have faith! Do not enter houses other than your own until you have announced [your arrival] and greeted their occupants. That is better for you. Maybe you will take admonition. (27)
فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فِيهَآ أَحَدًا فَلَا تَدْخُلُوهَا حَتَّىٰ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِن قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ ۖ هُوَ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (٢٨)
But if you do not find anyone in them, do not enter them until you are given permission. And if you are told: ‘Turn back,’ then do turn back. That is more decent for you. Allah knows best what you do. (28)
لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيهَا مَتَـٰعٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ (٢٩)
There will be no sin upon you in entering [without announcing] uninhabited houses wherein you have goods belonging to you. Allah knows whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal. (29)
قُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا۟ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا۟ فُرُوجَهُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَزْكَىٰ لَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ (٣٠)
Tell the faithful men to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts. That is more decent for them. Allah is indeed well aware of what they do. (30)
وَقُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَّ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلَىٰ جُيُوبِهِنَّ ۖ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا لِبُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ ءَابَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ ءَابَآءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ أَبْنَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ أَبْنَآءِ بُعُولَتِهِنَّ أَوْ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ أَوْ بَنِىٓ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ أَوْ بَنِىٓ أَخَوَٰتِهِنَّ أَوْ نِسَآئِهِنَّ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُنَّ أَوِ ٱلتَّـٰبِعِينَ غَيْرِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْإِرْبَةِ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ أَوِ ٱلطِّفْلِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰ عَوْرَٰتِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۖ وَلَا يَضْرِبْنَ بِأَرْجُلِهِنَّ لِيُعْلَمَ مَا يُخْفِينَ مِن زِينَتِهِنَّ ۚ وَتُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا أَيُّهَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٣١)
And tell the faithful women to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts, and not to display their charms, beyond what is [acceptably] visible, and let them draw their scarfs over their bosoms, and not display their charms except to their husbands, or their fathers, or their husband’s fathers, or their sons, or their husband’s sons, or their brothers, or their brothers’ sons, or their sisters’ sons, or their women, or their slave girls, or male dependants lacking [sexual] desire, or children who are not yet conscious of female sexuality. And let them not thump their feet to make known their hidden ornaments. Rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful, so that you may be felicitous. (31)
وَأَنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْأَيَـٰمَىٰ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ مِنْ عِبَادِكُمْ وَإِمَآئِكُمْ ۚ إِن يَكُونُوا۟ فُقَرَآءَ يُغْنِهِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٢)
Marry off those who are single among you, and the upright among your male and female slaves. If they are poor, Allah will enrich them out of His grace, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (32)
وَلْيَسْتَعْفِفِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ نِكَاحًا حَتَّىٰ يُغْنِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبْتَغُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِمَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ فَكَاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْرًا ۖ وَءَاتُوهُم مِّن مَّالِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تُكْرِهُوا۟ فَتَيَـٰتِكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْبِغَآءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّنًا لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَمَن يُكْرِههُّنَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِكْرَٰهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٣)
Those who cannot afford marriage should be continent until Allah enriches them out of His grace. As for those who seek an emancipation deal from among your slaves, make such a deal with them if you know any good in them, and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you. Do not compel your female slaves to prostitution when they desire to be chaste, seeking the transitory wares of the life of this world. Should anyone compel them, Allah will be forgiving and merciful to them following their compulsion. (33)
وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ وَمَثَلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٤)
Certainly We have sent down to you manifest signs and a description of those who passed before you, and an advice for the Godwary. (34)

(Commentary)

These are rulings and laws (أحكام وشرائع) that are proportionate and appropriate to what has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَهْلِها — O you who have faith! Do not enter houses other than your own until you have announced your presence and greeted their occupants. (24:27) etc. Intimacy (الأنس) with a thing or toward it refers to familiarity and the heart’s tranquility toward it. Seeking intimacy (الاستيناس) is to seek that through an action that leads to it, such as seeking permission to enter a house by mentioning Allah, clearing one's throat, and the like, so that the owner of the house becomes aware that there is someone who wishes to enter upon him. Thus, he may prepare for that, as he might be in a state in which he does not wish anyone to see him or for an observer to look upon him.

From this, it becomes clear that the benefit (مصلحة) of this ruling is to veil the private affairs (عورات) of people and to safeguard the dignity of faith (كرامة الإيمان). When the one entering seeks intimacy upon wanting to enter a house other than his own, and informs the owner of the house of his seeking intimacy regarding his entry, and then enters and greets him, he has assisted him in veiling his private state and has granted him security from himself.

Persistence in this beautiful conduct leads to the strengthening of brotherhood, affection, and general cooperation in manifesting what is beautiful and veiling what is unseemly. To this, He refers by His saying: ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ — That is better for you, so that you may take admonition. (24:27). That is, perhaps by persisting in this conduct, you will remember what you must observe and revive of the tradition of brotherhood and the harmony of hearts, beneath which lies every social felicity.

It has been said that His saying: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (so that you may take admonition) is a justification for an omitted part, the estimation being: "You were told such-and-such so that you might remember the exhortations of Allah and act according to their requirements," and there is no harm in this interpretation.

It has also been said that in His saying: حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا (until you have announced your presence and greeted), there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير), and the original order is "until you greet and announce your presence." This is as you see [unlikely].

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فِيها أَحَداً فَلا تَدْخُلُوها حَتَّى يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ — And if you do not find anyone in them, do not enter them until permission is given to you. (24:28) ... etc. That is, if you know that there is no one in them—meaning the one who possesses the right to grant permission—then do not enter them until permission is given to you by the one who possesses that right. The meaning is not that one should peek into the house and look inside, and if he sees no one, he refrains from entering; for the context (السياق) testifies that the prohibition is actually against looking and peering into the private affairs of people.

This verse clarifies the ruling on entering the house of another when there is no one inside who possesses the right to grant permission. The previous verse clarifies the ruling on entering when there is someone who possesses the right to grant permission and does not forbid it. As for entering when there is someone who possesses the right to grant permission but forbids it and does not allow entry, its ruling is clarified by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ قِيلَ لَكُمُ ارْجِعُوا فَارْجِعُوا هُوَ أَزْكى لَكُمْ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ — And if it is said to you, ‘Turn back,’ then do turn back: that is more decent for you. And Allah knows best what you do. (24:28).

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيها مَتاعٌ لَكُمْ — There is no sin upon you in entering uninhabited houses wherein there is some utility for you. (24:29) etc. The apparent context is that His saying: فِيها مَتاعٌ لَكُمْ (wherein there is some utility for you) is an adjective after an adjective for His saying: بُيُوتاً (houses), not a new sentence justifying His saying: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ (There is no sin upon you). It appears that utility (المتاع) here carries the meaning of benefit or enjoyment (الاستمتاع).

Thus, it contains the permissibility of entering houses prepared for various types of utility which are naturally uninhabited, such as inns (الخانات), public baths (الحمامات), mills (الأرحية), and the like; for their being designated for utility constitutes a general permission for entering them.

It has sometimes been said that what is meant by utility (المتاع) is the nominal meaning, which is furniture or goods placed for buying and selling, as in commercial houses and shops; for they are permitted for entry by a general permission. This is not without some distance [from the truth] due to the inadequacy of the wording to support it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ ذلِكَ أَزْكى لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما يَصْنَعُونَ — Tell the faithful men to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts. That is more decent for them. Allah is indeed well aware of what they do. (24:30). Lowering (الغض) is to bring the eyelid over the eyelid. Looks (الأبصار) is the plural of sight (بصر), which is the organ of vision. From here, it appears that the preposition "from" (مِنْ) in "from their looks" (مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ) is for the beginning of an end (ابتداء الغاية), not redundant, nor for genus, nor for partitivity (التبعيض), as various commentators have suggested. The meaning is that they should perform the act of lowering, originating from their looks.

As for His saying: قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ, since "they should lower" (يَغُضُّوا) is consequent upon His saying: "Tell" (قُلْ) in the manner of a response to a condition (جواب الشرط), this indicates that the "saying" (القول) carries the meaning of a command (الأمر). The meaning is: "Command them to lower their looks," and the estimation is: "Command them to lower [their looks], for if you command them to do so, they will lower [them]." The verse is a command to lower the gaze, or if you wish, say: a prohibition against looking at what is not permissible to look at, whether a male or female stranger, due to the absolute nature (الإطلاق) of the expression.

And His saying: وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ (and to guard their private parts) means: "And command them to guard their private parts." The term furjah or farj (الفرج) refers to the gap or opening between two things, and it is used as a metonym (كنى به) for the private parts (السوأة). Upon this, the usage of the Quran—which is full of refinement and etiquette—has proceeded. Then its usage for this meaning became so frequent that it became like an explicit term (النص), as mentioned by al-Raghib.

The juxtaposition between His saying: يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ (lower their looks) and يَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ (guard their private parts) suggests that what is meant by guarding the private parts is veiling them from being seen, not guarding them from adultery or sodomy as has been said. It has been narrated in a report from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Every verse in the Quran regarding the guarding of private parts refers to adultery—except for this verse, for it refers to looking."

Based on this, it is possible for the first of the two phrases to be restricted by the second, and the meaning of the verse would be the prohibition of looking at the private parts and the command to veil them.

Then He pointed to the aspect of benefit (المصلحة) in the ruling and urged them to be mindful regarding it by His saying: ذلِكَ أَزْكى لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما يَصْنَعُونَ — That is more decent for them. Allah is indeed well aware of what they do. (24:30).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِناتِ يَغْضُضْنَ — And tell the faithful women to cast down their looks... (24:31) etc. The discussion regarding His saying: وَقُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِناتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصارِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَ is similar to what has passed regarding His saying: قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ. Thus, it is not permissible for them to look at what is not permissible to look at, and it is obligatory for them to veil their private parts from both male and female strangers.

As for His saying: وَلا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها — and not to display their charms, except for what is outward (24:31). Displaying (الإبداء) is to make manifest. What is meant by their charms (زينتهن) is the places of adornment, because the adornment itself, such as earrings or bracelets, is not prohibited from being displayed. Thus, what is meant by displaying the charms is displaying the places of those charms on the body.

Allah, the Glorified, has excepted from this "what is outward" (ما ظهر). Narrations have stated that what is meant by "what is outward" are the face, the hands, and the feet, as will come [later], if Allah wills.

And His saying: وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلى جُيُوبِهِنَ — and let them draw their scarfs over their bosoms (24:31). Khumur (الخُمُر) is the plural of khimar (خمار), which is what a woman covers her head with and which drapes over her chest. Juyub (الجيوب) is the plural of jayb (جيب), which is well-known, and what is meant by juyub here are the bosoms (الصدور). The meaning is: let them cast the ends of their head-coverings over their bosoms to veil them.

And His saying: وَلا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا لِبُعُولَتِهِنَ — and not to display their charms except to their husbands — until His saying — أَوْ بَنِي أَخَواتِهِنَ — or their sisters’ sons (24:31). The bu'ulah (البعولة) are their husbands. The other seven groups are their unmarriageable kin (محارمهن) through lineage or cause (النسب والسبب). The grandfathers of the husbands fall under the ruling of their fathers, and the grandsons of the husbands fall under the ruling of their sons.

And His saying: أَوْ نِسائِهِنَ — or their women (24:31). The possessive construction (الإضافة) indicates that what is meant by them are the believing women. Thus, it is not permissible for them to disrobe before other [non-believing] women, as has been mentioned in narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

And His saying: أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ — or their slave-girls (24:31). Its absolute nature includes both male and female slaves, and narrations have been reported to this effect, as will come, if Allah wills. This is one of the instances where "what" (ما) is used for rational beings (أولي العقل).

And His saying: أَوِ التَّابِعِينَ غَيْرِ أُولِي الْإِرْبَةِ مِنَ الرِّجالِ — or male attendants who have no desire (24:31). Irba (الإربة) is need, and what is meant here is the sexual desire (الشهوة) that necessitates marriage. "From the men" (مِنَ الرِّجالِ) is an explanation for the "attendants" (التابعين). What is meant by them, as explained by the narrations, are the simple-minded men (البله) who are under the guardianship of others and possess no sexual desire.

And His saying: أَوِ الطِّفْلِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلى عَوْراتِ النِّساءِ — or children who are not yet aware of women’s private parts (24:31). That is, the group of children—the definite article "the" (اللام) being for exhaustiveness (الاستغراق)—who have not gained strength or "prevailed" (يظهروا)—from zuhur (الظهور) meaning dominance—over matters concerning women that are unseemly to mention explicitly. This is—as has been said—a metonym for puberty (البلوغ).

And His saying: وَلا يَضْرِبْنَ بِأَرْجُلِهِنَّ لِيُعْلَمَ ما يُخْفِينَ مِنْ زِينَتِهِنَ — and let them not thump their feet to make known their hidden ornaments (24:31). This occurs through the sound made by items of adornment such as anklets, necklaces, earrings, and bracelets.

And His saying: وَتُوبُوا إِلَى اللهِ جَمِيعاً أَيُّهَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ — and rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful, so that you may be felicitous (24:31). What is meant by repentance (التوبة)—according to what the context provides—is returning to Him, the Exalted, by complying with His commands and refraining from His prohibitions; in short, following His path.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْكِحُوا الْأَيامى مِنْكُمْ وَالصَّالِحِينَ مِنْ عِبادِكُمْ وَإِمائِكُمْ — Marry off those who are single among you, and the upright among your male slaves and your female slaves. (24:32). Inkah (الإنكاح) is to marry off. Ayama (الأيامى) is the plural of ayyim (أيم), which refers to a male who has no female with him, or a female who has no male with her. Sometimes a woman is called ayyimah. What is meant by "the upright" (الصالحين) are those who are fit for marriage, not those who are righteous in their deeds.

And His saying: إِنْ يَكُونُوا فُقَراءَ يُغْنِهِمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — If they are poor, Allah will enrich them out of His grace (24:32). This is a beautiful promise of enrichment and abundance of provision (سعة الرزق), which He emphasized by His saying: وَاللهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ — and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (24:32). Provision (الرزق) follows the suitability of the one provided for, by the will of Allah, the Glorified. There will come to you, if Allah wills, in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِثْلَ ما أَنَّكُمْ تَنْطِقُونَ — By the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is indeed as real as your speaking [to one another]! (51:23), a discussion on the meaning of the abundance of provision.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلْيَسْتَعْفِفِ الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ نِكاحاً حَتَّى يُغْنِيَهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — Those who cannot afford marriage should be continent until Allah enriches them out of His grace. (24:33). Isti'faf (الاستعفاف) and ta'affuf (التعفف) are close in meaning. What is meant by "not finding marriage" (عدم وجدان النكاح) is the lack of ability to provide the dower (المهر) and maintenance (النفقة). The meaning of the verse is a command to be continent for the one who is unable to marry, and to guard against falling into adultery until Allah enriches him from His grace.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَبْتَغُونَ الْكِتابَ مِمَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ فَكاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً — As for those of your slaves who seek a contract [of manumission], make the contract with them if you know any good in them (24:33) etc. What is meant by "the contract" (الكتاب) is the mukatabah (المكاتبة) — the written agreement of manumission. Seeking the contract is for the slave to ask his master to enter into a mukatabah agreement with him, whereby he pays the master a sum of money in exchange for his freedom. In the verse is a command to the masters to respond to them if they know "good" (خيرا) in them, which is a metonym for ascertaining their suitability for that.

And His saying: وَآتُوهُمْ مِنْ مالِ اللهِ الَّذِي آتاكُمْ — and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you (24:33). This is an indication to give them the wealth for the mukatabah from the obligatory zakat (الزكاة المفروضة), as a portion of the zakat is designated for them, as He, the Exalted, said: وَفي الرِّقابِ — and for [the freeing of] slaves (9:60), or it refers to waiving a portion of the mukatabah payment.

In this verse and the preceding verses are numerous jurisprudential (فقهية) discussions that should be referred to in the books of jurisprudence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُكْرِهُوا فَتَياتِكُمْ عَلَى الْبِغاءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً — Do not compel your slave-girls to prostitution if they intend to be chaste (24:33). Fatayat (الفتيات) are female slaves and maidservants. Bigha' (البغاء) is adultery (الزنا), and it is on the form of mufa'alah from baghy. Tahassun (التحصن) is continence and marriage. "Seeking the transient goods of the life of this world" (وابتغاء عرض الحياة الدنيا) is the seeking of money. The meaning is clear.

The prohibition of compulsion was conditioned upon the "intention to be chaste" (إرادة التحصن) because compulsion (الإكراه) cannot be realized in one who does not desire chastity. Then He promised them forgiveness in the event of compulsion by His saying: وَمَنْ يُكْرِهْهُنَّ فَإِنَّ اللهَ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِكْراهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — and should anyone compel them, then after their compulsion Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (24:33). Its meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ وَمَثَلاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ — Certainly We have sent down to you manifest signs and an example of those who passed away before you and an advice for the God-conscious. (24:34). Mathal (المثل) is the description (الصفة). It is possible that His saying: وَلَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا etc., is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of His saying: تُوبُوا (repent) in the previous verse, or it is a new sentence (استيناف). The meaning is: "I swear, We have sent down to you verses that clarify for you the knowledge of religion (معارف الدين) through which you will be felicitous, and a description of those who preceded—their righteous and their wicked—by which it becomes distinct to you what you should adopt and what you should avoid, and an advice for the God-conscious (المتقين) among you."

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration to Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Abdillah, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ ـ حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَهْلِها — "He said: Seeking intimacy (الاستيناس) is the sound of the sandal and the greeting (التسليم)."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Saduq in Ma'ani al-Akhbar from Muhammad ibn al-Hasan in a marfu' report from Abd al-Rahman from him (peace be upon him).

In al-Majma', from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, who said: "We said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is seeking intimacy (الاستيناس)?' He said: 'A man speaks by glorifying Allah (التسبيحة), praising Him (التحميدة), and magnifying Him (التكبيرة)—and he clears his throat for the occupants of the house.'"

From Sahl ibn Sa'd, who said: "A man peeked into one of the rooms of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had a comb (مدرى) with which he was scratching his head. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'If I had known you were looking, I would have poked your eyes with this—seeking permission (الاستيذان) is only because of looking.'"

It is narrated that a man said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Should I seek permission to enter upon my mother?" He said: "Yes." The man said: "She has no servant other than me—should I seek permission every time I enter?" He said: "Would you like to see her naked?" The man said: "No." He said: "Then seek permission to enter upon her."

It is narrated that a man sought permission to enter upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and cleared his throat. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to a woman called Rawdah: "Go to this man and teach him, and tell him: 'Say: Peace be upon you, may I enter?'" The man heard her and said it, so the Prophet said: "Enter."

I say: It is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from a group of compilers of traditions: the first narration from Abu Ayyub, the second from Sahl ibn Sa'd, and the fourth from Amr ibn Sa'd al-Thaqafi.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ubadah ibn al-Samit: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was asked about seeking permission in houses, and he said: 'Whoever's eye enters before he seeks permission and greets—he has indeed disobeyed Allah and there is no permission for him.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فِيها أَحَداً ـ فَلا تَدْخُلُوها حَتَّى يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ, he said: "Its meaning is: if you do not find anyone in it to give you permission—do not enter it until you are given permission."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا ـ بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيها مَتاعٌ لَكُمْ, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "They are the public baths, the inns, and the mills; you enter them without permission."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Abu Amr al-Zubayri, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), in a hadith mentioning what Allah has made obligatory upon the limbs, he said: "وفرض على البصر أن لا ينظر إلى ما حرم الله عليه ، وأن يعرض عما نهى الله عنه مما لا يحل له ـ وهو عمله وهو من الإيمان. فقال تبارك وتعالى : «قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ» فنهاهم أن ينظروا إلى عوراتهم ـ وأن ينظر المرء إلى فرج أخيه ـ ويحفظ فرجه أن ينظر إليه ، وقال : «وَقُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِناتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصارِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَ» من أن تنظر إحداهن إلى فرج أختها ـ وتحفظ فرجها من أن ينظر إليه. وقال : كل شيء في القرآن من حفظ الفرج فهو من الزنا ـ إلا هذه الآية فهو من النظر." — "And He made it obligatory upon the sight that it should not look at what Allah has forbidden to it, and that it should turn away from what Allah has prohibited of that which is not permissible for it—and this is its action and it is part of faith. Thus, He, the Blessed and Exalted, said: 'Tell the faithful men to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts.' So He forbade them from looking at their private parts—and that a man should look at the private part of his brother—and that he should guard his private part from being looked at. And He said: 'And tell the faithful women to cast down their looks and to guard their private parts'—from one of them looking at the private part of her sister—and that she should guard her private part from being looked at. And He said: 'Every instance in the Quran regarding the guarding of the private part refers to adultery—except for this verse, for it refers to looking.'"

I say: Al-Qummi narrated in his Tafsir, following the hadith, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Abu Basir, from him (peace be upon him). Similar reports were narrated from Abu al-Aliyah and Ibn Zayd.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Sa'd al-Iskaf, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "A young man from the Ansar met a woman in Medina—and women used to wear their head-coverings behind their ears—so he looked at her as she approached. When she passed, he looked at her and entered an alleyway which he named as belonging to the sons of so-and-so, and he began looking behind her. A bone in the wall or a piece of glass struck his face and cut it. When the woman had passed, he looked and saw blood flowing onto his garment and chest. He said: 'By Allah, I shall surely go to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and inform him.' He said: So he came to him, and when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw him, he said: 'What is this?' So he informed him, and Gabriel descended with this verse: قُلْ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا مِنْ أَبْصارِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا فُرُوجَهُمْ ـ ذلِكَ أَزْكى لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما يَصْنَعُونَ."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Ali ibn Abi Talib in a similar fashion. The apparent meaning of the hadith is that what is meant by the command to lower the gaze in the verse is the prohibition of looking at a female stranger in general, just as the apparent meaning of some previous narrations is that it is a prohibition against looking at the private parts of another specifically.

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Marwak ibn Ubayd, from some of our companions, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'What is permissible to be seen of a woman when one is not a mahram?' He said: 'The face, the two hands, and the two feet.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Khisal from some of our companions from him (peace be upon him), and its wording is: "The face, the two hands, and the two feet."

In Qurb al-Isnad by al-Himyari, from Ali ibn Ja'far, from his brother Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about a man: what is proper for him to look at of a woman who is not permissible for him? He said: 'The face, the hand, and the place of the bracelet.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Abbad ibn Suhayb, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: 'لا بأس بالنظر إلى رءوس أهل تهامة والأعراب ـ وأهل السواد والعلوج ـ لأنهم إذا نهوا لا ينتهون. قال : والمجنونة والمغلوبة على عقلها ، ولا بأس بالنظر إلى شعرها وجسدها ما لم يتعمد ذلك.'" — "There is no harm in looking at the heads of the people of Tihamah, the Bedouins, the people of the Sawad, and the non-Arabs (العلوج)—because when they are forbidden, they do not desist. He said: And the insane woman and the one whose intellect is overcome; there is no harm in looking at her hair and her body as long as it is not done intentionally."

I say: It is as if he (peace be upon him) means by his saying "as long as it is not done intentionally" the presence of suspicious desire (الريبة).

In al-Khisal, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "يا علي أول نظرة لك والثانية عليك لا لك" — "O Ali, the first look is for you, and the second is against you, not for you."

I say: A similar report was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several compilers of traditions from Buraydah from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and its wording is: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to Ali: 'Do not follow a look with another look—for the first is for you, but the latter is not for you.'"

In Jawami' al-Jami', from Umm Salamah, who said: "I was with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Maymunah was with him, when Ibn Umm Maktum approached—and that was after we had been commanded to wear the hijab. He said: 'Observe the hijab.' We said: 'O Messenger of Allah, is he not blind and cannot see us?' He said: 'Are you two blind? Do you not see him?'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and al-Bayhaqi from her.

In al-Faqih, Hafs ibn al-Bakhtari narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "It is not proper for a woman to uncover herself before a Jewish or Christian woman, for they describe that to their husbands."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ, it was said: "Its meaning is male and female slaves," and that was narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Abdillah, who said: "I asked him about 'male attendants who have no desire.' He said: 'The simpleton who is under guardianship and does not approach women.'"

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Ja'far, from his father, from his ancestors (peace be upon them), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'من ترك التزويج مخافة العيلة ـ فقد أساء ظنه بالله عز وجل إن الله يقول «إِنْ يَكُونُوا فُقَراءَ يُغْنِهِمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ»'" — "Whoever abandons marriage for fear of poverty has indeed thought ill of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic; for Allah says: 'If they are poor, Allah will enrich them out of His grace.'"

I say: Regarding the previous meanings, there are very many narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them); whoever desires them should refer to the books of hadith.

In al-Faqih, al-Ala' narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَكاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً — "He said: The 'good' (الخير) is that he bears witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, and that he has a work in his hand by which he can earn, or that he has a craft."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to al-Ala' ibn Fudayl from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَكاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً ـ وَآتُوهُمْ مِنْ مالِ اللهِ الَّذِي آتاكُمْ — "He said: You should waive for him some of his installments (نجومه) that you did not intend to reduce, and do not exceed what is in your soul. I said: 'How much?' He said: 'Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) waived one thousand out of six thousand for a slave.'"

I say: It was narrated in Majma' al-Bayan, and likewise in al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ali (peace be upon him): "one-fourth of the wealth." What is understood from the apparent meanings of the reports is that no specific proportional amount is fixed.

The narration of al-Ayyashi has already preceded under the saying: وَفي الرِّقابِ — and for [the freeing of] slaves (9:60) in the ninth volume of this book, stating that the mukatab is given from the portion of the zakat designated for slaves.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُكْرِهُوا فَتَياتِكُمْ عَلَى الْبِغاءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً, he said: "The Arabs and Quraysh used to buy slave-girls and impose a heavy tax upon them, saying: 'Go and commit adultery and earn.' So Allah forbade them from that and said: 'Do not compel your slave-girls to prostitution'—until His saying—'all-forgiving, all-merciful,' meaning that Allah, the Exalted, will not take them to task for that if they were compelled to it."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِتَبْتَغُوا عَرَضَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا, it was said: "Abdullah ibn Ubayy had six slave-girls whom he used to compel to earn through adultery. When the prohibition of adultery was revealed, they came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and complained to him, so the verse was revealed."

I say: As for his having slave-girls whom he compelled to adultery, narrations have been reported to this effect in al-Durr al-Manthur, just as it narrated this report. As for this being after the revelation of the prohibition of adultery, it is weakened by the fact that adultery was not prohibited in Medina, but rather in Mecca before the Hijrah; indeed, its prohibition was among the essentials of Islam since the true call appeared. It has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-An'am that the prohibition of indecencies (الفواحش), including adultery, is among the general rulings that are not specific to one law (شريعة) over another.

Ayahs

۞ ٱللَّهُ نُورُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ مَثَلُ نُورِهِۦ كَمِشْكَوٰةٍ فِيهَا مِصْبَاحٌ ۖ ٱلْمِصْبَاحُ فِى زُجَاجَةٍ ۖ ٱلزُّجَاجَةُ كَأَنَّهَا كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّىٌّ يُوقَدُ مِن شَجَرَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ لَّا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلَا غَرْبِيَّةٍ يَكَادُ زَيْتُهَا يُضِىٓءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نَارٌ ۚ نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ ۗ يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ لِنُورِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ لِلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٣٥)
Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth. The parable of His Light is a niche wherein is a lamp—the lamp is in a glass, the glass as it were a glittering star—lit from a blessed olive tree, neither eastern nor western, whose oil almost lights up, though fire should not touch it. Light upon light. Allah guides to His Light whomever He wishes. Allah draws parables for mankind, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (35)
فِى بُيُوتٍ أَذِنَ ٱللَّهُ أَن تُرْفَعَ وَيُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ فِيهَا بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ (٣٦)
In houses Allah has allowed to be raised and wherein His Name is celebrated; He is glorified therein, morning and evening, (36)
رِجَالٌ لَّا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجَـٰرَةٌ وَلَا بَيْعٌ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَإِقَامِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَإِيتَآءِ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ ۙ يَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (٣٧)
by men whom neither trade nor bargaining distracts from the remembrance of Allah and the maintenance of prayer and the giving of zakat. They are fearful of a day wherein the hearts and the sights will be transformed, (37)
لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَزِيدَهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٣٨)
so that Allah may reward them by the best of what they have done, and enhance them out of His grace, and Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning. (38)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ كَسَرَابٍۭ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ ٱلظَّمْـَٔانُ مَآءً حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَهُۥ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْـًٔا وَوَجَدَ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥ فَوَفَّىٰهُ حِسَابَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٣٩)
As for the faithless, their works are like a mirage in a plain, which the thirsty man supposes to be water. When he comes to it, he finds it to be nothing; but there he finds Allah, who will pay him his full account, and Allah is swift at reckoning. (39)
أَوْ كَظُلُمَـٰتٍ فِى بَحْرٍ لُّجِّىٍّ يَغْشَىٰهُ مَوْجٌ مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ مَوْجٌ مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ سَحَابٌ ۚ ظُلُمَـٰتٌۢ بَعْضُهَا فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ إِذَآ أَخْرَجَ يَدَهُۥ لَمْ يَكَدْ يَرَىٰهَا ۗ وَمَن لَّمْ يَجْعَلِ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ نُورًا فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن نُّورٍ (٤٠)
Or like the manifold darkness in a deep sea, covered by billow upon billow, overcast by clouds; manifold [layers of] darkness, one on top of another: when he brings out his hand, he can hardly see it. One whom Allah has not granted any light has no light. (40)
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلطَّيْرُ صَـٰٓفَّـٰتٍ ۖ كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلَاتَهُۥ وَتَسْبِيحَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٤١)
Have you not regarded that Allah is glorified by everyone in the heavens and the earth, and the birds spreading their wings. Each knows his prayer and glorification, and Allah knows best what they do. (41)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤٢)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and toward Allah is the destination. (42)
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزْجِى سَحَابًا ثُمَّ يُؤَلِّفُ بَيْنَهُۥ ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُۥ رُكَامًا فَتَرَى ٱلْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَـٰلِهِۦ وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن جِبَالٍ فِيهَا مِنۢ بَرَدٍ فَيُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَصْرِفُهُۥ عَن مَّن يَشَآءُ ۖ يَكَادُ سَنَا بَرْقِهِۦ يَذْهَبُ بِٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (٤٣)
Have you not regarded that Allah drives the clouds, then He composes them, then He piles them up, whereat you see the rain issuing from its midst? And He sends down hail from the sky, out of the mountains that are in it, and He strikes with it whomever He wishes, and turns it away from whomever He wishes. The brilliance of its lightening almost takes away the sight. (43)
يُقَلِّبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (٤٤)
Allah alternates the night and the day. There is indeed a lesson in that for those who have insight. (44)
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَ كُلَّ دَآبَّةٍ مِّن مَّآءٍ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰ بَطْنِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰ رِجْلَيْنِ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰٓ أَرْبَعٍ ۚ يَخْلُقُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤٥)
Allah created every animal from water. Among them are some that creep upon their bellies, and among them are some that walk on two feet, and among them are some that walk on four. Allah creates whatever He wishes. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (45)
لَّقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٤٦)
Certainly We have sent down illuminating signs, and Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path. (46)

(Commentary)

The verses contain a comparison between the believers—those characterized by the reality of faith—and the disbelievers. They distinguish the believers by the fact that they are guided through their righteous deeds (الصالحات) toward a light from their Lord. This light grants them the knowledge of Allah (معرفة الله), the Glorified, and leads them toward the best reward and grace from Allah, the Exalted, on the Day when the veil is lifted from their hearts and eyes. As for the disbelievers, their deeds lead them only toward a mirage (سراب) that has no reality; they are in layers of darkness, one above another, and since Allah has not ordained any light for them, they have no light.

The Glorified has explained this reality by stating that He, the Exalted, possesses a general light (نور عام) by which the heavens and the earth are illuminated; thus, they appear in existence after they were not manifest therein. It is evident that the manifestation of one thing by another requires that the manifestor be manifest by its own essence. That which is manifest by its essence and makes others manifest is light (النور). Thus, He, the Exalted, is a Light who makes the heavens and the earth manifest by His radiance upon them, just as physical lights make dense bodies manifest to the senses by shining upon them—except that the manifestation of things by the Divine Light is identical to their existence (وجودها), whereas the manifestation of dense bodies by physical lights is distinct from the origin of their existence.

He also possesses a specific light (نور خاص) by which the believers are illuminated and to which they are guided by their righteous deeds. This is the light of knowledge (نور المعرفة) which will illuminate their hearts and eyes on the Day when hearts and eyes will be overturned. They will be guided by it to their eternal felicity, witnessing with their own eyes what was hidden from them in the Unseen (الغيب) during the life of this world. The Exalted has likened this light to a lamp (مصباح) within a glass (زجاجة) inside a niche (مشكاة), fueled by oil of ultimate purity, such that the glass glistens as if it were a brilliant star, increasing in light upon light. This lamp is placed in houses of worship wherein men from among the believers glorify Allah—men whom neither trade nor sale can distract from the remembrance of their Lord and His worship.

This is the description of the light of His knowledge with which Allah has honored the believers, followed by eternal felicity, while He has denied it to the disbelievers, leaving them in darkness, unable to see. Thus, He has singled out those who occupy themselves with their Lord and turn away from the fleeting goods of the worldly life with a light from Himself. Allah does whatever He wills; to Him belongs the kingdom and to Him is the ultimate resort. He judges as He intends, sending down the rain and the hail from a single cloud, alternating the night and the day, and creating from among the animals those that crawl on their bellies, those that walk on two legs, and those that walk on four—and He has created all from water.

The verses are not lacking in connection to what preceded them, as the exposition of rulings and laws in the previous section concluded with His saying: وَلَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ وَمَثَلاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ — "Certainly We have sent down to you clarifying signs, and an example of those who passed away before you, and an advice for the God-conscious (المتقين)." Clarification (البيان) is the manifestation of the realities of knowledge; thus, it is a Divine illumination.

Furthermore, these verses are part of the Quran, and the Glorified has named the Quran "light" (نور) in several places of His speech, such as His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ نُوراً مُبِيناً — "and We have sent down to you a manifest light" (4:174).

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ to the end of the verse. The niche (المشكاة), according to what al-Raghib and others mentioned, is a non-penetrating wall-recess (كوة); it is what is made in the wall of a house to place items such as a lamp or other things upon it, and it is not a lantern.

The brilliant (الدري): refers to a great star with abundant light, counted among the heavenly bodies. Kindling (الإيقاد) means lighting or igniting. Oil (الزيت) is the fat extracted from olives.

Regarding His saying: اللهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth." Light (النور) is well-known; it is that by which dense bodies become manifest to our vision. Thus, things are manifest through it, while it is manifest and disclosed to us by its own essence. It is that which is manifest by its essence (الظاهر بذاته) and makes other sensible things manifest (المظهر لغيره) to the eye. This is the primary meaning for which the word "light" was coined. Subsequently, it was generalized to everything by which any sensible thing is disclosed, either by way of metaphor or as a secondary literal meaning. Thus, each of the senses was considered a light or possessing a light by which its objects of perception are manifested, such as hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Then it was generalized to the non-sensible; thus, the intellect (العقل) was considered a light by which intelligibles (المعقولات) are manifested. All of this is based on analyzing the meaning of visible light as that which is manifest by its essence and makes others manifest.

Since the existence (وجود) of a thing is that by which it manifests itself to other things, it is a perfect instance of light. Furthermore, since all contingent beings (الممكنة الوجود) only exist through the creative act of Allah, the Exalted, He is the most perfect instance of light. Thus, there is an existence and a light with which things are characterized—which is their existence and their borrowed light taken from Him, the Exalted—and there is an existence and a light subsisting by His essence (قائم بذاته), through which things are brought into existence and illuminated.

He, the Glorified, is the Light by which the heavens and the earth are manifested. This is the meaning of His saying: اللهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ, where light is attributed to the heavens and the earth and then predicated of the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة). Based on this, one should interpret the view of those who said the meaning is "Allah is the Illuminator (منور) of the heavens and the earth." The primary intent is that the light intended here is not the borrowed light subsisting in them—which is the existence predicated of them—for Allah is far exalted and sanctified above that.

From this, it is understood that He, the Exalted, is not unknown to any thing, since the manifestation of every thing to itself or to others is only through His manifestation. Thus, He is manifest by His essence to it and before it. To this reality, His saying, the Exalted, refers two verses later: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالطَّيْرُ صَافَّاتٍ كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلاتَهُ وَتَسْبِيحَهُ — "Have you not regarded that Allah is glorified by whoever is in the heavens and the earth, and the birds in flight? Each knows his prayer and glorification." For there is no meaning to glorification (التسبيح) and the knowledge of it and of prayer (الصلاة) while being ignorant of the One to Whom they pray and Whom they glorify. It is thus similar to His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification" (17:44). The discussion regarding this will come to you, Allah willing.

In summary, the meaning of light in His saying: اللهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ is His light, the Exalted, from which the general light radiates, illuminating everything. This is equivalent to the existence (وجود) of every thing and its manifestation to itself and to others, which is the general mercy (الرحمة العامة).

His saying: مَثَلُ نُورِهِ — "The parable of His light." He, the Exalted, describes His light. The attribution of light to the pronoun referring to Him—which appears to be an attribution of possession—is evidence that the intent is not to describe the Light which is Allah Himself, but rather the borrowed light (النور المستعار) that He emanates. Nor is it the general borrowed light by which everything is manifested—which is the existence that things receive from Him and are characterized by. The evidence for this is His saying after completing the parable: يَهْدِي اللهُ لِنُورِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ — "Allah guides to His light whomever He wishes." For if it were the general light, it would not be specific to some things over others; rather, it is His specific light for the believers who possess the reality of faith, as the context indicates.

The Exalted has attributed a light to Himself in other parts of His speech, as in His saying: يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ وَاللهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِ — "They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah shall perfect His light" (61:8); and His saying: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose description is that he is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122); and His saying: يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ — "He will give you two shares of His mercy, and ordain for you a light by which you may walk" (57:28); and His saying: أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ فَهو عَلى نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ — "Is he whose breast Allah has opened to Islam so that he follows a light from his Lord...?" (39:22). This is the light that Allah ordains for His believing servants, by which they are illuminated on their path to their Lord; it is the light of faith (الإيمان) and knowledge (المعرفة).

The intent is not the Quran, as some have said, for the verse describes the state of the believers in general, both before and after the revelation of the Quran. Furthermore, this light is a description (وصف) of them by which they are characterized, as indicated by His saying: لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ — "they shall have their reward and their light" (57:19), and His saying: يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا أَتْمِمْ لَنا نُورَنا — "they will say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us’" (66:8). The Quran is not a description of them, though if it is considered in terms of the knowledge it discloses, it returns to what we have stated.

His saying: كَمِشْكاةٍ فِيها مِصْباحٌ الْمِصْباحُ فِي زُجاجَةٍ — "as a niche wherein is a lamp, the lamp is in a glass." The object of comparison (المشبه به) is the totality of what is mentioned—the niche containing a lamp, the lamp in a glass, etc.—not merely the niche; otherwise, the meaning would be corrupted. This is frequent in the parables of the Quran.

His saying: الزُّجاجَةُ كَأَنَّها كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّيٌ — "the glass as it were a brilliant star." The comparison of the glass to a brilliant star is due to the increased brilliance and radiance of the lamp's light when the glass is placed over it. The flame thereby becomes more steady, without flickering from air currents or winds; thus, it is like a brilliant star in its glistening light and steady radiance.

His saying: يُوقَدُ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ لا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلا غَرْبِيَّةٍ يَكادُ زَيْتُها يُضِيءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نارٌ — "kindled from a blessed tree, an olive [tree], neither eastern nor western, whose oil almost illuminates even though fire has not touched it." This is a predicate after a predicate for the lamp; i.e., the lamp is ignited, taking its fuel from a blessed olive tree—meaning it is lit from olive oil taken from it. The meaning of the tree being "neither eastern nor western" is that it is not growing on the eastern side nor the western side such that the sun falls upon it at only one end of the day and shade covers it at the other, which would prevent its fruit from ripening fully and the oil from being pure and the illumination from being excellent. Rather, it is in an open, sunny spot (ضاحية), receiving its share of the sun throughout the day, so its oil is excellent due to the perfect ripening of its fruit.

The evidence for this meaning is His saying: يَكادُ زَيْتُها يُضِيءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نارٌ, for the apparent context is that this refers to the purity of the oil and its perfect readiness for ignition, which is a branch of the two descriptions: "neither eastern nor western."

As for the view of some that "neither eastern nor western" means it is not from the trees of this world which must grow either in the east or the west, or the view of others that it means it is not from the trees of the far east of the inhabited world nor its far west, but from the trees of the Levant (الشام) situated between the east and the west—whose oil is the best—these are not understood from the context.

His saying: نُورٌ عَلى نُورٍ — "light upon light." This is a predicate for a suppressed subject, which is a pronoun referring to the light of the glass understood from the context. The meaning is: the aforementioned light of the glass is a great light upon a similar light; i.e., in the perfection of its brilliance.

Regarding the meaning of "light upon light," it is said to be the doubling of light, not its multiplicity. Thus, it does not mean a specific or non-specific light above another similar light, nor that it is a total of only two lights, but rather that it is a doubled light without a limit to its doubling. This expression is common in speech.

This meaning is not without merit, although the intent of multiplicity also possesses subtlety and precision. For the light radiating from the lamp has a relation to it by way of essence and reality, and a relation to the glass over it by way of metaphor and figurative use. The light varies according to the variation of these two relations and multiplies by their multiplicity, even though in reality it belongs only to the lamp, while the glass is empty-handed of it. Thus, the glass, in view of the multiplicity of relations, has a light other than the light of the lamp, though it subsists through it and is derived from it.

This consideration applies exactly to the subject of the parable (الممثل له), for the light of faith and knowledge is a borrowed light radiating upon the hearts of the believers, kindled from His light, the Exalted, subsisting through it and derived from it.

In summary, the subject of the parable is the light of Allah radiating upon the hearts of the believers. The parable is the object of comparison: the light radiating from a glass over a lamp kindled from excellent, pure oil, placed in a niche. The light of the lamp radiating from the glass is gathered and reflected by the niche upon those illuminated by it, shining upon them with ultimate strength and quality.

The niche (المشكاة) was chosen to indicate the gathering of light within its hollow and its reflection into the interior of the house. The consideration of the oil being from an olive tree "neither eastern nor western" indicates the purity and quality of the oil, which affects the purity of the light radiating from its ignition and the excellence of the illumination, as evidenced by its oil almost illuminating even if fire has not touched it. The consideration of "light upon light" indicates the doubling of the light or the fact that the glass derives its illumination from the light of the lamp.

His saying: يَهْدِي اللهُ لِنُورِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ — "Allah guides to His light whomever He wishes." This is a new sentence (استئناف) providing the reason for the specific endowment of the believers with the light of faith and knowledge and the deprivation of others.
