It is known from the context that the intent of "whomever He wishes" refers to the people He mentions afterward: رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَلا بَيْعٌ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ — "men whom neither trade nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah," etc. Thus, "whomever He wishes" refers to the believers in the description of the perfection of their faith.

The meaning is: Allah has only guided those characterized by the perfection of faith to His light, excluding those characterized by disbelief—whom He will mention later—solely by His will. The meaning is not that Allah guides some individuals to His light and not others by His mere whim, such that it would require saying that He only wills guidance when the recipient is prepared for it through a good inner state and conduct—which is specific to the people of faith as opposed to the people of disbelief. Understand this.

The evidence for this is what follows in His saying: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ to the end of the verses, as will be explained, Allah willing.

His saying: وَيَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْأَمْثالَ لِلنَّاسِ وَاللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ — "Allah draws parables for mankind, and Allah has knowledge of all things." This is an indication that the parable drawn is a mode of knowledge. The parable was chosen because it is the easiest way to clarify realities and subtleties, shared by both the scholar and the commoner, each taking from it what is allotted to him. The Exalted said: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ وَما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ — "We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except those who possess knowledge" (29:43).

His saying, the Exalted: فِي بُيُوتٍ أَذِنَ اللهُ أَنْ تُرْفَعَ وَيُذْكَرَ فِيهَا اسْمُهُ — "In houses Allah has allowed to be raised and wherein His Name is celebrated." Permission (الإذن) in something is the notification of the removal of the barrier to its performance. The intent of "raising" (الرفع) is the raising of status and rank, which is veneration (التعظيم). Since greatness and loftiness belong to Allah alone, and no one shares in them except by relation to Him, the permission from Allah, the Exalted, to raise these houses is solely due to the relation of what is within them to Him.

From this, it becomes clear that the reason for their being raised is what is conjoined to it: the celebration of His Name therein. The context indicates continuity or readiness for it, so the meaning returns to something like: "that His Name be celebrated therein, and thus their status is raised thereby."

His saying: فِي بُيُوتٍ — "In houses" is connected to His saying in the previous verse: كَمِشْكاةٍ — "as a niche," or His saying: يَهْدِي اللهُ — "Allah guides," etc., and the result is the same. Among these houses, the mosques (المساجد) are certain, for they are prepared for the celebration of His Name and dedicated to that. The Exalted has said: وَمَساجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللهِ كَثِيراً — "and mosques wherein the Name of Allah is much celebrated" (22:40).

His saying, the Exalted: يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ فِيها بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ رِجالٌ to the end of the verse. His glorification (تسبيحه), the Exalted, is His sanctification from everything that does not befit the court of His holiness. Mornings (الغدو) is the plural of ghadah (morning), and afternoons (الآصال) is the plural of asil (late afternoon). Distraction (الإلهاء) is to divert a person from what concerns and matters to him. Trade (التجارة), according to al-Raghib, is the management of capital in pursuit of profit. He said: "There is no word in their speech with a ta followed by a jim other than this term." Sale (البيع), as he said, is giving the priced item and taking the price. The overturning (قلب) of a thing, as he mentioned, is to turn it from one face to another; taqlib is the intensive form of it, and taqallub is its acceptance. Thus, the overturning of hearts and eyes is their transformation from one mode of perception to another.

His saying: يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ فِيها بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ — "glorifying Him therein in the mornings and the afternoons" is a description of the "houses" or a new sentence to clarify His saying: وَيُذْكَرَ فِيهَا اسْمُهُ. The glorification being in the mornings and afternoons is a metaphor for their continuity in it, not that glorification is restricted to these two times.

The sufficiency of mentioning glorification (التسبيح) without mentioning praise (التحميد) with it is because He, the Exalted, is known by all His attributes of perfection; there is no veil over Him, since the premise is that He is Light, and Light is that which is manifest by its essence and makes others manifest. Sincerity of knowledge only requires the negation of deficiencies from Him and His sanctification from what does not befit Him. When glorification is complete, nothing else remains with it, and knowledge is perfected. Then, when knowledge is perfected, commendation and praise occur. In short, description with attributes of perfection takes place after the attainment of knowledge, as the Exalted said: سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "Clear is Allah of what they allege, [all] except Allah’s chosen servants" (37:159-160). Thus, He sanctified Him from what they describe Him with, except what He was described with by those of His servants whom He chose for Himself. A discussion on the meaning of His praise (حمده) preceded in the commentary of Surah al-Hamd.

In another explanation, His praise—which is His commendation with attributes of perfection—is equivalent to the attainment of the light of knowledge, while His glorification—which is sanctification by negating what does not befit Him—is a preamble to its attainment. The verse is in the position of explaining their traits that necessitate their guidance to His light; therefore, it naturally restricted itself to mentioning the preamble, which is glorification. Understand that.

His saying: رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَلا بَيْعٌ — "men whom neither trade nor sale distracts." When trade (التجارة) is contrasted with sale (البيع), the understood meaning according to convention is the continuity of earning through buying and selling, while sale is the single, momentary act of earning. The difference between them is the difference between the momentary and the continuous. Thus, the meaning of negating "sale" after negating "trade"—despite it being negated by the negation of trade—is to indicate that they are not distracted from their Lord in their earnings constantly, nor at any single moment. In other words, neither a continuous trade nor any particular sale among the sales they conduct during their trade makes them forget their Lord.

It is said: the reason for negating "sale" after negating "trade" is that the profit in a sale is immediate and actual, unlike trade which is the profession. Thus, the absence of distraction by trade does not necessitate the absence of distraction by a currently profitable sale. Therefore, sale was negated a second time after the negation of trade, and the word "no" (لا) was repeated to remind of the negation and emphasize it. This is a good explanation.

His saying: عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَإِقامِ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءِ الزَّكاةِ — "from the remembrance of Allah and the maintenance of prayer and the giving of zakat." Iqam is iqamah (maintenance) with the ta omitted for lightness.

The intent of maintaining prayer and giving zakat is the performance of all righteous deeds that Allah, the Exalted, has commanded His servants to perform in their worldly life. Maintaining prayer represents the performance of the servant's duties of servitude toward Allah, the Glorified, and giving zakat represents his duties toward the creation, as each is a pillar in its respective domain.

The contrast between the "remembrance of Allah" (ذِكْرِ اللهِ) and "maintaining prayer and giving zakat"—while they, especially prayer, are part of the remembrance of Allah—suggests that the remembrance of Allah intended here is the remembrance of the heart (الذكر القلبي) which is the opposite of forgetfulness and heedlessness; it is a cognitive remembrance (ذكر علمي), just as prayer and zakat are practical remembrance (ذكر عملي).

Thus, the mentioned contrast suggests that the meaning of His saying: عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَإِقامِ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءِ الزَّكاةِ is that they are not occupied by anything away from their continuous remembrance of their Lord with their hearts, nor from their timed remembrance through their deeds of prayer and zakat. At that point, the beauty of the contrast between trade and sale on one hand, and the remembrance of Allah and maintenance of prayer, etc., on the other, becomes apparent. The meaning returns to the fact that neither a continuous nor a momentary distractor diverts them from the continuous and the timed remembrance. Understand that.

His saying: يَخافُونَ يَوْماً تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ الْقُلُوبُ وَالْأَبْصارُ — "fearing a day in which hearts and sights will be overturned." This is the Day of Resurrection. The "hearts and sights" intended are those of both the believers and the disbelievers, as the terms are plural and prefixed with the definite article, which denotes generality.

As for the overturning of hearts and sights, the verses describing the state of the Day of Resurrection indicate that it occurs with the manifestation of the reality of the matter and the lifting of the veil, as the Exalted said: فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22); and He said: وَبَدا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ ما لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ — "and there will appear to them from Allah what they had never reckoned" (39:47), among other verses.

Thus, hearts and sights will be diverted on that Day from the worldly witnessing and vision that distracts from Allah and veils the truth and reality, toward another kind of witnessing and vision. This is the vision by the light of faith and knowledge. The believer will see by the light of his Lord—which is the light of faith and knowledge—looking toward the honor of Allah. The disbeliever will be blinded and will find nothing but what grieves him. The Exalted said: وَأَشْرَقَتِ الْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّها — "The earth will shine with the light of its Lord" (39:69); and He said: يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ — "The day you will see the faithful men and women, with their light moving swiftly before them and on their right" (57:12); and He said: وَمَنْ كانَ فِي هذِهِ أَعْمى فَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ أَعْمى — "But whoever is blind in this [world] will be blind in the Hereafter" (17:72); and He said: وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ إِلى رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ — "Some faces will be fresh on that day, looking at their Lord" (75:22-23); and He said: كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجُوبُونَ — "No indeed! They will be barred from their Lord on that day" (83:15).

From what has passed, it has become clear:

First: The reason for specifically mentioning this attribute—the overturning of hearts and sights—among the descriptions of the Day of Resurrection. This is because the discourse is aimed at explaining what serves as a means to His guidance toward His light—which is the light of faith and knowledge by which one is illuminated on the Day of Resurrection and by which one sees.

Second: That the "hearts and sights" intended are the souls (النفوس) and their insights (بصائرها).

Third: That describing the Day with His saying: تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ الْقُلُوبُ وَالْأَبْصارُ is to explain the cause of fear. They only fear the Day because of the overturning of hearts and sights within it, and they only fear this overturning because one of its two outcomes is the deprivation of the light of Allah and the vision of His honor—which is eternal wretchedness and everlasting punishment. In reality, they fear for themselves.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللهُ أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا وَيزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَاللهُ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ — "that Allah may reward them by the best of what they have done, and enhance them out of His grace, and Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning." It appears that the lam in "that He may reward them" (لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ) denotes the ultimate purpose. What Allah has mentioned during the discourse are their righteous deeds and the beautiful reward for every righteous act, as stipulated in His speech. Thus, His saying that He rewards them by the best of what they have done means that He rewards them for their deeds in every category with the reward of the best deed in that category. This returns to the fact that He, the Exalted, purifies their deeds; He does not scrutinize them with reprimand for aspects that would necessitate their deficiency or the lowering of their status, but rather considers the good among them as the best.

This meaning is supported by His saying at the end of the verse: وَاللهُ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ, for its apparent meaning is the absence of meticulousness in the reckoning of good deeds by overlooking their deficient aspects, thus joining the good to the best.

His saying: وَيَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — "and enhance them out of His grace." Grace (الفضل) is a gift. This is an explicit text stating that He, the Exalted, gives them from His grace what is not in exchange for their righteous deeds. Even clearer is His saying in another place: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ — "They will have therein whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35), where its apparent meaning is that this promised "more" is something beyond what their will can encompass.

His speech, the Glorified, has indicated that their reward is that they shall have whatever they wish. The Exalted said: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُتَّقُونَ لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "Those are the God-conscious (المتقون). They will have whatever they wish with their Lord. That is the reward of the virtuous" (39:33-34); and He said: أَمْ جَنَّةُ الْخُلْدِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ كانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزاءً وَمَصِيراً لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ خالِدِينَ — "or the Garden of Abidance, which the God-conscious have been promised? It will be their reward and resort. They will have therein whatever they wish, abiding [forever]" (25:15-16); and He said: لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ كَذلِكَ يَجْزِي اللهُ الْمُتَّقِينَ — "They will have therein whatever they wish. Thus does Allah reward the God-conscious" (16:31).

Thus, this "more" (المزيد) which is beyond the reward for deeds is something higher and greater than what human will can encompass or what human effort can reach. This is the most wondrous thing the Quran promises the believers and gives them glad tidings of; so reflect deeply upon it.

His saying: وَاللهُ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ is a new sentence whose ultimate purpose is to explain the two previous clauses by His will, similar to His previous saying: يَهْدِي اللهُ لِنُورِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ, as explained before.

The summary is that they performed righteous deeds and had a reward equivalent to their deeds, as is apparent from His saying: وَتُوَفَّى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ — "and every soul shall be paid in full for what it has done" (16:111) and similar verses. However, He, the Exalted, rewards them for each of their deeds with the reward of the best deed performed in its category, without being meticulous in the reckoning. This is a gift. Then He provides them with something that is higher and more exalted than what their will can encompass; this too is a gift and a provision without reckoning. Provision (الرزق) from Allah is a pure gift, without the recipients possessing any part of it or deserving it from Him, the Exalted; thus, He may specify whatever He wishes of it for whomever He wishes.

However, He, the Exalted, has promised them provision and sworn to fulfill it in His saying: فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ — "By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth" (51:23). Thus, He has granted them the right to its essence—which is what He rewards them with according to their deeds—but as for what is beyond that, He has not granted them ownership of it; thus, He may specify it for whomever He wishes. This can only be explained by His will. There is a completion to this discourse that will come to you, Allah willing, in an independent discussion.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَعْمالُهُمْ كَسَرابٍ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ الظَّمْآنُ ماءً to the end of the verse. A mirage (السراب) is what glistens in the desert like water but has no reality. A plain (القيع والقاع) is level ground; their singular forms are qi'ah and qa'ah, like tinah (a fig) and tamrah (a date). The thirsty (الظمآن) is the one who is parched.

When the Glorified mentioned the believers and described them as remembering Him in venerated houses, distracted from Him by neither trade nor sale, and that Allah—Who is the Light of the heavens and the earth—guides them thereby to His light and honors them with the light of His knowledge, He contrasted this by mentioning those who disbelieve. He described their deeds, at times, as having no reality, like a mirage in a plain that leads to no end; and at other times, as being like layers of darkness, one above another, with no light accompanying them, serving as barriers against the light. This verse contains the first description.

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَعْمالُهُمْ كَسَرابٍ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ الظَّمْآنُ ماءً حَتَّى إِذا جاءَهُ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْئاً — "As for the faithless, their works are like a mirage in a plain, which the thirsty man supposes to be water, until when he comes to it he finds it to be nothing." He likened their deeds—which they perform of sacrifices, litanies, and other acts of worship to draw near to their deities—to a mirage in a plain which a person supposes to be water, but it has no reality that would produce the effects of water, such as quenching thirst.

It was said "the thirsty man supposes it to be water"—even though a mirage appears as water to every observer—because the intent is to describe his journey toward it, and no one journeys toward it except the thirsty man, driven by his thirst. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَهُ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْئاً — "until when he comes to it he finds it to be nothing." It is as if it were said: like a mirage in a plain which the thirsty man imagines to be water, so he journeys toward it and approaches it to quench his thirst and relieve his parchedness; he continues to journey until, when he comes to it, he finds it to be nothing.

The expression "he comes to it" (جاءَهُ) was used rather than saying "he reached it" or "arrived at it" to intimate that there is One Who desires his coming and awaits him—and that is Allah, the Glorified. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: وَوَجَدَ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ فَوَفَّاهُ حِسابَهُ — "and he finds Allah with him, and He pays him his full account." This conveys that these people desire through their deeds to attain something toward which their primordial nature (الفطرة) and innate disposition (الجبلة) drive them—which is the felicity (السعادة) that every human being desires by his nature. However, their deeds do not lead them to it. Nor do the deities from whom they seek a good reward through their deeds have any reality. Rather, that to which their deeds lead, and which encompasses them and rewards them, is Allah, the Glorified; so He pays them their account in full. Paying the account in full is a metaphor for rewarding according to what the account of deeds necessitates and delivering what the owner of the deeds deserves.

Thus, in the verse, there is a comparison of their deeds to a mirage; a comparison of them to a thirsty man who desires water and has fresh water near him, but he turns away from it and does not listen to his Master who advises him and invites him to drink it, but instead supposes the mirage to be water and journeys toward it. There is also a comparison of their return to Allah, the Glorified, at the arrival of their appointed terms (الآجال)—at which point deeds are completed—to the thirsty man journeying to the mirage when he comes to it and finds his Master there, who had been advising him and inviting him to drink the water.

These are a people who were distracted from the remembrance of their Lord and the righteous deeds that lead to His light, wherein lies their felicity. They supposed that their felicity lay with others besides Him—the deities they call upon and the deeds that draw them near to them. They devoted themselves to those mirage-like deeds and exhausted what they could perform during their lifespans until their terms arrived and they approached the Abode of the Hereafter. They found nothing of what they had hoped for from their deeds, nor any trace of the divinity of their deities; so Allah paid them their account in full, and Allah is swift at reckoning.

His saying: وَاللهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ — "and Allah is swift at reckoning" is due to His knowledge encompassing the little and the much, the insignificant and the momentous, the subtle and the grand, the preceding and the succeeding, all equally.

Know that although the verse apparently describes the state of the disbelievers among the people of religions, especially the polytheists among the idolaters, the explanation applies to others as well, such as those who deny the Creator (الصانع). For every human being, whoever they may be, sees a felicity for themselves in life and does not doubt that the means to attain it are the deeds they perform. If they are among those who believe in the Creator and see Him as the influencer of their felicity in some way, they use their deeds as a means to attain His pleasure and the success in the felicity He ordains for them. If they are among those who deny Him and attribute influence to something else, they use their deeds to direct what they believe to be the influencer—such as time (الدهر), nature (الطبيعة), or matter (المادة)—toward the felicity of their worldly life, beyond which they believe in nothing.

These people see the influencer in whose hand lies the felicity of their life as something other than Him, the Exalted, while there is no influencer but Him. They see their worldly efforts as leading them to their felicity, but they are nothing but a mirage with no reality. They continue to strive until, when the deeds ordained for them are completed by the arrival of their named terms, they find nothing there and witness that what they had hoped for was nothing but a passing phantom or the dream of a sleeper. At that point, Allah pays them their account in full, and Allah is swift at reckoning.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ كَظُلُماتٍ فِي بَحْرٍ لُجِّيٍّ يَغْشاهُ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ سَحابٌ — "Or like layers of darkness in a fathomless sea, covered by waves, with waves above them, and clouds above them." This is a second comparison for their deeds, through which it appears that they are veils (حجب) accumulated upon their hearts, veiling them from the light of knowledge. It has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, that they are in darkness, as in His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْلِياؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُمْ مِنَ النُّورِ إِلَى الظُّلُماتِ — "As for the faithless, their patrons are the rebels, who drive them out of light into darkness" (2:257); and His saying: كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها — "like one whose description is that he is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122); and His saying: كَلَّا بَلْ رانَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجُوبُونَ — "No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. No indeed! They will be barred from their Lord on that day" (83:14-15).

His saying: أَوْ كَظُلُماتٍ فِي بَحْرٍ لُجِّيٍ is conjoined to "like a mirage" (كَسَرابٍ) in the previous verse. A fathomless sea (البحر اللجي) is a sea with surging waves, related to the lujjah (depth) of the sea, which is the surging of its waves. The meaning is: their deeds are like layers of darkness existing in a fathomless sea.

His saying: يَغْشاهُ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ مَوْجٌ مِنْ فَوْقِهِ سَحابٌ — "covered by waves, with waves above them, and clouds above them" is a description of the sea, brought to establish the layers of darkness assumed within it. Its description is that it is covered and surrounded by a wave, above which is another wave, above which are clouds, all of which prevent it from being illuminated by the lights of the sun, the moon, and the stars.

His saying: ظُلُماتٌ بَعْضُها فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ — "layers of darkness, one above another" is a confirmation to explain that the intended darknesses are those accumulated upon one another, not scattered ones. He emphasized this by His saying: إِذا أَخْرَجَ يَدَهُ لَمْ يَكَدْ يَراها — "when he holds out his hand, he can hardly see it." For the closest thing a person witnesses of himself is his own self, and he is more capable of seeing his hand than his other limbs because he can bring it close to his sight as he wishes. If he holds out his hand and can hardly see it, the darkness is extreme.

These people, as they journey toward Allah and return to Him through their deeds, are like one riding a fathomless sea covered by waves, above which are waves, above which are clouds, in accumulated darkness of the most intense kind, with no light there to illuminate by which he might be guided to the shore of salvation.

His saying: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَجْعَلِ اللهُ لَهُ نُوراً فَما لَهُ مِنْ نُورٍ — "and he for whom Allah has not ordained any light has no light." This is a negation of light for them because Allah has not ordained it for them. How could it be otherwise? For the Ordainer of light is Allah, Who is the Light of everything. If He does not ordain light for a thing, it has no light, as there is no ordainer besides Him, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالطَّيْرُ صَافَّاتٍ to the end of the verse. When the Glorified mentioned that He is the Light by which the heavens and the earth are illuminated, and that He specifies the believers among His servants with His additional light while the disbelievers have no share in that, He began to argue for this using what is in this verse and the four following verses.

The fact that He, the Exalted, is the Light of the heavens and the earth is evidenced by the fact that whatever is in the heavens and the earth exists with an existence (وجود) that is not from itself, nor from anything within them, due to its being like them in its poverty (الفاقة). Thus, the existence of whatever is within them is from Allah, to Whom all needs ultimately return.

The existence of everything within them, just as it manifests existence itself, points to the One Who manifests it by the existence He has emanated upon it. Thus, He is a Light by which the thing is illuminated, and it points to its Illuminator by the light that has radiated upon it. There is a light by which everything is illuminated; thus, everything within them points to the fact that behind it is something sanctified from the darkness that covered it, the poverty that accompanied it, and the deficiency that is inseparable from it. This is the glorification (تسبيح) of whatever is in the heavens and the earth for Him, the Glorified. Its necessary consequence is the negation of independence for everything besides Him and the removal of any deity or lord who manages affairs apart from Him, the Exalted.

To this, His saying refers: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالطَّيْرُ صَافَّاتٍ كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلاتَهُ وَتَسْبِيحَهُ — "Have you not regarded that Allah is glorified by whoever is in the heavens and the earth, and the birds in flight? Each knows his prayer and glorification." By this, He argues for His being the Light of the heavens and the earth, because light is that by which the illuminated thing is manifested, and then by its manifestation, it points to its manifestor. He, the Exalted, manifests and brings into existence by His manifestation and creation of things, and then they point to His manifestation and existence.

The verse adds to this by pointing to subtleties that complete the explanation:

Among them: Its specific mention of "whoever is in the heavens and the earth and the birds in flight"—who are the rational beings and some of those possessing a spirit—along with the generality of glorification for others, according to His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise."

Perhaps this is by way of choosing matters from the wonders of creation for mention. For the manifestation of the rational being, indicated by the expression "whoever is in the heavens and the earth," is among the wondrous matters of creation that amaze the intellect of the possessor of intellect. Similarly, the formation of birds in flight in the atmosphere is among the most wondrous and exquisite things seen of the actions of sentient animals.

It appears from some that the intent of "whoever is in the heavens" etc., is all things, and He only expressed it with the term for rational beings because the glorification attributed to them is among the affairs of rational beings, or to draw attention to the strength of that evidence and the clarity of that indication, treating the "language of state" (لسان الحال) as equivalent to the "language of speech" (المقال).

However, this does not harmonize with the attribution of knowledge to them in His subsequent saying: كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلاتَهُ وَتَسْبِيحَهُ — "Each knows his prayer and glorification."

Among them: Beginning the speech with His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ — "Have you not regarded." In this is an indication of the manifestation of their glorification and the clarity of their evidence for His sanctification, such that no doubter can doubt it. Often, certain knowledge (العلم الجازم) is expressed as "vision" (الرؤية), as in His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ — "Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth?" (18:19). The address therein is general to every possessor of intellect, even if it is specific in terms of the wording.

It is possible that it is an address specific to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and Allah had shown him the glorification of whoever is in the heavens and the earth and the birds in flight among what He showed him of the dominion (ملكوت) of the heavens and the earth. This is not unprecedented for him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as he showed the people the glorification of the pebbles in his hand, as reported in reliable narrations.

Among them: That the verse generalizes knowledge to everything mentioned in the heavens and the earth and the birds. Some discussion regarding this preceded in the commentary of His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification" (17:44), and the completion of the discourse on it will come in the commentary of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah, Allah willing.

The view of some that the pronoun in His saying: قَدْ عَلِمَ — "knows" refers to Him, the Exalted, is refuted by its lack of harmony with the context, especially His subsequent saying: وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ — "and Allah knows best what they do." Similar to this is the view of others that the attribution of knowledge to the totality of what preceded is metaphorical, treating the non-knower as a knower due to the strength of its evidence for its glorification and sanctification.

Among them: Specifically mentioning glorification (التسبيح) while things also point to His attributes of perfection—which is praise (التحميد)—just as they glorify Him, as evidenced by proof and supported by His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ. Perhaps the reason for this is that the verses are aimed at monotheism (التوحيد) and the negation of partners, and that is more closely related to sanctification (التنزيه). For whoever calls upon another deity besides Allah or relies upon another in some way only disbelieves by affirming a characteristic of the existence of that thing for the Deity, the Exalted; thus, its negation is only achieved through sanctification, not praise. Understand this.

As for His saying: كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلاتَهُ وَتَسْبِيحَهُ, his prayer (صلاته) is his supplication (دعاؤه). Supplication is the direction of the supplicant toward the one called upon for his need. In it is an indication of a need in the supplicant from which the one called upon is free. Thus, it is closer to indicating sanctification than to commendation and praise.

Among them: That the verse attributes glorification and the knowledge of it to whoever is in the heavens and the earth, thus encompassing both the believer and the disbeliever. From this, it appears that there are two lights: a general light (نور عام) that encompasses all things, in which the believer and the disbeliever are equal—to which verses like the verse of the Covenant (آية الذر) refer: وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى شَهِدْنا أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ — "and made them testify concerning themselves, ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes, we testify,’ lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, ‘Indeed we were unaware of this’" (7:172); and His saying: فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22), among others—and a specific light (نور خاص), which is what the verses mention and which is specific to His friends among the believers.

Thus, the light with which He, the Exalted, illuminates His creation, like the mercy with which He has mercy upon them, is of two types: general and specific. The Exalted has said: وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ — "and My mercy embraces all things" (7:156); and His saying: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy" (45:30). He has combined the two in His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً — "O you who have faith! Be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) and have faith in His Apostle. He will give you two shares of His mercy, and ordain for you a light" (57:28). The light mentioned therein is the "light upon light" corresponding to the second of the two shares of mercy.

His saying: وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ — "and Allah knows best what they do." Among their actions is their glorification of Him, the Glorified. This glorification, although in some stages it is their very existence, the application of the name "glorification" allows it to be counted as an action of theirs with this consideration.

In mentioning His knowledge, the Exalted, of what they do immediately after mentioning their glorification, there is an encouragement for the believers and a gratitude to them that their Lord knows this of them and will reward them with a beautiful reward. It is also a notification of the completion of the argument against the disbelievers. For among the levels of His knowledge, the Exalted, is the writing of deeds and the Manifest Book (الكتاب المبين) in which their deeds are established; thus, their glorification through their existence is established therein, followed by their denial with their tongues.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَإِلَى اللهِ الْمَصِيرُ — "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and toward Allah is the destination." The context of the verse—situated between His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ etc., which is an argument for the encompassment of His general light for everything, and His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يُزْجِي etc., and what follows it, which is an argument for the specificity of the specific light—suggests that it is like a mediator between the two categories; i.e., between the two matters, serving as an argument for both. His kingdom, the Exalted, over everything and His being the destination for them is evidence for His generalizing His general light and His specifying His specific light; He does what He wishes and judges as He intends.

His saying: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ specifies the kingdom and restricts it to Him, the Exalted. Thus, He may do what He wishes and judge as He intends; He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned. The necessary consequence of restricting the kingdom to Him is His being the destination (المصير) for everything. Since there is no king but Him and to Him is the return and destination of everything, He may do what He wishes and judge as He intends.

From here, it appears that the intent—and Allah knows best—of His saying: وَإِلَى اللهِ الْمَصِيرُ is His being the ultimate authority (مرجعية) in all matters, rather than the Resurrection alone, similar to His saying: أَلا إِلَى اللهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ — "Behold! To Allah do all matters eventually come" (42:53).

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يُزْجِي سحاباً ثُمَّ يُؤَلِّفُ بَيْنَهُ ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُ رُكاماً فَتَرَى الْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلالِهِ — "Have you not regarded that Allah drives the clouds, then He joins them together, then He piles them up, whereat you see the rain issuing from their midst?" Driving (الإزجاء) is pushing. Piled up (الركام) is what is accumulated one upon another. Rain (الودق) is the rainfall. Midst (الخلال) is the plural of khalal, which is the gap between two things.

The address is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his capacity as a listener, thus encompassing every listener. The meaning is: Have you not seen, you and everyone who sees, that Allah drives the scattered clouds with the winds, then joins them together, then makes them accumulated one upon another, so you see the rain issuing from their gaps and openings and descending upon the earth?

His saying: وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ مِنْ جِبالٍ فِيها مِنْ بَرَدٍ فَيُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَصْرِفُهُ عَنْ مَنْ يَشاءُ يَكادُ سَنا بَرْقِهِ يَذْهَبُ بِالْأَبْصارِ — "And He sends down from the sky, from the mountains [of clouds] within it, some hail, and He strikes with it whomever He wishes, and turns it away from whomever He wishes. The brilliance of its lightning almost takes away the sight." The sky (السماء) is the direction of height. His saying: "from the mountains within it" (مِنْ جِبالٍ فِيها) is an explanation of the sky. Mountains (الجبال) is the plural of jabal (mountain), which is well-known. His saying: "of hail" (مِنْ بَرَدٍ) is an explanation of the mountains. Hail (البرد) is the pieces of ice descending from the sky. Its being "mountains" within it is a metaphor for its abundance and accumulation. Brilliance (السنا) is light.

The speech is conjoined to His saying: "He drives" (يُزْجِي). The meaning is: Have you not regarded that Allah sends down from the sky some of the hail accumulated within it like mountains, striking with it whomever He wishes, thus destroying farms and orchards and perhaps killing people and livestock, and turning it away from whomever He wishes so they are not harmed by it? The light of its lightning almost takes away the sight.

The verse—as the context suggests—is aimed at providing the reason for what preceded regarding His specifying the believers with His light. The meaning is: the matter in that regard returns to His will, the Exalted, just as you see that when He wishes, He sends down from the sky rain in which there are benefits for people, their livestock, their farms, and their orchards; and when He wishes, He sends down hail, striking with it whomever He wishes and turning it away from whomever He wishes.

His saying, the Exalted: يُقَلِّبُ اللهُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِأُولِي الْأَبْصارِ — "Allah alternates the night and the day. There is indeed a lesson in that for those who have insight." This is another explanation of the matter returning solely to His will, the Exalted. The alternation (تقليب) of the night and the day is their management by replacing one with the other. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ خَلَقَ كُلَّ دَابَّةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى بَطْنِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى رِجْلَيْنِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى أَرْبَعٍ — "Allah created every animal from water. Among them are those that crawl on their bellies, and among them are those that walk on two legs, and among them are those that walk on four." This is another explanation of the matter returning purely to His will, the Exalted, as He creates every animal from water, yet their modes of walking differ. Among them are those that crawl on their bellies, such as snakes and worms; among them are those that walk on two legs, such as humans and birds; and among them are those that walk on four, such as cattle and beasts of prey. The Glorified restricted Himself to these three types—though there are others—to achieve the purpose with this amount of brevity.

His saying, the Exalted: يَخْلُقُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ "Allah creates whatever He wishes" (24:45) is a causal explanation for the previously mentioned diversity among living creatures despite the unity of the matter from which they were created. It clarifies that the matter rests purely upon the will (المشية) of Allah; thus, He may extend a flow (فيض) from His bounties to all His creation, such as the general light and general mercy, or He may specify a particular flow from His bounties for some of His creation to the exclusion of others, such as the specific light and specific mercy.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ "Indeed Allah has power over all things" (24:45) is a causal explanation for His saying: "Allah creates whatever He wishes." For the attribution of absolute power (القدرة) over every thing necessitates that no thing, in its coming into being (كينونته), depends on any matter beyond His will; otherwise, His power over it would be conditional upon the occurrence of that matter, which is a contradiction. This is a subtle chapter of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — which will become somewhat clearer, Allah willing, in the following discussion.

Philosophical Discussion

We do not doubt that the contingent existents (الموجودات الممكنة) we find are effects (معلولة) terminating at the Necessary Being (الواجب), the Exalted. Many of them—especially in material things—depend for their existence upon conditions (شروط) without which they cannot be realized. For example, a human being who is a "son" depends for his existence upon the existence of parents and many other temporal (زمانية) and spatial (مكانية) conditions. Since it is necessary that everything upon which a thing depends is a part of its complete cause (علته التامة), the Necessary Being, the Exalted, is—according to this view—a part of its complete cause, not the complete cause alone.

Yes, in relation to the totality of the world (مجموع العالم), He is the complete cause, as it does not depend on anything other than Him. The same applies to the First Emanation (الصادر الأول), which the rest of the parts of the totality follow. As for the other parts of the world, He, the Exalted, is a part of their complete cause, due to the necessity of their dependence on the causes that precede them and the conditions and preparatory factors (المعدات) that accompany them.

This is the case if we consider each of the parts independently (بحياله) and then attribute it alone to the Necessary Being, the Exalted.

However, there is another, subtler perspective: the existential connection (الارتباط الوجودي)—which cannot be denied—between every thing and its contingent causes, conditions, and preparatory factors dictates a type of union (اتحاد) and connection (اتصال) between them. Thus, a single part is not absolutely separate; rather, in its determined existence (وجوده المتعين), it is restricted (مقيد) by everything it is connected to, its identity (الهوية) being continuous with others.

The human "son," whom we considered in the previous example from the first perspective as an independent and absolute existent—finding him dependent on many causes and conditions, with the Necessary Being, the Exalted, being one of them—becomes, according to this second perspective, an identity restricted by all the causes and conditions other than the Necessary Being upon which he was considered dependent. The reality (حقيقة) of Zayd, for instance, is: the human who is the son of so-and-so (father) and so-and-so (mother), born at such a time and such a place, preceded by such and such, and accompanied in his existence by such and such contingent beings.

This, then, is the reality of Zayd, for example. It is necessary that that whose reality is such does not depend on anything other than the Necessary Being. Thus, the Necessary Being is his complete cause, who has no dependence on anything else, and he has no need for anything other than His will. His power (قدرته), the Exalted, in relation to him, is absolute, unconditional, and unrestricted. This is His saying, the Exalted: يَخْلُقُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ "Allah creates whatever He wishes. Indeed Allah has power over all things."

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ وَاللهُ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "Certainly We have sent down manifest signs, and Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path" (24:46). He intends the Verse of Light and what follows it, which clarify the description of His light, the Exalted. The straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — is His way, upon which there is no path for wrath or straying for those guided to it, as He said: اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray" (1:6-7). The discussion regarding this has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-Hamd.

The concluding of the verse with His saying: وَاللهُ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "and Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path" is the reason for not restricting His saying: لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ "Certainly We have sent down manifest signs" with the phrase "to you" (إليكم), unlike the verse preceding the signs: لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ آياتٍ مُبَيِّناتٍ وَمَثَلاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "Certainly We have sent down to you manifest signs, and an example of those who passed away before you, and an advice for the God-conscious" (24:34).

For if it were said: "Certainly We have sent down to you manifest signs and Allah guides...", it would immediately come to mind that the verbal clarification is itself the guidance to the straight path, and that the addressees in general are guided to the straight path, while among them are the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness. And Allah knows best.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission from al-Abbas ibn Hilal, who said: "I asked al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: اللهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ 'Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth.' He (عليه‌السلام) said: 'A Guide for the inhabitants of the heavens and a Guide for the inhabitants of the earth.'"

In the narration of al-Barqi: "The guidance of those in the heavens and the guidance of those in the earth."

I say: If the intended meaning of guidance (الهداية) is the specific guidance—which is the guidance toward religious felicity—then it is an interpretation of one level of the meaning. If the intended meaning is the general guidance—which is the bringing of every thing to its perfection—then it accords with what has preceded.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ishaq ibn Jarir, who said: "A woman asked me to introduce her to Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), so I sought permission for her and he granted it. She entered with a female client (مولاة) of hers and said to him: 'O Abu Abdillah, the saying of Allah: زَيْتُونَةٍ لا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلا غَرْبِيَّةٍ "an olive tree, neither eastern nor western"—what did He mean by this?' He (عليه‌السلام) said to her: 'O woman, indeed Allah did not strike parables for trees; He only struck parables for the children of Adam.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission from Talhah ibn Zayd, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father (عليه‌السلام) regarding this verse: اللهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ "Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth." He said: "He began with the light of His own Self: مَثَلُ نُورِهِ 'The parable of His light' is the parable of His guidance in the heart of the believer. كَمِشْكاةٍ فِيها مِصْباحٌ 'as a niche wherein is a lamp'—the lamp is the interior of the believer, the glass is his heart, and the lamp is the light that Allah has placed in his heart."

"يُوقَدُ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ 'kindled from a blessed tree'—he said: 'The tree is the believer.' زَيْتُونَةٍ لا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلا غَرْبِيَّةٍ 'an olive tree, neither eastern nor western'—he said: 'On the dark side of the mountain; not western, meaning it has no east, and not eastern, meaning it has no west. When the sun rises, it rises upon it, and when it sets, it sets upon it.' يَكادُ زَيْتُها يُضِيءُ 'its oil almost glows'—the light in his heart almost glows even if he does not speak."

"نُورٌ عَلى نُورٍ 'Light upon light'—an obligation (فريضة) upon an obligation, and a Sunnah (سنة) upon a Sunnah. يَهْدِي اللهُ لِنُورِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ 'Allah guides to His light whomever He wishes'—Allah guides to His obligations and His Sunnahs whomever He wishes. وَيَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْأَمْثالَ لِلنَّاسِ 'and Allah draws parables for mankind'—this is a parable Allah has struck for the believer."

Then he said: "The believer moves within five lights: his entry is light, his exit is light, his knowledge is light, his speech is light, and his destination on the Day of Resurrection to Paradise is light." I said to Ja'far (عليه‌السلام): "They say: 'The parable of the light of the Lord.'" He said: "Glory be to Allah! There is no likeness (مثل) for Allah. Allah said: فَلا تَضْرِبُوا لِلَّهِ الْأَمْثالَ 'So do not coin parables for Allah' (16:74)."

I say: The tradition supports what preceded in the commentary of the verse. He (عليه‌السلام) sufficed in interpreting some phrases of the verse by mentioning some of the instances (المصاديق), such as what he mentioned regarding the end of His saying: "its oil almost glows" and His saying: "Light upon light."

As for his saying: "Glory be to Allah! There is no likeness for Allah," he only negates that the parable should be a likeness for the Light which is His name, the Exalted, predicated of Him. For its being a likeness for Him, the Exalted, would lead to incarnation (الحلول) or transformation (الانقلاب)—exalted is He far above that. Rather, it is a parable for His light bestowed upon the heavens and the earth. As for the pronoun in His saying: مَثَلُ نُورِهِ "The parable of His light," there is no harm in it referring back to Him, the Exalted, while maintaining the correct meaning.

In al-Tawhid, it is narrated from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) that he was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: اللهُ نُورُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ـ مَثَلُ نُورِهِ كَمِشْكاةٍ فِيها مِصْباحٌ "Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth. The parable of His light is a niche wherein is a lamp." He said: "It is a parable Allah struck for us. The Prophet and the Imams (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) are among the pointers (دلالات) of Allah and His signs (آيات) by which one is guided to monotheism, the interests of the religion, the laws of Islam, the Sunnahs, and the obligations. And there is no power except by Allah, the All-High, the All-Great."

I say: The narration is of the type that points to some of the instances (المصاديق), and it is of the most excellent instances, namely the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the pure ones from his household (عليه‌السلام). Otherwise, the verse, by its literal meaning, encompasses others besides them from the prophets (عليهم‌السلام), the successors, and the saints (الأولياء).

Indeed, the verse is not general for all believers because it includes in their description attributes that do not encompass everyone, such as His saying: رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَلا بَيْعٌ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ "men whom neither trade nor bargaining distracts from the remembrance of Allah" etc.

Several reports have been narrated through Shi'i chains regarding the application (تطبيق) of the individual terms of the verse to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his household (عليه‌السلام). These are instances of application (التطبيق) rather than formal exegesis (التفسير). Evidence for this is their variation in the manner of application, such as the narration of al-Kulayni in Rawdat al-Kafi, with his chain from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), in which it says: "The niche is the heart of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the lamp is the light in which there is knowledge, the glass is Ali or his heart, and the blessed tree, the olive that is neither eastern nor western, is Abraham (عليه‌السلام)—he was neither a Jew nor a Christian. And His saying: يَكادُ زَيْتُها يُضِيءُ 'its oil almost glows' etc., means their children almost speak with prophecy even if an angel has not descended upon them."

And what he narrated in al-Tawhid, with his chain to Isa ibn Rashid from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام), in which it says: "The niche is the light of knowledge in the chest of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the glass is the chest of Ali. يَكادُ زَيْتُها يُضِيءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نارٌ 'its oil almost glows though no fire should touch it'—the scholar from the family of Muhammad almost speaks with knowledge before he is asked. نُورٌ عَلى نُورٍ 'Light upon light'—an Imam aided by the light of knowledge and wisdom following an Imam from the family of Muhammad."

And what is in al-Kafi, with its chain from Salih ibn Sahl al-Hamdani from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), in which it says: "The niche is Fatimah (عليه‌السلام), the lamp is al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام), the glass is al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام), the blessed tree is Abraham (عليه‌السلام), and لا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلا غَرْبِيَّةٍ 'neither eastern nor western'—he was neither a Jew nor a Christian, and نُورٌ عَلى نُورٍ 'Light upon light'—an Imam after an Imam, and يَهْدِي اللهُ لِنُورِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ 'Allah guides to His light whomever He wishes'—Allah guides to the Imams (عليهم‌السلام) whomever He wishes."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: زَيْتُونَةٍ لا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلا غَرْبِيَّةٍ "an olive tree, neither eastern nor western." He said: "The heart of Abraham; neither Jewish nor Christian."

I say: This is of the type of mentioning some of the instances (المصاديق), and similar reports have been narrated through Shi'i chains from some of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) as mentioned before.

In it, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Anas ibn Malik and Buraydah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse: فِي بُيُوتٍ أَذِنَ اللهُ أَنْ تُرْفَعَ 'In houses Allah has allowed to be raised' (24:36). A man stood up to him and said: 'Which houses are these, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'The houses of the prophets.' Then Abu Bakr stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, is this house among them?'—referring to the house of Ali and Fatimah. He said: 'Yes, among the most excellent of them.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Majma' from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as a disconnected (مرسلا) report, and al-Qummi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir with his chain from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), and its wording is: "He said: 'They are the houses of the prophets, and the house of Ali (عليه‌السلام) is among them.'" In any case, it is of the type of mentioning some of the instances as previously stated.

In Nahj al-Balaghah, from a speech of his (عليه‌السلام) when he recited: رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَلا بيعٌ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ "men whom neither trade nor bargaining distracts from the remembrance of Allah": "Indeed, for remembrance (الذكر) there are people who have taken it in exchange for the world—so neither trade nor bargaining distracts them from it. They pass the days of life with it, calling out with warnings against the prohibitions of Allah into the ears of the heedless. They command justice (القسط) and follow it themselves, and they forbid the wrong (المنكر) and refrain from it themselves. It is as if they have crossed the world to the Hereafter while they are still in it, witnessing what lies beyond that. It is as if they have looked into the unseen (غيوب) of the people of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — during their long stay therein, and the Resurrection has realized its punishment upon them. Thus, they have uncovered the veil of that for the people of the world, until it is as if they see what people do not see and hear what they do not hear."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَلا بَيْعٌ "men whom neither trade nor bargaining distracts": "It is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) that they are a people who, when the time for prayer arrives, leave their trade and go to the prayer—and they are greater in reward than those who did not engage in trade."

I say: Meaning those who did not engage in trade but occupied themselves with the remembrance of Allah, as found in other narrations.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ibn Mardawayh and others from Abu Hurayrah and Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying, the Exalted: رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَلا بَيْعٌ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ "men whom neither trade nor bargaining distracts from the remembrance of Allah." He said: "They are those who travel through the land seeking the bounty of Allah."

I say: It seems the narration is incomplete, and its completion is in what was narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "They were men who sought the bounty of Allah, buying and selling, but when they heard the call to prayer, they cast aside what was in their hands and stood up for the mosque and prayed."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ "and Allah is swift at reckoning" (24:39). The Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) was asked: "How does He reckon them all at once?" He replied: "Just as He provides for them all at once."

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain from Mus'adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu Abdillah from his father from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made the clouds as sieves for the rain—they melt the hail until it becomes water so that it does not harm anything it hits. And what you see in it of hail and thunderbolts is a vengeance from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, with which He strikes whomever He wishes of His servants.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى بَطْنِهِ ـ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى رِجْلَيْنِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى أَرْبَعٍ "Some of them crawl on their bellies, some of them walk on two legs, and some of them walk on four" (24:45). He said: "On two legs are humans, on their bellies are snakes, and on four are cattle." And Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) said: "And among them are those who walk on more than that."

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلرَّسُولِ وَأَطَعْنَا ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٧)
They say, ‘We have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and we obey.’ Then, after that, a part of them refuse to comply, and they do not have faith. (47)
وَإِذَا دُعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٤٨)
When they are summoned to Allah and His Apostle that He may judge between them, behold, a part of them turn aside. (48)
وَإِن يَكُن لَّهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ يَأْتُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ مُذْعِنِينَ (٤٩)
But if justice be on their side, they come compliantly to him. (49)
أَفِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ أَمِ ٱرْتَابُوٓا۟ أَمْ يَخَافُونَ أَن يَحِيفَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ بَلْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٥٠)
Is there a sickness in their hearts? Do they have doubts, or fear that Allah and His Apostle will be unjust to them? Indeed, it is they who are the wrongdoers. (50)
إِنَّمَا كَانَ قَوْلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذَا دُعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٥١)
All the response of the faithful, when they are summoned to Allah and His Apostle that He may judge between them, is to say, ‘We hear and obey.’ It is they who are the felicitous. (51)
وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَخْشَ ٱللَّهَ وَيَتَّقْهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (٥٢)
Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, and fears Allah and is wary of Him—it is they who will be the triumphant. (52)
۞ وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ لَئِنْ أَمَرْتَهُمْ لَيَخْرُجُنَّ ۖ قُل لَّا تُقْسِمُوا۟ ۖ طَاعَةٌ مَّعْرُوفَةٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٥٣)
They swear by Allah with solemn oaths that if you order them they will surely march out. Say, ‘Do not swear! Honourable obedience [is all that is expected of you]. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do.’ (53)
قُلْ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْهِ مَا حُمِّلَ وَعَلَيْكُم مَّا حُمِّلْتُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُطِيعُوهُ تَهْتَدُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٥٤)
Say, ‘Obey Allah, and obey the Apostle.’ But if you turn your backs, [you should know that] he is only responsible for his burden and you are responsible for your own burden, and if you obey him, you will be guided, and the Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (54)
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَمَا ٱسْتَخْلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى ٱرْتَضَىٰ لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْنًا ۚ يَعْبُدُونَنِى لَا يُشْرِكُونَ بِى شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٥٥)
Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, just as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely change their state to security after their fear, while they worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me. Whoever is ungrateful after that—it is they who are the transgressors. (55)
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٥٦)
Maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and obey the Apostle so that you may receive [Allah’s] mercy. (56)
لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٥٧)
Do not suppose that those who are faithless can frustrate [Allah] on the earth. Their refuge shall be the Fire, and it is surely an evil destination. (57)

(Commentary)

The verses encompass the premise of the obligation to obey the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and that it is inseparable from the obedience to Allah, the Exalted. They also address the necessity of referring to his judgment and decree, stating that turning away from him is a sign of hypocrisy (النفاق). The passage concludes with a beautiful promise for the righteous among the believers and a threat to the disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَيَقُولُونَ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَبِالرَّسُولِ وَأَطَعْنا ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّى فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ» — And they say, ‘We believe in Allah and the Apostle, and we obey.’ Then a group of them turns away after that. (24:47) etc. This is an exposition of the state of some of the hypocrites (المنافقين) — those who outwardly profess faith while concealing disbelief — as they initially displayed faith and obedience, then subsequently turned away. Faith (الإيمان) in Allah is the heart's conviction in His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and in the religion He has legislated. Faith in the Messenger is the conviction that he is a messenger sent from his Lord, whose command is His command, whose prohibition is His prohibition, and whose judgment is His judgment, without him having any independent authority in the matter. Obedience (الطاعة) to Allah is the practical application of what He has legislated, while obedience to the Messenger is complying with his commands, abstaining from his prohibitions, and accepting what he has judged and decreed.

Thus, faith in Allah and obedience to Him pertain to the religion itself and its legislation, whereas faith in the Messenger and obedience to him pertain to what the Messenger conveys of the religion—insofar as he is the conveyor—and what he judges and decrees in disputes, as well as submitting to him in all of that.

There is, therefore, a distinction between the two types of faith and the two types of obedience in terms of the breadth or narrowness of their scope. This is indicated by the specific detail in the phrasing where it says: "We believe in Allah and in the Messenger" (آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَبِالرَّسُولِ). The repetition of the preposition "in" (بـ) points to the distinctness of the faith and obedience, rather than saying "We believe in Allah and the Messenger" by omitting the second preposition. Nevertheless, the two faiths are inseparable; one cannot exist without the other. The Exalted says: «وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ» — and seek to separate Allah from His apostles (4:150).

So, His saying: "And they say, ‘We believe in Allah and the Apostle, and we obey’" means: We have bound our hearts to the religion of Allah and its legislation, and to the fact that the Messenger only informs of the truth and only judges with the truth.

His saying: «ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّى فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ» — Then a group of them turns away after that — means that a party of those who said, "We believe in Allah and the Apostle, and we obey," subsequently turned away from the implications of their statement after having uttered it.

His saying: «وَما أُولئِكَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — and they are not believers — means those who made that claim are not truly believers. The demonstrative pronoun "those" (أُولئِكَ) refers to all who made the claim, not specifically the group that turned away, as suggested by the context, because the discourse is intended to censure them all.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَإِذا دُعُوا إِلَى اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ» — When they are summoned to Allah and His Apostle that he may judge between them, behold, a group of them is averse. (24:48). The context of the verse testifies that these verses were revealed concerning certain hypocrites who were summoned to the judgment of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding a dispute that occurred between them and others, but they refused to refer back to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); thus, the verses were revealed.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only judged between them by the judgment of Allah according to what Allah showed him, as the Exalted said: «إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ» — Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth, so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you. (4:105). Thus, the judgment has a relationship to him through direct execution, and a relationship to Allah, the Glorified, insofar as the judgment is in the light of His Sharia (شريعته) and by His appointment of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for judgment and decree.

From this, it becomes clear that the "summons to Allah that He may judge between them" refers to the summons to follow what His legislation requires in the matter of dispute. The "summons to His Messenger that he may judge between them" refers to the summons to follow what he decrees directly. It is apparent that the pronoun in "that he may judge" (لِيَحْكُمَ) refers to the Messenger. The subject was made singular rather than dual to indicate that the judgment of the Messenger is the judgment of the Exalted.

This verse, in relation to the previous one, is like the particular (الخاص) in relation to the universal (العام); it narrates a specific instance of their turning away, whereas the turning away mentioned in the previous verse was absolute.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَإِنْ يَكُنْ لَهُمُ الْحَقُّ يَأْتُوا إِلَيْهِ مُذْعِنِينَ» — But if the right is theirs, they come to him submissively. (24:49). Submission (الإذعان) is compliance (الانقياد). The apparent context, especially the phrase "they come to him" (يَأْتُوا إِلَيْهِ), suggests that "the right" (الحق) refers to the judgment of the Messenger, based on the claim that it is a truth from which he never deviates. The meaning is: if the right—which is the judgment of the Messenger—is in their favor and not against them, they come to his judgment submissively. Thus, they do not turn away from it except when it is against them, not for them. This implies that they follow their whims (الهوى) and do not desire to follow the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: «أَفِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ أَمِ ارْتابُوا أَمْ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يَحِيفَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَرَسُولُهُ» — Is there a sickness in their hearts? Or do they have doubts? Or do they fear that Allah and His Apostle will be unjust to them? (24:50) to the end of the verse. Injustice (الحيف) is wrongdoing (الجور).

The apparent context of the verses suggests that "sickness of the hearts" (مرض القلوب) refers to weakness of faith, as in the saying of the Exalted: «فَلا تَخْضَعْنَ بِالْقَوْلِ فَيَطْمَعَ الَّذِي فِي قَلْبِهِ مَرَضٌ» — so do not be complaisant in your speech, lest he in whose heart is a sickness should aspire (33:32), and His saying: «لَئِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهِ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْمُرْجِفُونَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ» — If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness and the rumourmongers in Madinah do not desist, We will surely rouse you against them (33:60), and other verses.

As for the view that "sickness of the hearts" refers to hypocrisy (النفاق), as some have interpreted it, this is refuted by His statement at the beginning of the verses: "and they are not believers," for He had already judged them to be hypocrites. There is no meaning, after establishing their hypocrisy, in asking whether they are hypocrites and then turning away from that by saying: "Rather, it is they who are the wrongdoers."

His saying: «أَمِ ارْتابُوا» — Or do they have doubts? — the absolute nature of "doubt" (الارتياب) suggests it refers to their doubt in their religion after having believed, rather than doubt in the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) suitability for judgment or his justice, as such specific doubts would naturally require a clarifying context.

His saying: «أَمْ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يَحِيفَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَرَسُولُهُ» — Or do they fear that Allah and His Apostle will be unjust to them? — means: do they turn away because they fear that Allah and His Messenger will wrong them, either because the divine Sharia followed by the Prophet's judgment is based on injustice and the suppression of legitimate rights, or because the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) does not observe the truth in his decree?

His saying: «بَلْ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ» — Rather, it is they who are the wrongdoers — is a transition (إضراب) from the previous three possibilities. This is because if the cause of their turning away were the sickness of their hearts or their doubt, they would not have come to him submissively even when the right was in their favor; rather, they would have turned away whether the right was for them or against them. As for the fear that Allah and His Messenger would be unjust to them, there is no basis for it, for Allah and His Messenger are free from injustice. Thus, their turning away from responding to the summons to the judgment of Allah and His Messenger is only because they are truly wrongdoers (ظالمون).

It appears that "wrongdoing" (الظلم) here refers to transgressing the bounds of faith despite professing it verbally, as He said just before: "and they are not believers," or specifically transgressing against non-monetary rights. If absolute wrongdoing were intended, the transition from the three previous possibilities to it would not be sound, as those possibilities are themselves forms of absolute wrongdoing. This is also indicated by the following verse.

It has become clear from the foregoing that the deliberation over the causes of turning away—assuming they were not hypocrites—is confined to these three distinct categories. The summary of the meaning is that they are hypocrites and not believers; for if they were not so, their turning away would be due to either the weakness of their faith, its disappearance through doubt, or a fear without a justifying cause. Fear of referring to a judge's decree only occurs when his injustice or deviation from truth to falsehood is suspected, and such a thing is not suspected regarding the judgment of Allah and His Messenger.

There has been lengthy discussion in the commentaries regarding the deliberation and transition in this verse, but what we have mentioned should suffice. Whoever desires more may refer to the more extensive works.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّما كانَ قَوْلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذا دُعُوا إِلَى اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ يَقُولُوا سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا» — All the response of the faithful, when they are summoned to Allah and His Apostle that he may judge between them, is to say, ‘We hear and we obey.’ (24:51) to the end of the verse. The context of "All the response of the faithful" (إِنَّما كانَ قَوْلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ), using the past tense "was" (كانَ) and the description of "faith" (الإيمان) in "the faithful" (الْمُؤْمِنِينَ), indicates that this is a requirement of the very nature of faith. For the requirement of faith in Allah and His Messenger, and the heart's commitment to follow what Allah and His Messenger judge, is to respond to the summons to their judgment rather than rejecting it.

Accordingly, the meaning of "when they are summoned to Allah and His Apostle that he may judge between them" refers to the summons by some people who are in dispute with them—such as one litigant summoning another to the judgment of Allah and His Messenger. This is indicated by the use of the word "when" (إِذا). If it meant the summons of Allah and His Messenger in the sense of the obligation for believers to refer their disputes to them, that would be a perpetual ruling that does not require temporal qualification.

From this, the weakness of the view that the omitted subject of "they are summoned" (دُعُوا) is Allah and His Messenger—meaning "when Allah and His Messenger summon them"—becomes apparent. Yes, the ultimate reference of the summons is to the summons of Allah and His Messenger.

In any case, the verse restricts the response of the believers, upon being summoned to the judgment of Allah and His Messenger, to their saying: "We hear and we obey." This is hearing and obedience to the divine summons, whether the summoner is one of the litigants or Allah and His Messenger, or if it refers to hearing and obeying the judgment of Allah and His Messenger itself, though the latter is less likely.

The restriction of the believers' response to "We hear and we obey" implies that rejecting the summons is not the response of believers; rather, it is a transgression beyond the bounds of faith, as conveyed by His saying: "Rather, it is they who are the wrongdoers," as previously explained. Thus, the verse serves as a justification for the transition at the end of the previous verse.

The verse concludes with His saying: «وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ» — and it is they who are the felicitous. This restricts felicity (الفلاح) to them, not them to felicity.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَخْشَ اللهَ وَيَتَّقْهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ» — Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, and fears Allah and is wary of Him—it is they who will be the triumphant. (24:52). The appearance of this verse in the context of the preceding verses, and its connection to the one before it, indicates that it serves as a justification—like a universal premise (الكبرى الكلية)—for the previous verse, which judged the felicity of those who respond to the summons of Allah and His Messenger with hearing and obedience, conditioned by faith. It is as if it were said: Only he who responds to the judgment of Allah and His Messenger while being a believer has attained felicity, because he is obedient to Allah and His Messenger, truly believing in his heart through the fear (خشية) of Allah and in his outward conduct through godwariness (تقوى) of Him. And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger in what is decreed for him, and fears Allah and is wary of Him, it is they who are the triumphant (الفائزون). Triumph (الفوز) is felicity (الفلاح).

The verse includes the one who summons to the judgment of Allah and His Messenger among the litigants, just as it includes the one summoned if he responds with hearing and obedience. Thus, in addition to justifying the ruling of the previous verse, it generalizes the good promise to both the summoner and the summoned.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لَئِنْ أَمَرْتَهُمْ لَيَخْرُجُنَّ قُلْ لا تُقْسِمُوا طاعَةٌ مَعْرُوفَةٌ» — They swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths that if you command them they will surely go forth. Say, ‘Do not swear! [What is required is] a recognized obedience.’ (24:53) to the end of the verse. Effort (الجهد) is capacity. The estimation in "They swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths" (أَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ) is: they swore by Allah the utmost of their effort in their oaths, meaning they swore by their most emphatic oaths.

It is apparent that "they will surely go forth" (لَيَخْرُجُنَّ) refers to going forth for Jihad (الجهاد) — the striving in the way of Allah — as occurs in several verses, such as His saying: «وَلَوْ أَرادُوا الْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا لَهُ عُدَّةً وَلكِنْ كَرِهَ اللهُ انْبِعاثَهُمْ فَثَبَّطَهُمْ وَقِيلَ اقْعُدُوا مَعَ الْقاعِدِينَ لَوْ خَرَجُوا فِيكُمْ ما زادُوكُمْ إِلَّا خَبالاً» — Had they desired to go forth, they would have made some preparation for it.
