But Allah was averse to their being dispatched, so He held them back, and it was said, ‘Sit with those who sit.’ Had they gone forth among you, they would have only added to your confusion (9:46-47).

His saying: «قُلْ لا تُقْسِمُوا» — Say, ‘Do not swear!’ — is a prohibition against swearing. His saying: «طاعَةٌ مَعْرُوفَةٌ» — a recognized obedience — is the predicate of an omitted subject, which is the pronoun referring to "going forth." The sentence serves as a justification for the prohibition of swearing, which is why it is presented as a separate clause. His saying: «إِنَّ اللهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ» — Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do — is part of the justification.

The meaning of the verse is: They swore by Allah with their most emphatic oaths that if you command them to go forth for Jihad, they will surely go forth. Say to them: Do not swear, for going forth for Jihad is a recognized obedience in the religion—it is an obligation that does not need to be made mandatory by an emphatic oath. If you are swearing to please Allah and His Messenger thereby, then Allah is well aware of what you do; He is not deceived by the emphasis of your oaths.

It has been said that "going forth" refers to their leaving their homes and wealth if the Messenger were to judge so. In this view, "a recognized obedience" is a subject with an omitted predicate, the estimation being: "A recognized obedience to the Prophet is better than your swearing." The meaning of the verse would be: They swore by Allah with the most emphatic oaths that if you command them and judge against them in their disputes to leave their homes and wealth, they will surely leave them. Say to them: Do not swear, for a good obedience from you to the Prophet is better than your swearing by Allah, and Allah is well aware of what you do.

The critique of this is that while this meaning emphasizes the connection of the verse to what preceded it—unlike the previous interpretation—it does not align with the earlier explicit mention of their rejection of the summons to Allah and His Messenger for judgment. Since they had turned away and were averse to the judgment of Allah and His Messenger, it would not be possible for them to swear to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that if he commanded them in his judgment to leave their homes and wealth, they would surely do so; this is obvious. Unless, of course, those who swore were a different group from those who rejected the summons and were averse to the judgment, in which case there would be no evidence to support interpreting "they will surely go forth" in this manner.

His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما عَلَيْهِ ما حُمِّلَ وَعَلَيْكُمْ ما حُمِّلْتُمْ» — Say, ‘Obey Allah and obey the Apostle.’ But if you turn away, then he is only responsible for what he has been charged with and you are responsible for what you have been charged with. (24:54) to the end of the verse. This is a command to obey Allah in what He has revealed of the religion, and a command to obey the Messenger in what he brings to them from their Lord and what he commands them regarding their religious and worldly affairs. Beginning the discourse with "Say" (قُلْ) indicates that all obedience is ultimately to Allah. He emphasized this by saying "and obey the Apostle" rather than "and obey me," because obedience to the Messenger, as a Messenger, is obedience to the One who sent him; thus, the proof is established.

Therefore, He followed the discourse:

First, with His saying: «فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما عَلَيْهِ ما حُمِّلَ وَعَلَيْكُمْ ما حُمِّلْتُمْ» — But if you turn away, then he is only responsible for what he has been charged with and you are responsible for what you have been charged with. That is, if you turn away and avert yourselves from obeying the Messenger, it does not harm the Messenger, for he is only responsible for the duty he was charged with, and nothing of your burden touches him; and you are responsible for the duty you were charged with, and nothing of it touches him, for all obedience is to Allah, the Glorified.

Second, with His saying: «وَإِنْ تُطِيعُوهُ تَهْتَدُوا» — and if you obey him, you will be guided. That is, although each of you and he has what they were charged with, if you obey the Messenger, you will be guided, because what he brings to you and what he commands you is from Allah and by His command, and obedience to him is obedience to Allah, in which there is guidance.

Third, with His saying: «وَما عَلَى الرَّسُولِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ» — The Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. This is in the position of a justification for what preceded it; meaning that the duty the Messenger was charged with is only communication (التبليغ). Thus, there is no blame upon him if you oppose what he communicated. Since he is a Messenger, he only bears the responsibility of communication; therefore, obedience to him is obedience to the One who sent him, and in the obedience to the One who sent him—who is Allah, the Glorified—is your guidance.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ» — Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors (24:55) to the end of the verse.

The apparent placement of the verse suggests it was revealed as a conclusion to the previous verses of the Surah. It is a Medinan verse and was not revealed in Mecca before the Hijra (الهجرة) — the migration of the Prophet from Mecca to Medina — as supported by its context, especially its conclusion.

The verse, therefore, is a beautiful promise to those who believe and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات) that Allah, the Exalted, will establish for them a righteous society (مجتمع صالح) exclusive to them. He will make them successors in the earth, empower their religion for them, and replace their fear with security, such that they will not fear the plot of a hypocrite nor the opposition of a disbeliever, worshipping Him and not associating anything with Him.

So, His saying: «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ» — Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds — the word "of" (مِنْ) here is partitive (تبعيضية), not explanatory. The address is to the general body of Muslims, among whom are hypocrites and believers, and among the believers are those who perform righteous deeds and those who do not. The promise is specific to those among them who purely believe and perform righteous deeds.

His saying: «لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ» — that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors. If the intended meaning of succession (الاستخلاف) is the granting of divine vicegerency (الخلافة الإلهية), as mentioned regarding Adam, David, and Solomon (عليه‌السلام) — where the Exalted said: «إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً» — ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth’ (2:30), and said: «يا داوُدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ» — ‘O David! Indeed We have made you a vicegerent on the earth’ (38:26), and said: «وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمانُ داوُدَ» — And Solomon inherited from David (27:16) — then the "those before them" would refer to the vicegerents of Allah among His prophets and saints. However, this is not without its difficulties, as will be discussed.

If the intended meaning is the inheriting of the earth and the granting of authority to one people over another, as He said: «إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ» — ‘The earth indeed belongs to Allah, and He gives its inheritance to whomever He wishes of His servants, and the outcome will be in favour of the God-conscious’ (7:128), and said: «أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ» — ‘My righteous servants shall inherit the earth’ (21:105), then "those before them" refers to the believers from the nations of past prophets whom Allah saved while destroying the disbelievers and the transgressors among them—such as the people of Noah, Hud, Salih, and Shu'ayb. This is as He informed of their gathering in His saying: «وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِرُسُلِهِمْ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا فَأَوْحى إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ وَلَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مِنْ بعدهم ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خافَ مَقامِي وَخافَ وَعِيدِ» — The faithless said to their apostles, ‘We will surely expel you from our land, or you shall revert to our creed.’ Then their Lord revealed to them: ‘We will surely destroy the wrongdoers, and We will surely settle you in the land after them. This is for him who fears standing before Me and fears My threat’ (14:13-14). These are the ones who were sincere to Allah, so He saved them, and they formed a righteous society and lived in it until time grew long for them and their hearts hardened.

As for the view of those who say that "those who were made successors before them" refers to the Children of Israel (بنو إسرائيل) when Allah destroyed Pharaoh and his hosts and made them inherit the land of Egypt and the Levant and empowered them therein, as the Exalted said concerning them: «وَنُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوارِثِينَ وَنُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ» — And We desired to show favour to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs, and to establish them in the land (28:5-6).

The critique of this is that the Israelite society formed after their rescue from Pharaoh and his hosts was never free from disbelief, hypocrisy, and transgression, nor was it purely for those who believed and performed righteous deeds, even for a moment, as the Quran explicitly states in many verses. There is no basis for likening the succession of those who believe and perform righteous deeds to their succession, which included the disbeliever, the hypocrite, the wicked, and the righteous.

Furthermore, if the intention were to liken the essence of their succession to the essence of the succession of those before them—namely the Children of Israel—regardless of its nature, there would be no need to single out the Israelite society for comparison. At the time of the verse's revelation and before it, there were nations stronger and more numerous than them, such as the Romans, the Persians, the Chaldeans, and others. The Exalted said regarding the first 'Ad and Thamud: «إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفاءَ مِنْ بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ» — when He made you successors after the people of Noah (7:69), and said: «إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفاءَ مِنْ بَعْدِ عادٍ» — when He made you successors after ‘Ad (7:74). He even addressed the disbelievers of this Ummah (الأمة) — the global community of Muslims — saying: «وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ الْأَرْضِ» — It is He who has made you successors on the earth (6:165), and said: «هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ» — It is He who made you successors on the earth. So whoever is faithless, his faithlessness will be to his own detriment (35:39).

If you say: Why is it not possible that the comparison is to the Children of Israel, and then the specific character of this righteous society is fulfilled by what follows in His saying: "and He will surely establish for them their religion" until the end of the promise?

I say: Yes, but in that case, there would be no reason to restrict the succession of the Children of Israel as the point of comparison, nor for "those before them" to refer only to the Children of Israel, as previously mentioned.

His saying: «وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى لَهُمْ» — and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them. To empower (تمكين) something is to settle it in a place; it is a metaphor for the stability of a thing without removal, agitation, or shaking, such that it produces its effect without hindrance or barrier. The empowerment of religion means it is practiced in society without disbelief in it or disdain for its commands, and its fundamental knowledge is embraced without disagreement or dispute. Allah, the Glorified, has judged in several places in His speech that disagreement in religion stems from the transgression of the dissenters, as in His saying: «وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ» — and none differed about it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves (2:213).

The "religion which He has approved for them" refers to the religion of Islam (الإسلام). The religion is attributed to them as an honor for them and because it is a requirement of their primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being.

His saying: «وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْناً» — and He will surely give them security in exchange for their fear. This is like His saying "and He will surely establish for them," conjoined to His saying "that He will surely make them successors." The core meaning is: He will surely exchange their fear for security. The attribution of the exchange to them is either a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي) or involves an omitted possessive indicated by His saying "after their fear," the estimation being: "He will surely exchange their fear," or that "security" (أَمْناً) means "secure" (آمين).

In any case, "fear" (الخوف) refers to what the believers endured in the early days of Islam from the disbelievers and the hypocrites.

His saying: «يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً» — They will worship Me without ascribing any partners to Me. It is most consistent with the context that this is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the pronoun in "and He will surely give them," meaning: He will surely exchange their fear for security while they worship Me, not associating anything with Me.

The shift (الالتفات) in the discourse from the third person to the first person, the emphasis of "they worship Me" with "without ascribing any partners to Me," and the use of the indefinite "anything" (شيئاً) in the context of negation—which indicates the absolute negation of polytheism (الشرك) — the act of associating partners with Allah — all necessitate that the intended meaning is their worship of Allah with pure sincerity (إخلاص), untainted by any manifest or hidden polytheism. In short, Allah will replace their society with a secure society in which none but Allah is worshipped and no lord other than Him is taken.

His saying: «وَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ» — Whoever is faithless after that—it is they who are the transgressors. The apparent context suggests that "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to the promised state. Accordingly, it is most appropriate that "disbelieved" (كَفَرَ) refers to ingratitude (كفران) as opposed to gratitude (شكر). The meaning is: whoever is ungrateful and does not thank Allah after the fulfillment of this promise—through disbelief, hypocrisy, or other destructive sins—it is they who are the transgressors (الفاسقون), complete in their transgression, which is the departure from the state of servitude.

Disagreement among the commentators has intensified regarding this verse.

It has been said that it was revealed concerning the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and that Allah fulfilled His promise to them by making them successors in the earth, empowering their religion, and exchanging their fear for security through the strength He gave to Islam after the Prophet's passing during the days of the Rightly Guided Caliphs (الخلفاء الراشدين). The intended "succession" would be the succession of the four caliphs after the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or the first three of them. The attribution of succession to all of them, while it was specific to some—the four or the three—is like attributing the matter of a part to the whole, as when it is said "the tribe of so-and-so killed," when only some of them killed.

It has also been said that it is general for the Ummah of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The intended meaning of their succession, the empowerment of their religion, and the exchange of their fear for security is their inheriting the earth as Allah made the nations before them inherit it, or the succession of the caliphs after the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) according to various reports, the empowerment of Islam, and the defeat of the enemies of religion. Allah fulfilled His promise by granting victory to Islam and the Muslims after the passing of the Prophet, as they conquered cities and subdued regions.

According to both views, the verse is among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Quran, as it informed of a matter before the time of its realization, when it was not expected at that time.

It has also been said that it concerns the promised Mahdi (عليه‌السلام), regarding whom narrations have reached the level of mass-transmission (تواترت) stating that he will appear and fill the earth with justice and equity as it was filled with oppression and wrongdoing, and that "those who believe and perform righteous deeds" refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams from his household (عليه‌السلام).

What the context of the noble verse provides—based on the preceding investigation while avoiding the laxities that commentators might commit in interpreting the verses—is that the promise is for a part of the Ummah, not all of it, nor for specific individuals among them. They are those who believe and perform righteous deeds. The verse is explicit in this, and there is no textual or rational evidence indicating that they are specifically the companions, or the Prophet and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم الصلاة والسلام), nor that "those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds" refers to the entire Ummah. Diverting the promise to a specific group among them is either to honor them or due to increased concern for them; all of this is arbitrary without justification.

The intended meaning of their succession in the earth, as He made those before them successors, is the formation of a believing, righteous society among them that inherits the earth as the past nations of power and might inherited it. This succession is established through their righteous society without being specific to certain individuals among them, just as it was for those before them. As for the intention of divine vicegerency in the sense of authority (الولاية) over society—as was the case for David, Solomon, and Joseph (عليه‌السلام), which is divine sovereignty—it is unlikely that He would refer to His noble prophets with the phrase "those before them" (الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ). This phrase or its equivalent occurs in more than fifty places in His speech, and in none of them are the past prophets intended, despite their frequent mention in the Quran. Rather, Allah mentions them with the phrase "messengers before you" (رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ) or "messengers before me" (رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِي) or similar expressions in relation to the pronoun referring to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The intended meaning of the empowerment of their religion which He approved for them, as mentioned, is the stability of the religion on its own foundation, such that it is not shaken by their disagreement over its principles, nor by their laxity in implementing its rulings and practicing its branches, and the society's freedom from the stain of hypocrisy.

The intended meaning of exchanging their fear for security is the spread of security and peace over their society, such that they do not fear an enemy within their society or outside it, whether manifest or hidden, regarding their religion or their worldly life.

The view of some that it refers to fear of an external enemy—as the Muslims feared the disbelievers and polytheists who sought to extinguish the light of Allah and invalidate the call—is an arbitrary claim refuted by the absolute nature of the term without a specific indicator. Furthermore, the verse is in the context of bestowing favor (الامتنان), and what favor is there for a people who have no external enemy but whose society is surrounded by corruption and general calamity, with no security for life, honor, or property, where freedom belongs to the ruling power and precedence to the transgressing group?

The intended meaning of their worshipping Allah without ascribing any partners to Him is what the reality of the term's meaning provides: the universality of sincere worship and the destruction of every foundation of honor except the honor of godwariness (التقوى).

The conclusion of all this is that Allah, the Glorified, promises those among them who believe and perform righteous deeds that He will establish for them a righteous society, pure from the stain of disbelief, hypocrisy, and transgression, which will inherit the earth. Nothing will govern the beliefs of its members or their actions except the true religion. They will live securely without fear of an enemy, internal or external, free from the plots of the plotters, the oppression of the oppressors, and the dictates of the dictators.

This good and pure society, with its attributes of virtue and sanctity, has not been realized nor formed since the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was sent until this day of ours. If it is to be applied, it must apply to the time of the appearance of the Mahdi (عليه‌السلام), according to what has been narrated of his description in the mass-transmitted reports from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), but on the condition that the address is to the righteous society, not to him (عليه‌السلام) alone.

If you say: What is the meaning of the promise then for "those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds," when the Mahdi (عليه‌السلام) was not one of the addressees at the time of revelation, nor was any of the people of the time of his appearance among them?

I say: There is a confusion here between individual and social addresses—that is, the address directed to the individuals of a people as specific persons, and the address directed to them as a people characterized by such-and-such a description. The first does not extend beyond their persons, and what it contains of promise, threat, or otherwise does not apply to others. The second extends to everyone who possesses the description mentioned therein, and what it contains of ruling applies to them. The address of the verse is of the second type, as previously explained.

Most of the Quranic addresses directed to the believers and the disbelievers are of this type. This includes the addresses censuring the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), especially the Jews, for what their ancestors did, and the polytheists for what their fathers did.

Of this type specifically is the promise mentioned in the saying of the Exalted: «فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ لِيَسُوؤُا وُجُوهَكُمْ» — So when the second prediction came to pass, [We sent them] to sadden your faces (17:7), for those promised did not live to the time of the fulfillment of this promise. Similar is the promise mentioned in the speech of Dhu al-Qarnayn as narrated by Allah: «فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا» — ‘But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it to the ground, and the promise of my Lord is true’ (18:98). Likewise is His promise to the people of the coming of the Hour and the folding of the carpet of worldly life by the blowing of the Trumpet, as He said: «ثَقُلَتْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً» — It is heavy in the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but all of a sudden (7:187). Thus, promising the righteous among the believers, by virtue of their being righteous believers, with a promise that the individuals of the time of revelation do not personally witness, and when the individuals of the society that witnesses the fulfillment of the promise do not yet exist, is something that presents no problem whatsoever.

The truth is that if the verse is given its full meaning, it only applies to the promised society that will be formed with the appearance of the Mahdi (عليه‌السلام). However, if one is lax in interpreting its individual words and sentences, and the intended meaning of the succession of those who believe and perform righteous deeds is the succession of the Ummah through a kind of predominance and the like; and the empowerment of their religion which He approved for them is their being known in the world as the Muslim Ummah, with Islam as their religion, even if they split into seventy-three sects, each declaring the other disbelievers and sanctioning each other's blood, honor, and property; and the exchange of their fear for security while they worship Allah and do not associate anything with Him is the might and power of the Ummah in the world and its spread over most of the inhabited earth, and the outward performance of prayer, fasting, and pilgrimage, even if security has departed from among themselves and truth and reality have bid them farewell—then the view is that the one promised this promise is the Ummah. The intended meaning of their succession is the might and power Allah granted them after the Hijra and after the Prophet's passing. There is no reason to restrict that to the time of the Rightly Guided Caliphs; rather, it continues after that until the time of the decline of the Islamic Caliphate.

As for applying the verse to the caliphate of the Rightly Guided Caliphs, or the first three, or specifically Ali (عليه‌السلام), there is no way to do so at all.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ» — Maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and obey the Apostle so that you may receive [His] mercy. (24:56). The suitability of the verse's content to what the previous verses were set out to explain indicates that it is a completion of them.

His saying: «وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ» — Maintain the prayer and give the zakat — is in reality a command to obey Him, the Exalted, in what He has legislated for His servants. The specific mention of prayer (الصلاة) and zakat (الزكاة) — the prescribed alms-tax — is because they are the two pillars of the obligations pertaining to Allah, the Exalted, and to the creation. His saying: «وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ» — and obey the Apostle — is the implementation of his authority (ولايته) (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in judgment and governance.

His saying: «لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ» — so that you may receive [His] mercy — is a justification for the command based on the benefit within the commanded act. The meaning—as provided by the context—is: Obey Allah and obey the Messenger, for in these two obediences is the hope that divine mercy will encompass you, so He fulfills His promise to you or prepares you for its fulfillment. For the removal of hypocrisy from among the Muslims and the general righteousness and agreement upon the word of truth is the key to the formation of a righteous society that will pour forth every good upon them.

His saying, the Exalted: «لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَمَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ وَلَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ» — Do not consider the faithless as frustrating [Allah’s purpose] on the earth. Their abode will be the Fire, and it is surely an evil destination. (24:57). This is a completion of the previous verses, and in it is a confirmation of what has passed of the promise of succession in the earth, the empowerment of religion, and the exchange of fear for security.

The Exalted addresses His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) after the promise—with an emphatic address—not to think that the disbelievers can frustrate Allah on the earth, preventing Him with their power and might from fulfilling His promise. This is, in reality, a specific glad tiding for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding what He has honored his Ummah with, and that his enemies will be defeated and overcome. Therefore, He singled him out with the address by way of a shift (التفات).

Since the mentioned prohibition implies that the disbelievers will eventually cease their opposition to the religion and its people, He conjoined to it His saying: "Their abode will be the Fire," etc., as if it were said: They are vanquished in this world, and their dwelling is the Fire in the Hereafter, and it is surely an evil destination.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: «وَيَقُولُونَ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ» — And they say, ‘We believe in Allah’ — the verses: It was said: The verses were revealed concerning a man from the hypocrites—between whom and a Jewish man there was a legal dispute—so the Jew summoned him to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the hypocrite summoned him to Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf.

Al-Balkhi narrated that there was a dispute between Ali and Uthman—regarding land he bought from Ali in which stones appeared—and he wanted to return it due to the defect, but Ali would not take it back—so he said: "Between me and you is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)." Al-Hakam ibn Abi al-'As said: "If you take him to his cousin, he will judge for him, so do not take him to him." Thus, the verses were revealed. This is narrated from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) or something close to it.

I say: In the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani, narrated from al-Dahhak, the dispute was between Ali and al-Mughirah ibn Wa'il, and he mentioned a similar story.

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: «إِنَّما كانَ قَوْلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — All the response of the faithful — the verse: It is narrated from Abu Ja'far: That the one intended by the verse is the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the saying of the Exalted: «فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما عَلَيْهِ ما حُمِّلَ ـ وَعَلَيْكُمْ ما حُمِّلْتُمْ» — But if you turn away, then he is only responsible for what he has been charged with and you are responsible for what you have been charged with — the verse: Ibn Jarir, Ibn Qani', and al-Tabarani narrated from 'Alqamah ibn Wa'il al-Hadrami from Salamah ibn Yazid al-Juhani, who said: I said: "O Messenger of Allah, what if there are leaders over us after you who take from us the right due from us—and deny us the right that Allah has made for us? Should we fight them and hate them?" The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: عليهم ما حملوا وعليكم ما حملتم — "Upon them is what they were charged with, and upon you is what you were charged with."

I say: Some other narrations with this meaning are reported therein, but one should not doubt that Islam—with its spirit of reviving the truth and destroying falsehood—refuses to permit the authority of oppressors who are manifest in their wrongdoing, or to allow silence and the endurance of injustice and persecution in the face of tyrants and profligates for one who finds a way to rectify the matter. It has become clear through social studies today that the despotism of rulers in their opinions and their following of whims in their dictates is a greater danger and has a more foul effect than inciting turmoil and waging wars in the way of compelling them toward truth and justice.

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ» — Allah has promised those of you who have faith — the verse: There is disagreement regarding the verse—and it is narrated from the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that it is concerning the Mahdi from the progeny of Muhammad.

He said: Al-Ayyashi narrated with his chain from Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام): That he read the verse and said: "They are, by Allah, our followers (شيعتنا), the Ahl al-Bayt—He will do that for them at the hands of a man from us, and he is the Mahdi of this Ummah, and he is the one about whom the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: لو لم يبق من الدنيا إلا يوم لطول الله ذلك اليوم حتى يأتي رجل من عترتي اسمه اسمي ـ يملأ الأرض عدلا وقسطا كما ملئت ظلما وجورا — 'If there remained of the world but a single day, Allah would prolong that day until a man from my progeny comes, whose name is my name—he will fill the earth with justice and equity as it was filled with oppression and wrongdoing.'" Similar to this is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (عليه‌السلام).

I say: With this, the reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) have arrived, and the explanation of the verse's application to that has preceded.

He said in al-Majma', after quoting the narration: "Based on this, the intended meaning of 'those who believe and perform righteous deeds' is the Prophet and his household (عليهم الصلاة والسلام)." End quote. You have already seen that the intended meaning is general, and the narration does not indicate more than that, as he (عليه‌السلام) said: "They are, by Allah, our followers, the Ahl al-Bayt—He will do that for them at the hands of a man from us..." the hadith.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Bara' regarding His saying: «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ» — Allah has promised those of you who have faith — the verse—he said: "It was revealed concerning us when we were in intense fear."

I say: Its apparent meaning is that "those who believe" refers to the companions, but you have seen that the verse contains no indication of this at all; rather, the indication is to the contrary.

In it, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani in al-Awsat, al-Hakim—who authenticated it—Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il, and al-Diya' in al-Mukhtarah narrated from Ubayy ibn Ka'b, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his companions arrived in Medina and the Ansar (الأنصار) — the helpers who welcomed the Prophet to Medina — gave them refuge, the Arabs shot at them from a single bow—so they would not spend the night except in arms, nor would they reach the morning except in them—so they said: 'Do you think we will live until we spend the night secure and tranquil—fearing none but Allah?' Then was revealed: «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ» — Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds — the verse."

I say: This does not indicate more than the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول). As for who is intended by "those who believe," and when Allah fulfilled or will fulfill this promise, it does not address that.

Similar is his other narration: When «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ» — Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds — was revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he said: "Give glad tidings to this Ummah of brilliance, loftiness, religion, victory, and empowerment in the earth—so whoever among you performs the work of the Hereafter for the sake of the world—he shall have no portion in the Hereafter."

For giving glad tidings to the Ummah of succession does not necessitate that the intended meaning of "those who believe" in the verse is the entire Ummah, or specifically the companions, or a numbered few among them.

In Nahj al-Balaghah, in a speech of his to Umar when he consulted him—regarding his setting out to fight the people of Persia when they gathered for war—he (عليه‌السلام) said: "This matter's victory or defeat was never by large or small numbers. It is the religion of Allah which He has made manifest, and His army which He has honored and supported—until it reached where it reached and rose where it rose. And we are upon a promise from Allah, the Exalted, where He said, exalted is His Name: وعد الله الذين آمنوا منكم وعملوا الصالحات ـ ليستخلفنهم في الأرض ـ وليمكنن لهم دينهم الذي ارتضى لهم ـ وليبدلنهم من بعد خوفهم أمنا — Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth... and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and He will surely give them security in exchange for their fear.

And Allah, the Exalted, is the fulfiller of His promise and the helper of His army. The position of the leader in Islam is like the position of the string for the beads—if the string breaks, they scatter, and many a scattered thing never regathers. The Arabs today, though they are few—they are many through Islam and honored through unity—so be the axis and turn the millstone with the Arabs, and let them face the fire of war without you—for if you depart from this land—the Arabs will break away from you from its edges and regions—until what you leave behind of vulnerabilities—will be more important to you than what is before you. And the time has come for the non-Arabs to look at you tomorrow and say: 'This is the root of the Arabs; if you cut it off, you will be at rest'—and that will be more intense for their ferocity against you and their greed for you.

As for what you mentioned of their numbers—we did not fight in the past with large numbers—we only fought with victory and aid."

I say: This has been used as evidence in Ruh al-Ma'ani for what he approved of—that the intended meaning of succession in the verse is the appearance of Islam and the rising of its status in the time of the Rightly Guided Caliphs. But it is far from that; rather, it is evidence to the contrary. For the apparent meaning of his speech is that the divine promise had not yet completed its fulfillment, and that they were on its path at that time, as he says: "And Allah is the fulfiller of His promise," and that the religion had not yet been empowered nor the fear exchanged for security. And how could it be otherwise? For they were between two fears: fear of the Arabs breaking away from within and fear of the enemies attacking from without.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu al-Sha'tha', who said: I was sitting with Hudhayfah and Ibn Mas'ud—so Hudhayfah said: "Hypocrisy has gone—hypocrisy was only during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and today it is only disbelief after faith." Ibn Mas'ud laughed and then said: "Why do you say that?" He said: "Because of this verse: «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ» — Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds — to the end of the verse."

I say: I wonder, where did the hypocrites of the Prophet's time go? The evidence of the noble Book and history indicates that they were no less than a third of the people of Medina, and most of them were there. Did they become sincere in Islam on the day of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passing, or did their views change while they were waiting for misfortunes to befall and turning things upside down?

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِيَسْتَـْٔذِنكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَبْلُغُوا۟ ٱلْحُلُمَ مِنكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَ مَرَّٰتٍ ۚ مِّن قَبْلِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْفَجْرِ وَحِينَ تَضَعُونَ ثِيَابَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظَّهِيرَةِ وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْعِشَآءِ ۚ ثَلَـٰثُ عَوْرَٰتٍ لَّكُمْ ۚ لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا عَلَيْهِمْ جُنَاحٌۢ بَعْدَهُنَّ ۚ طَوَّٰفُونَ عَلَيْكُم بَعْضُكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٥٨)
O you who have faith! Your slaves and any of you who have not yet reached puberty should seek your permission three times: before the dawn prayer, and when you put off your garments at noon, and after the night prayer. These are three times of privacy for you. Apart from these, it is not sinful of you or them to frequent one another [freely]. Thus does Allah clarify the signs for you, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (58)
وَإِذَا بَلَغَ ٱلْأَطْفَـٰلُ مِنكُمُ ٱلْحُلُمَ فَلْيَسْتَـْٔذِنُوا۟ كَمَا ٱسْتَـْٔذَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٥٩)
When your children reach puberty, let them ask permission [at all times] just as those [who grew up] before them asked permission. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (59)
وَٱلْقَوَٰعِدُ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ٱلَّـٰتِى لَا يَرْجُونَ نِكَاحًا فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْهِنَّ جُنَاحٌ أَن يَضَعْنَ ثِيَابَهُنَّ غَيْرَ مُتَبَرِّجَـٰتٍۭ بِزِينَةٍ ۖ وَأَن يَسْتَعْفِفْنَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُنَّ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٦٠)
As for women advanced in years who do not expect to marry, there will be no sin upon them if they put off their cloaks, without displaying their adornment. But it is better for them to be modest, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (60)
لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بُيُوتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ ءَابَآئِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ إِخْوَٰنِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخَوَٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَعْمَـٰمِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ عَمَّـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخْوَٰلِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ خَـٰلَـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكْتُم مَّفَاتِحَهُۥٓ أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ ۚ لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ جَمِيعًا أَوْ أَشْتَاتًا ۚ فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُم بُيُوتًا فَسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُبَـٰرَكَةً طَيِّبَةً ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٦١)
There is no blame upon the blind, nor any blame upon the lame, nor any blame upon the sick, nor upon yourselves if you eat from your own houses, or your fathers’ houses, or your mothers’ houses, or your brothers’ houses, or your sisters’ houses, or the houses of your paternal uncles, or the houses of your paternal aunts, or the houses of your maternal uncles, or the houses of your maternal aunts, or those whose keys are in your possession, or those of your friends. There will be no blame on you whether you eat together or separately. So when you enter houses, greet yourselves with a salutation from Allah, blessed and good. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may exercise your reason. (61)
إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَإِذَا كَانُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرٍ جَامِعٍ لَّمْ يَذْهَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَسْتَـْٔذِنُوهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَـْٔذِنُونَكَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ فَإِذَا ٱسْتَـْٔذَنُوكَ لِبَعْضِ شَأْنِهِمْ فَأْذَن لِّمَن شِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمُ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٦٢)
Indeed the faithful are those who have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and when they are with him in a collective affair, they do not leave until they have sought his permission. Indeed those who seek your permission are those who have faith in Allah and His Apostle. So when they seek your permission for some of theirs [private] work, give permission to whomever of them you wish and plead with Allah to forgive them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (62)
لَّا تَجْعَلُوا۟ دُعَآءَ ٱلرَّسُولِ بَيْنَكُمْ كَدُعَآءِ بَعْضِكُم بَعْضًا ۚ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَسَلَّلُونَ مِنكُمْ لِوَاذًا ۚ فَلْيَحْذَرِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُخَالِفُونَ عَنْ أَمْرِهِۦٓ أَن تُصِيبَهُمْ فِتْنَةٌ أَوْ يُصِيبَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٦٣)
Do not consider the Apostle’s summons amongst you to be like your summoning one another. Allah certainly knows those of you who slip away shielding one another. Those who disobey his orders should beware lest an affliction should visit them or a painful punishment should befall them. (63)
أَلَآ إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ مَآ أَنتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَيَوْمَ يُرْجَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ (٦٤)
Behold! To Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. He certainly knows your present state. The day they are brought back to Him He will inform them about what they have done, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (64)

(Commentary)

The remaining rulings mentioned in the Surah are presented here, and the Surah concludes with the final verses, which contain an indication that Allah, the Glorified, only legislates what He legislates based on His knowledge (العلم), and the reality of these matters will be manifested and revealed to them when they return to Him.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِيَسْتَأْذِنْكُمُ الَّذِينَ مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ — "O you who have faith! Let those whom your right hands possess... (24:58)" to the end of the verse. "Putting aside the clothes" (وضع الثياب) refers to taking them off, which is an allusion (كناية) to their being in a state in which they might not wish to be seen by an outsider. "Noontime" (الظهيرة) is the time of the midday heat. "Privacy" (العورة) — a state of vulnerability or nakedness — is literally the "shameful part" (السوأة), so named because of the shame (العار) that attaches to a person when it is exposed; what is intended by it in the verse is that which ought to be covered.

Thus, His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith!) etc., is a follow-up to His previous saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَدْخُلُوا (O you who have faith! Do not enter...) etc., which stipulated that entering a house is contingent upon permission. This verse acts as an exception to that general rule (العموم) regarding slaves and children, in that it suffices for them to seek permission three times a day.

His saying: لِيَسْتَأْذِنْكُمُ الَّذِينَ مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ (Let those whom your right hands possess seek your permission) means: command them to seek your permission to enter. The apparent meaning of "those whom your right hands possess" refers to male slaves rather than female slaves, although the wording does not preclude generality (العموم) by way of predominance (التغليب), and the narrations have been transmitted to this effect, as will follow.

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَبْلُغُوا الْحُلُمَ مِنْكُمْ (and those of you who have not reached puberty) refers to children who have reached the age of discernment (المميزين) before puberty. The evidence for restricting this to those with discernment is His subsequent saying: ثَلاثُ عَوْراتٍ لَكُمْ (three times of privacy for you).

His saying: ثَلاثَ مَرَّاتٍ (three times) means every day, as evidenced by His detailed explanation: مِنْ قَبْلِ صَلاةِ الْفَجْرِ وَحِينَ تَضَعُونَ ثِيابَكُمْ مِنَ الظَّهِيرَةِ (before the dawn prayer, and when you put off your garments at noon) — meaning the time of the midday heat — وَمِنْ بَعْدِ صَلاةِ الْعِشاءِ (and after the night prayer). He has indicated the reason for the ruling by His saying: ثَلاثُ عَوْراتٍ لَكُمْ (three times of privacy for you), meaning these three times are three periods of vulnerability for you, during which it is naturally inappropriate for others to see you.

His saying: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلا عَلَيْهِمْ جُناحٌ بَعْدَهُنَ (There is no sin on you or on them besides these [times]) means there is no hindrance for you in not commanding them to seek permission, nor for them in not seeking permission, outside of these times. He indicated the reason for negating the sin by His saying: طَوَّافُونَ عَلَيْكُمْ بَعْضُكُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ (they go the rounds among you, some of you [serving] others), meaning they frequently move about you, some of you attending to others for service; thus, seeking permission every time they enter would normally cause hardship (حرج), so they should limit it to the three times of privacy.

Then He said: كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمُ الْآياتِ (Thus does Allah clarify the signs for you) — meaning the rulings of His religion which are signs (الآيات) pointing toward Him — وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ (and Allah is All-knowing), knowing your states and what they require of rulings, حَكِيمٌ (All-wise), observing your best interests in His decrees.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا بَلَغَ الْأَطْفالُ مِنْكُمُ الْحُلُمَ فَلْيَسْتَأْذِنُوا — "When the children among you reach puberty, let them seek permission... (24:59)" etc. This is a clarification that the ruling of seeking permission three times for children is limited by the attainment of puberty. Once the children among you reach puberty (الحلم), they must seek permission just as those before them — the adult free men and women — sought permission. كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمْ آياتِهِ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you, and Allah is All-knowing, All-wise).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْقَواعِدُ مِنَ النِّساءِ اللَّاتِي لا يَرْجُونَ نِكاحاً — "As for women past childbearing, who have no hope of marriage... (24:60)" to the end of the verse. "The past-childbearing" (القواعد) is the plural of qa'idah (قاعدة), referring to a woman who has "sat back" (قعدت) from marriage and no longer hopes for it due to a lack of desire for intimacy with her because of her advanced age. Thus, His saying: اللَّاتِي لا يَرْجُونَ نِكاحاً (who have no hope of marriage) is an explanatory description (وصف توضيحي). It has also been said that it refers to those who have reached menopause, making the description restrictive (احترازي).

In al-Majma': "Displaying charms" (التبرج) is for a woman to manifest those beauties which she is obligated to cover. Its root is "appearance" (الظهور), from which comes "tower" (البرج) — a high building — because of its prominence.

The verse is effectively an exception to the general ruling of the veil (الحجاب). The meaning is: as for elderly, aged women, there is no harm if they do not veil themselves, provided they do not display their finery.

His saying: وَأَنْ يَسْتَعْفِفْنَ خَيْرٌ لَهُنَ (and that they should be modest is better for them) is an allusion to veiling; that is, veiling is better for them than putting aside their outer garments. His saying: وَاللهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing) is a justification for what He has legislated through these two Names; meaning He, the Exalted, is All-hearing, hearing what they ask by their primordial nature (الفطرة), and All-knowing, knowing the rulings they require.

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَى الْأَعْمى حَرَجٌ وَلا عَلَى الْأعرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلا عَلَى الْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ وَلا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ — "There is no blame on the blind, nor is there any blame on the lame, nor is there any blame on the sick, nor on yourselves if you eat from your own houses... (24:61)" until His saying: أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ (or your friend). The apparent meaning of the verse is that it establishes a right for the believers to eat from the houses of their relatives, or those they have been entrusted with, or the houses of their friends. They are permitted to eat from them according to their need, without extravagance or causing waste.

Thus, His saying: لَيْسَ عَلَى الْأَعْمى حَرَجٌ (There is no blame on the blind) until His saying: وَلا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ (nor on yourselves) — the conjunction of "on yourselves" with what preceded it indicates that the mention of those listed is not because the right is exclusive to them, but because they are people with disabilities (أرباب عاهات) for whom it is sometimes difficult to earn a living through their own labor. Otherwise, there is no difference between the blind, the lame, the sick, and others in this regard.

His saying: مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ آبائِكُمْ (from your own houses or the houses of your fathers) etc. — the inclusion of "your own houses" along with the houses of relatives and others is an indication of the negation of any difference in this religion — which is built upon the believers being guardians (أولياء) of one another — between their own houses, the houses of their relatives, those whose keys they possess, and the houses of their friends.

Furthermore, "your own houses" (بيوتكم) includes the house of the son and the spouse, as mentioned in the narrations. His saying: أَوْ ما مَلَكْتُمْ مَفاتِحَهُ (or those whose keys you possess) — "keys" (المفاتح) is the plural of miftah (مفتح), meaning a storehouse. The meaning is: or the house whose storehouses containing sustenance you possess — meaning you have authority over them — such as a man being a steward of a house, an agent, or one to whom the key has been handed.

His saying: أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ (or your friend) is conjoined to what preceded it, with "house" (بيت) being implied as understood from the context; the intended meaning is "or the house of your friend."

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا جَمِيعاً أَوْ أَشْتاتاً — "There is no blame on you whether you eat together or separately (24:61)." "Separately" (أشتاتاً) is the plural of shatt (شت), which is a verbal noun meaning "separation," used here to mean "separated" for emphasis, or as an adjective meaning "scattered." The meaning is: there is no sin upon you whether you eat as a group, some of you with others, or individually. The verse is general (عامة) even if its revelation was due to a specific cause, as has been narrated.

The commentators have intense disagreements regarding this section of the verse and the section before it. We have deemed it better to refrain from citing them or delving into their investigation; the meaning we have provided for both sections is what their context (السياق) yields.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمْ بُيُوتاً فَسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُبارَكَةً طَيِّبَةً — "And when you enter houses, greet yourselves with a greeting from Allah, blessed and good... (24:61)" etc. Since the houses were mentioned previously, He followed it by mentioning the etiquette of entering them, saying: "And when you enter houses."

His saying: فَسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ (greet yourselves) — what is intended is to greet those of its inhabitants who are within. He substituted "yourselves" (أنفسكم) to indicate that they are from one another, for all are human, and Allah created them from a male and a female, and moreover, they are believers, and faith (الإيمان) gathers and unites them more strongly than kinship or anything else.

It is not far-fetched that the meaning of "greet yourselves" is for the one entering to greet the people of the house, and for them to return the greeting to him.

His saying: تَحِيَّةً مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُبارَكَةً طَيِّبَةً (a greeting from Allah, blessed and good) means that the greeting is a salutation from Allah, which He has legislated and whose ruling He has sent down for the Muslims to greet one another with. It is "blessed" (مباركة) — possessing much enduring good — and "good" (طيبة) — pleasing to the soul — for the reality of this greeting is the spreading of security and safety upon the one greeted, which is the finest thing shared between two people meeting.

Then the Glorified concluded the verse by saying: كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمُ الْآياتِ (Thus does Allah clarify the signs for you) — the explanation of which has already passed — لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (so that you may exercise your reason) — meaning, so that you may learn the landmarks of your religion and act upon them, as has been said.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَإِذا كانُوا مَعَهُ عَلى أَمْرٍ جامِعٍ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا حَتَّى يَسْتَأْذِنُوهُ — "The faithful are only those who have faith in Allah and His Apostle and when they are with him on a collective matter, they do not leave until they have sought his permission... (24:62)." He mentioned "those who have faith in Allah and His Apostle" as a clarification of "the faithful" (المؤمنون), despite its apparent meaning, to indicate their embodiment of the reality of that meaning. That is, the faithful are only those who have believed in Allah and His Apostle with the reality of faith, attained certainty in His oneness, and whose souls are at peace and whose hearts are attached to His Apostle.

For this reason, He followed it with His saying: وَإِذا كانُوا مَعَهُ عَلى أَمْرٍ جامِعٍ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا حَتَّى يَسْتَأْذِنُوهُ (and when they are with him on a collective matter, they do not leave until they have sought his permission). A "collective matter" (الأمر الجامع) is that which gathers people to deliberate upon its aspects, consult, and resolve upon it, such as war and the like.

The meaning is: when they are with the Messenger, gathered with him for a matter of public concern, they do not go away or depart from the Messenger's presence until they have sought his permission to leave.

For this reason also, He followed it with His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَأْذِنُونَكَ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (Indeed those who seek your permission are the ones who believe in Allah and His Apostle), which is like the converse of the beginning of the verse, to indicate the necessary connection (الملازمة) and inseparability.

His saying: فَإِذَا اسْتَأْذَنُوكَ لِبَعْضِ شَأْنِهِمْ فَأْذَنْ لِمَنْ شِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ (So when they seek your permission for some work of theirs, give permission to whomever of them you wish) is a choice given by Allah, the Exalted, to His Messenger to permit whom he wishes and refuse whom he does not wish.

His saying: وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمُ اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (and plead with Allah for forgiveness for them; indeed Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful) is a command to him to seek forgiveness for them to comfort their souls and as a mercy toward them.

His saying, the Exalted: لا تَجْعَلُوا دُعاءَ الرَّسُولِ بَيْنَكُمْ كَدُعاءِ بَعْضِكُمْ بَعْضاً — "Do not consider the Apostle’s summons among you like the summons of one of you to another... (24:63)" to the end of the verse. The "Apostle's summons" (دعاء الرسول) is his calling people to a matter, such as calling them to faith and righteous deeds, calling them to consult with them on a collective matter, calling them to a congregational prayer, or commanding them regarding a matter of their worldly or otherworldly life. All of this constitutes a "summons" (دعاء) and a "call" (دعوة) from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

This meaning is supported by His saying at the end: قَدْ يَعْلَمُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ يَتَسَلَّلُونَ مِنْكُمْ لِواذاً (Allah certainly knows those of you who steal away, taking cover) and the subsequent threat to those who oppose his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) command, as is not hidden. This is more suitable to the context of the previous verse, for that verse praises those who respond to his call, attend his presence, and do not leave him until they seek his permission, while this verse censures and threatens those whom he calls but who steal away from him, taking cover, unconcerned with his summons and paying no heed.

From here, it becomes clear that what has been said — that the "Apostle's summons" refers to addressing him, such that he must be honored and not equated with other people, so one should not say to him "O Muhammad" or "O son of Abdullah," but rather "O Messenger of Allah" — is not well-founded.

Similarly, what has been said — that the "summons" refers to his supplication against them if they anger him, thus being a prohibition against exposing themselves to his prayer against them by provoking his anger, for Allah does not reject such a prayer — is also weak, because the end of the verse does not support either of these two views.

His saying: قَدْ يَعْلَمُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ يَتَسَلَّلُونَ مِنْكُمْ لِواذاً (Allah certainly knows those of you who steal away, taking cover). "Stealing away" (التسلل) is to exit from among others gently and with trickery, derived from drawing a sword from its scabbard. "Taking cover" (اللواذ) is to seek refuge and shelter in another to hide behind him. The meaning is: Allah knows those among you who exit from among the people while they are taking cover behind others and hiding behind them, thus departing without caring for the Apostle's summons or paying it any heed.

His saying: فَلْيَحْذَرِ الَّذِينَ يُخالِفُونَ عَنْ أَمْرِهِ أَنْ تُصِيبَهُمْ فِتْنَةٌ أَوْ يُصِيبَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ (So let those who oppose his command beware lest some ordeal should visit them or they should be visited by a painful punishment). The apparent context of the verse, based on the aforementioned meaning, is that the pronoun in "his command" (عن أمره) refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), which is his summons. Thus, the verse is a warning to those who oppose the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) command and his summons that an "ordeal" (فتنة) — meaning a calamity — might strike them, or a painful punishment might befall them.

It has been said that the pronoun in "his command" refers to Allah, the Glorified. Although no specific command from Allah, the Exalted, occurs in the verse, His prohibition mentioned in His saying: لا تَجْعَلُوا دُعاءَ الرَّسُولِ (Do not consider the Apostle’s summons...) etc., is in the sense of "respond to the Apostle's summons," which is a command. However, the first of the two views is more appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ — "Look! To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. He certainly knows what [state] you are in... (24:64)." This is a conclusion to the Surah, looking back to His saying at its opening: سُورَةٌ أَنْزَلْناها وَفَرَضْناها وَأَنْزَلْنا فِيها آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ (A surah which We have sent down and made obligatory, and We have sent down in it manifest signs...). What is at its conclusion is like a justification for what was at its opening.

Thus, His saying: أَلا إِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Look! To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth) is a clarification of the universality of ownership (الملك) and that everything is owned by Allah, the Glorified, and sustained by Him. Therefore, they are known to Him in all the particularities of their existence, and He knows what they require. Humans are among those whose true state and needs He knows; thus, the religion He legislates for them is what they need in their lives, just as the sustenance He provides for them is what they need for their survival.

So His saying: قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ (He certainly knows what [state] you are in) — meaning the reality of the state that indicates need — is like a conclusion resulting from the argument. That is, His ownership of you and of everything necessitates His knowledge of your state and what you require of the laws of religion, so He legislates them for you and makes them obligatory upon you.

His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُرْجَعُونَ إِلَيْهِ فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا وَاللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (and the day they are returned to Him, He will inform them of what they did. And Allah has knowledge of all things) is conjoined to His saying: ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ (what [state] you are in). That is, He also knows the day they are returned to Him — which is the Day of Resurrection — and He will inform them of the reality of what they did, and Allah is All-knowing of all things.

In this conclusion, there is an exhortation toward obedience and submission to the rulings He has legislated and made obligatory, and toward acting upon them, from the perspective that He will inform them of the reality of what they did. Just as in the beginning, there is an exhortation toward acceptance from the perspective that Allah only legislated them because of His knowledge of their need for them, and that they are what will fulfill their needs.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِيَسْتَأْذِنْكُمُ (O you who have faith! Let those... seek your permission), it is narrated that Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Abu Dawud, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan reported from Ibn Abbas that he said: "There is a verse that most people do not believe in: the verse of seeking permission. I indeed command this slave-girl of mine" — referring to a short slave-girl standing by his head — "to seek permission before entering upon me."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding the verse, he said: "Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, forbade anyone from entering upon anyone else during these three times — neither father, nor sister, nor mother, nor servant — except with permission. The times are: after the break of dawn, at midday, and after the final night prayer." Then He made it unrestricted after these three times, saying: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلا عَلَيْهِمْ جُناحٌ بَعْدَهُنَ (There is no sin on you or on them besides these [times]), meaning after these three times, طَوَّافُونَ عَلَيْكُمْ بَعْضُكُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ (they go the rounds among you, some of you [serving] others).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Zurarah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ (those whom your right hands possess), he said: "It is specific to men, excluding women." I said: "Do women seek permission during these three hours?" He said: "No, but they may enter and exit." Regarding وَالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَبْلُغُوا الْحُلُمَ مِنْكُمْ (and those of you who have not reached puberty), he said: "From among yourselves; they must seek permission — just as the permission of those who have reached puberty — during these three hours."

I say: Other narrations besides this have been reported from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them) stating that what is intended by "those whom your right hands possess" are males, excluding females.

In al-Majma', regarding the verse: "Its meaning is: command your male and female slaves to seek permission from you when they wish to enter your places of privacy — narrated from Ibn Abbas. It was also said: He intended male slaves specifically — narrated from Ibn Umar, and this is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them)."

I say: By these reports and the apparent meaning of the verse, the report narrated by al-Hakim from Ali (peace be upon him) regarding the verse is weakened, in which he said: "The women; for the men must seek permission."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Mardawayh reported from Ibn Umar that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Do not let the Bedouins overcome you regarding the name of your prayer, al-Isha (the night prayer); for it is indeed al-Isha in the Book of Allah, while they only call it al-Atamah (the darkness) because of the milking of camels.'"

I say: A similar report is narrated from Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, and its wording is: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Do not let the Bedouins overcome you regarding the name of your prayer.' Allah said: وَمِنْ بَعْدِ صَلاةِ الْعِشاءِ (and after the night prayer), while al-Atamah is only the darkness of the camels."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Hariz, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): that he read "that they put off their garments" and said: "The outer cloak (الجلباب) and the headscarf (الخمار), if the woman is elderly."

I say: There are other reports with this same meaning.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim reported from al-Dahhak that he said: "The people of Medina, before the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was sent, would not mix in their food with a blind person, a sick person, or a lame person. This was because the blind person cannot see the good parts of the food, the sick person cannot eat as much as the healthy person, and the lame person cannot jostle for the food. So a concession (رخصة) was revealed regarding eating with them."

In the same source, al-Tha'labi reported from Ibn Abbas that he said: "al-Harith went out on a military expedition with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and left Khalid ibn Zayd in charge of his family. Khalid felt restricted from eating from his food and was exhausted, so the verse was revealed."

In the same source, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Abi Hatim reported from Qatadah that he said: "This tribe of Banu Kinanah ibn Khuzaymah used to consider it a disgrace for one of them to eat alone during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), to the point that a man would drive a herd of full-uddered camels while hungry until he found someone to eat and drink with him. So Allah sent down: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا جَمِيعاً أَوْ أَشْتاتاً (There is no blame on you whether you eat together or separately)."

I say: There are other narrations with the meaning of these reports.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Zurarah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: أَوْ ما مَلَكْتُمْ مَفاتِحَهُ أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ (or those whose keys you possess, or your friend), he said: "These whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, named in this verse may eat without their permission from dates and condiments. Likewise, a woman may eat from her husband's house without his permission. As for food other than that, then no."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to a man: 'You and your wealth belong to your father.' Then Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'I do not like for him to take from his son's wealth except what he needs and what is indispensable for him; indeed, Allah does not love corruption (الفساد).'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "I asked him about a man whose son has wealth and the father is in need. He said: 'He may eat from it. As for the mother, she may not eat from it except as a loan to herself.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Jamil ibn Darraj, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "A woman has the right to eat and to give charity, and a friend has the right to eat from his brother's house and to give charity."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Ibn Abi Umayr, from one whom he mentioned, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: أَوْ ما مَلَكْتُمْ مَفاتِحَهُ (or those whose keys you possess), he said: "A man who has an agent (وكيل) who manages his wealth; he may eat without his permission."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ (that you eat from your own houses), it was said: "Its meaning is from the houses of your children. This is indicated by his (peace be upon him) saying: 'You and your wealth belong to your father,' and his (peace be upon him) saying: 'Indeed, the best of what a man eats is from his own earning, and his child is from his earning.'"

I say: There are many other narrations with these meanings.

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission to Abu al-Sabbah, he said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمْ بُيُوتاً فَسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ (And when you enter houses, greet yourselves...). He said: 'It is the man's greeting to the people of the house when he enters, and then they return it to him; that is your greeting to yourselves.'"

I say: This meaning was previously indicated in the commentary on the verse.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ (The faithful are only those who...) until His saying: حَتَّى يَسْتَأْذِنُوهُ (until they have sought his permission), he said: "It was revealed concerning a people who, when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) gathered them for a matter — such as a dispatch he was sending or a war that was imminent — would disperse without his permission. So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, forbade them from that."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا اسْتَأْذَنُوكَ لِبَعْضِ شَأْنِهِمْ فَأْذَنْ لِمَنْ شِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ (So when they seek your permission for some work of theirs, give permission to whomever of them you wish), he said: "It was revealed concerning Hanzalah ibn Abi Ayyash. That was because he married on the night before the morning of the Battle of Uhud. He sought permission from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to stay with his wife, so Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent down this verse: فَأْذَنْ لِمَنْ شِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ (give permission to whomever of them you wish). He stayed with his wife, then in the morning, while he was in a state of ritual impurity (جنب), he joined the battle and was martyred. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I saw the angels washing Hanzalah with the water of the clouds in silver basins between the heaven and the earth.' Thus, he was called 'the one washed by the angels' (غسيل الملائكة)."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا تَجْعَلُوا دُعاءَ الرَّسُولِ بَيْنَكُمْ كَدُعاءِ بَعْضِكُمْ بَعْضاً (Do not consider the Apostle’s summons among you like the summons of one of you to another), he said: "Do not call the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as you call one another." In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لا تَجْعَلُوا دُعاءَ الرَّسُولِ بَيْنَكُمْ كَدُعاءِ بَعْضِكُمْ بَعْضاً, he says: "Do not say: 'O Muhammad' or 'O Abu al-Qasim,' but say: 'O Prophet of Allah' and 'O Messenger of Allah.'"

I say: A similar report is narrated from Ibn Abbas. It has already been mentioned that the end of the verse does not align with this meaning so perfectly.
